title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper delivers the first rigorous analysis of the widely adopted stochastic “label trick” in graph neural network (GNN) training, in which a random subset of node labels is concatenated with features during message passing. Under linear, self‐excluded assumptions, the authors prove that the expected loss from stochastic label‐splitting is equivalent to optimizing a deterministic objective composed of (i) a self‐excluded label‐propagation term that prevents label leakage and preserves inference consistency, and (ii) an explicit, graph‐dependent regularizer on label weights whose strength is governed by the split probability α and the propagation operator’s spectrum. Building on this theory, they propose three practical variants: a trainable label‐propagation baseline, a deterministic label trick for linear‐layer GNNs, and an end‐to‐end trainable Correct & Smooth module. Experiments on standard node classification and regression benchmarks generally corroborate the theoretical insights.

Strengths  
1. Theoretical novelty and clarity  
   – The reduction of a stochastic sampling heuristic to a self‐excluded deterministic objective is elegant and provides the first principled explanation for why the label trick both mitigates label leakage and adaptively regularizes label weights (Alternative Reviews 1–4).  
   – By deriving an explicit regularization term and characterizing its dependence on α, the work gives practitioners insight into how the trick controls overfitting and shapes generalization.  

2. Unification and practical extensions  
   – The self‐excluded framework unifies several prior methods (e.g., SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS, Correct & Smooth) under one interpretable lens (Alternative Review 2).  
   – Proposed variants—trainable label propagation, deterministic linear‐layer integration, and end‐to‐end C&S—are simple to implement and directly motivated by the theory (Alternative Reviews 1–4).  

3. Empirical validation  
   – Experiments on OGB datasets and standard benchmarks show competitive or modestly improved accuracy, and initial studies illustrate how α influences training accuracy and regularization strength (Alternative Reviews 1–3).

Weaknesses and Questions  
1. Empirical gains are modest  
   – Across Tables 1–4, performance with the label trick often matches rather than surpasses strong baselines once standard deviations are considered. It remains unclear in which settings the trick yields consistent, statistically significant improvements (Main Review).  

2. Reliance on linear assumptions  
   – Core proofs assume linear predictors and MSE loss; extensions to nonlinear GNNs and cross‐entropy losses are heuristic or limited to extreme α→0/1 cases (Alternative Reviews 1–4). More empirical evidence is needed to show that the deterministic self‐excluded predictor recovers stochastic behavior in deep, nonlinear architectures.  

3. Computational and practical considerations  
   – Computing high‐order propagation matrices P and diagonal C can be expensive on large graphs. A detailed runtime and memory analysis, or discussion of sparse/approximate schemes, is missing (Alternative Review 1, 4).  
   – Guidance on choosing α in practice—perhaps based on graph density or via cross‐validation—is not provided, leaving users to tune an additional hyperparameter without principled direction (Alternative Reviews 1–4).  

4. Scope of empirical evaluation  
   – Experiments omit heterophily benchmarks, deeper GNNs (e.g., GAT), and long-range propagation settings where label‐based regularization might be more impactful (Alternative Reviews 3–4).  
   – Stability across random seeds and sensitivity to graph topology (sparsity, connectivity) warrant further study.  

5. Presentation and clarity  
   – Notation around multiple mask matrices (M_in, M_out) and derivations in the appendices can be dense. A streamlined exposition or intuitive walkthrough of proofs would improve readability (Alternative Review 1).  
   – Minor typos and notation inconsistencies should be corrected.

Recommendations for Improvement  
• Include wall‐clock runtime and memory comparisons between stochastic label trick, deterministic variants, and standard GNN training.  
• Conduct ablation studies on deep nonlinear GNNs to verify that deterministic and stochastic label tricks yield similar predictions.  
• Expand empirical evaluation to include heterophily graphs, deeper architectures, and dynamic α schedules; report performance stability across multiple seeds.  
• Provide practical guidelines for selecting α, perhaps relating it to graph statistics or regularization trade‐offs.  
• Simplify notation and move key intuitive steps into the main text to aid reader comprehension.

Decision  
Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold  
While the paper’s theoretical contributions are significant and its unifying framework and practical variants are well motivated, the empirical benefits are currently modest and the nonlinear extension remains under‐explored. With stronger ablations, clearer guidance on α, and deeper empirical validation, this work would make an even more compelling case for broad adoption.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper delivers the first thorough theoretical treatment of the widely used “label trick” in graph neural network (GNN) training, whereby a random subset of node labels is concatenated with input features. Beginning with the simplest linear MSE setting, the authors derive an equivalent deterministic objective that decomposes into (i) a self-excluded label‐propagation term preventing label leakage, and (ii) a graph-dependent regularizer whose strength is governed by the sampling probability α and the spectrum of the propagation operator. They then extend this framework to incorporate node features, provide cross‐entropy upper bounds, and discuss asymptotic behavior of nonlinear GNNs in the α→0/1 limits. Motivated by these insights, the paper proposes three practical variants—trainable label propagation, a deterministic label trick for linear‐propagation GNNs, and an end-to-end trainable Correct & Smooth module—and validates them on node classification and regression benchmarks with consistent performance gains.

Strengths  
1. Rigorous theoretical contribution: The reduction of the stochastic label trick to a self-excluded deterministic objective is elegant and fills an important gap in our understanding of why and how label injection serves as adaptive regularization.  
2. Clear decomposition: By isolating the data‐fitting term from the explicit graph-dependent regularizer, the work illuminates how α controls the trade-off between label leakage prevention and smoothness over the graph.  
3. Broad unification and extension: The framework subsumes and extends prior ad hoc methods (e.g., SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS, Correct & Smooth), presenting them as special cases of the proposed objective.  
4. Novel practical use cases: The three proposed variants are simple to implement, well motivated by theory, and offer a unified blueprint for integrating label propagation and smoothing into GNN training.  
5. Empirical validation: Experiments on OGB benchmarks, standard citation networks (Cora-full, PubMed), ogbn-arxiv, ogbn-products, and four regression datasets demonstrate consistent improvements and illustrate the theoretical effects of varying α.

Weaknesses and Questions  
1. Reliance on linear assumptions: The core proofs assume linear predictors and MSE loss; extensions to deep, nonlinear GNNs rest on limiting argumentation without formal bounds for intermediate α. Can the authors provide empirical evidence—e.g., ablations on deeper GCN or GAT models—that the deterministic self-excluded predictor closely matches stochastic behavior in practice?  
2. Computational cost: Computing high-order propagation matrices P and their diagonals C can be expensive on large graphs. Please clarify runtime and memory requirements, and whether sparse or approximate schemes (e.g., truncated propagation) suffice to retain performance with lower overhead.  
3. Sensitivity to α: While the paper explores α’s influence on regularization strength, practical guidance is limited. A short guideline—perhaps relating α to graph density or spectral properties—would help practitioners choose α without exhaustive cross-validation.  
4. Empirical scope: The current evaluation focuses on standard homophilous benchmarks. It would strengthen claims to include heterophily graphs, sensitivity to propagation depth k, and analysis under varying graph connectivity.  
5. Presentation density: Some notation (e.g., mask matrices M_in vs. M_out) and proof steps are deferred to the appendix, making the reading challenging for non-expert audiences. A streamlined exposition or illustrative example in the main text would improve clarity. Minor typos and inconsistent subscripts should also be addressed.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Include wall-clock runtime comparisons between the deterministic variants (requiring P and C) and standard stochastic label trick training.  
– Add ablations on deeper nonlinear GNN architectures to empirically assess the gap between stochastic and deterministic predictors.  
– Provide practical guidelines for selecting α, perhaps via a heuristic based on graph sparsity or initial validation performance.  
– Broaden empirical studies to heterophily datasets and varying graph sizes to demonstrate robustness.  
– Enhance the main text with an intuitive walkthrough of the key derivations and simplify notation where possible.

Decision  
8: Accept. This paper makes a significant and timely contribution by demystifying a pervasive GNN training heuristic, offering a solid theoretical foundation, and proposing actionable extensions that are both novel and empirically validated. While some clarifications and broader evaluations would strengthen the work, the current submission already provides valuable insights and tools for the community.

The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper tackles the widely used “label trick” in graph neural network (GNN) training, whereby a random subset of training labels is concatenated with node features during message passing. Under simplifying linear assumptions (no node features, MSE loss, linear propagation), the authors show that this stochastic scheme is equivalent—in expectation—to optimizing a deterministic self-excluded objective composed of a fitting term that prevents label leakage and an explicit graph-dependent regularizer on label weights. They extend the analysis to include node features, discuss the role of the sampling probability α in controlling regularization strength, and consider extreme α→0/1 limits for mildly nonlinear GNNs. Motivated by these insights, the paper proposes three practical variants: a trainable label‐propagation baseline, a deterministic label trick for linear-layer GNNs, and an end-to-end trainable Correct & Smooth module. Experiments on node classification and regression benchmarks report modest to consistent gains.

Recommendation  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Strengths  
1. Fundamental problem and unification  
   – The paper addresses an important gap in our understanding of how labels can be seamlessly integrated into GNNs, drawing a clear conceptual link between classical label‐propagation algorithms and modern message‐passing networks.  
   – By recasting stochastic label injection as a deterministic, self-excluded predictor with an explicit regularizer, it unifies and extends a variety of existing methods (e.g., SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS, Correct & Smooth) under a single theoretical framework.

2. Theoretical clarity under linear assumptions  
   – The derivation of the two-term objective (Equation 10) is elegant and interpretable: off-diagonal propagation prevents trivial identity mappings while the adaptive regularizer explains how α controls overfitting.  
   – The paper highlights the dependence of the implicit regularizer on graph structure (spectral properties) and sampling rate, offering insight into why the trick works in practice.

3. Practical motivation and initial empirical support  
   – The three proposed use cases are simple to implement and directly motivated by the theoretical analysis.  
   – Experiments on OGB benchmarks and standard citation/re gression datasets demonstrate that the deterministic variants yield performance on par with or slightly better than stochastic label tricks, corroborating the main claims.

Weaknesses  
1. Reliance on linearity and limited nonlinear guarantees  
   – The core analysis assumes linear predictors and MSE loss; while the paper sketches extensions to cross-entropy and nonlinear GNNs in limiting regimes (Theorem 3), intermediate α regimes remain heuristic. As noted by Alternative Reviews 1–4, a formal treatment or tighter bounds for standard deep GNN architectures (e.g., GCN, GAT with cross-entropy) are missing.  
   – Empirical evidence that the deterministic self-excluded predictor faithfully matches stochastic behavior in deep, nonlinear models would strengthen the work.

2. Empirical scope and ablations  
   – Although the experiments show consistent gains, they omit crucial ablations on:  
     • Runtime and memory costs of computing high-order propagation matrices P and diagonal corrections C for large graphs (Alternative 1, 4).  
     • Sensitivity to α across graph types (e.g., heterophily vs. homophily) and graph sizes (Alternative 2, 3).  
     • Performance on deeper GNN models and diverse real-world datasets beyond the current benchmarks (Alternative 4).  
   – A small wall-clock runtime comparison between standard stochastic tricks and the proposed deterministic variants would clarify practical trade-offs.

3. Clarity and notational density  
   – The paper is at times hard to read: excessive subscripts, dense mask-matrix notation (M_in, M_out), and a stream of “Remarks” interrupt the flow. Key derivations (e.g., the step from the penultimate to last term in Equation 5) are deferred with limited explanation.  
   – Section 4.1 (trainable Neumann series or smoothing) lacks detail: how exactly is this trained, and what is the final parametrization? Some connection to prior work on learning adjacency/propagation matrices is missing.

4. Missing references and credit  
   – The authors should cite and discuss concurrent or prior formal analyses of label propagation in GNNs, such as Jia & Benson (2021), and give proper credit to Wu et al. (2020.9.03509), who first proposed the label trick.  
   – It would also help to compare against the deterministic trick as a baseline in all plots (e.g., Figure 1 right) and specify which GNN architectures are used throughout Section 5.3.

Questions for the Authors  
– In Remark 4, if regularization is turned off, why is there “no chance for overfitting”?  
– Why isn’t the deterministic label trick included in the right‐hand plot of Figure 1, and which GNN backbone was used there?  
– For Table 3: if the derivation yields a regularization term in the deterministic case, was it actually used in these experiments, and if not, why?  
– Can you clarify the expectation notation in Equation 5? Should it simplify to a uniform average over training nodes as suggested by the main reviewer?

Minor Comments  
– Section 4.1: provide explicit training loss and refer to related papers on learning propagation matrices.  
– Table 2 (regression): report R² values in addition to MSE.  
– Typos/notational inconsistencies: “insure” → “ensure”; parentheses vs. square brackets in Equations 4 and 5.

Overall Assessment  
This submission offers a valuable theoretical lens on a pervasive heuristic in GNN training, with a clear reduction to a deterministic, regularized objective and compelling initial applications. However, the strong reliance on linear assumptions, the limited nonlinear theory, the overhead of propagation matrix computations, and the notational opacity collectively hinder its practical impact. Addressing these concerns—through tighter theoretical guarantees for nonlinear models, broader empirical ablations (runtime, heterophily, deep architectures), and a polished exposition—would be necessary to raise the contribution to an acceptance level.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

reviews.text:
Summary:

This paper tackles the popular “label trick” in graph‐based semi‐supervised learning, wherein a stochastic subset of known labels is concatenated with node features to prevent trivial label leakage in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Under linear and simplifying assumptions (MSE loss, no features), the authors derive a deterministic “self‐excluded” objective equivalent to the stochastic scheme. This decomposition reveals (i) a data‐fitting term that excludes the target label during training and (ii) an implicit graph‐dependent regularizer whose strength is controlled by the sampling rate α and the graph propagation operator. The work is extended to incorporate node features, cross‐entropy, and to propose three practical instantiations: trainable label propagation, a deterministic label trick for linear‐propagation GNNs, and an end‐to‐end trainable Correct & Smooth module. Experiments on node classification and regression benchmarks show modest gains and appear broadly consistent with the theoretical predictions.

Strengths:

1. Theoretical Clarity and Novelty  
   - The reduction of the stochastic label injection to an explicit deterministic objective is elegant. By showing that label sampling yields both a self‐excluded propagation term and an adaptive regularizer, the paper demystifies a widely used heuristic.  
   - The analysis unifies disparate methods (e.g., SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS, Correct & Smooth) under a single framework, illuminating how the split probability α governs regularization strength via the spectrum of the propagation operator.

2. Practical Relevance  
   - The three proposed variants are simple to implement and well motivated: (a) trainable label propagation baseline, (b) deterministic label trick for linear‐layer GNNs, and (c) end‐to‐end Correct & Smooth.  
   - Empirical results on OGB benchmarks (ogbn-arxiv, ogbn-products), citation networks (Cora-full, PubMed), and regression datasets demonstrate consistent, if modest, improvements over standard baselines.

3. Readability and Presentation  
   - The paper is generally well written and takes a commendable step back to clarify what often appears as “wizard engineering.”  
   - The main ideas are presented in a mostly clear fashion, with proofs deferred to appendices and key derivations highlighted in the body.

Weaknesses:

1. Over‐reliance on Linear Assumptions  
   - The core theorems rest on linear predictors and MSE losses; extensions to nonlinear GNNs and cross‐entropy are treated only at the extreme limits α→0,1 or via heuristic arguments (Theorem 3). This leaves a conceptual gap for intermediate α and practical architectures (e.g., deep GCN/GAT with cross‐entropy).  
   - No empirical ablation confirms that the deterministic self‐excluded predictor matches the stochastic label trick in deep nonlinear models.

2. Insufficient Empirical Depth  
   - The experiments lack studies on heterophilous graphs, deeper GNN architectures, and sensitivity to graph sparsity. The impact of α on training dynamics, runtime, and memory (especially for computing high‐order propagation matrices P and C) is not assessed.  
   - Runtime and scalability concerns: computing or approximating P = (I – λαS)^{-1} and C = diag(P) can be expensive on large graphs; no wall‐clock comparisons or sparse/approximate schemes are discussed.

3. Gaps in Motivation and Rigor  
   - The background conflates smoothness over graphs with i.i.d. assumptions, which may confuse readers. Definitions of graph formalism (adjacency/weight matrix, feature and label matrices X, Y) should be stated up front.  
   - Some claims are under‐justified—for example, why exactly self‐excluded prediction underperforms full stochastic execution, and why this trick outperforms classical Laplacian regularization (Pfau et al.) or MixUp‐style regularizers (Cabannes et al., Lopez-Paz et al.).  
   - Notational inconsistencies and minor mistakes (e.g., missing subscripts in Eq.(1), unclear definition of P in Eq.(3), overfull boxes in the appendix) impede a rigorous reading.

4. Lack of Practical Guidance  
   - While the dependence of regularization on α is discussed theoretically, there is no clear guideline on how to choose α in practice. A short recommendation, perhaps based on graph density or training accuracy, would help practitioners.

Detailed Comments:

• Page 1: The introduction could be more concise. Discussing i.i.d. samples alongside graph smoothness adds confusion—these are distinct modeling assumptions.  
• Page 2: Please formalize the graph notation: define X∈R^{n×d}, Y∈R^{n×c}, S∈R^{n×n} symmetric, and clarify weighted vs. unweighted edges.  
• Page 3: In the approximation P ≈ PY, it would be clearer to state the infinite‐series expansion of (I – λαS)^{-1}.  
• Page 4: A brief note on SGC’s role and relevance would contextualize Section 3.2. Also, specify how P is fixed when defining new models in Eq.(3).  
• Appendix: Address overfull boxes and ensure all arXiv references are updated to their published venues.

Conclusion and Recommendation:

This paper makes an important initial stride toward theoretically understanding a pervasive GNN training heuristic and proposes actionable variants. However, the analysis remains tied to linear settings, and the empirical evaluation does not fully substantiate its claims in modern nonlinear architectures. Furthermore, questions about computational cost, rigorous comparisons to alternative regularizers, and practical guidelines on α selection remain open. While the work is valuable and points to promising directions, it is not yet sufficiently mature for publication.

Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.

The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper addresses the widely adopted stochastic “label trick” in graph neural networks (GNNs) by proposing a fully deterministic reformulation based on a self-exclusion operator that prevents label leakage during propagation. Under linear predictor and MSE assumptions, the authors prove (Theorem 1) that the stochastic scheme is equivalent to minimizing a deterministic objective comprising (i) a self-excluded label‐propagation term and (ii) an explicit graph-structured regularizer whose strength is controlled by the sampling probability α. They further derive an upper bound with respect to cross-entropy loss (Theorem 2), extend the analysis in limiting cases to mildly nonlinear GNNs, and propose three practical variants: (a) trainable label propagation, (b) a deterministic label trick for linear-propagation GNNs, and (c) an end-to-end Correct & Smooth module. Empirical evaluations on node classification and regression benchmarks demonstrate modest gains or competitive performance.

Strengths  
1. Theoretical novelty and clarity  
   • The reduction of the stochastic label-injection heuristic to an interpretable deterministic objective is elegant and fills a gap in our theoretical understanding. By explicitly decomposing the loss into a self-exclusion term and a graph-dependent regularizer, the work explains why the label trick prevents leakage and controls overfitting.  
   • Deriving an upper bound for the cross-entropy loss (Theorem 2) is a valuable addition that broadens the scope beyond MSE.

2. Unification and practical value  
   • By recasting existing methods such as SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS, and Correct & Smooth within the same framework, the paper unifies a range of hybrid propagation techniques.  
   • The proposed applications—trainable label propagation, deterministic linear-layer GNNs, and end-to-end C&S—are simple to implement and offer practitioners a clear path to leverage the theory.

3. Empirical support  
   • Experiments across OGB benchmarks and classic citation graphs show consistent, if modest, performance improvements and validate the role of α in controlling regularization strength.  
   • Figure 1’s ablation on α effectively illustrates the theory’s prediction about the bias–variance tradeoff induced by different sampling rates.

Weaknesses  
1. Motivation and practical advantage  
   • The paper does not fully justify why one should replace a simple stochastic label trick with a deterministic variant, aside from sampling issues. It remains unclear whether the deterministic approach offers computational savings or robustness benefits in large-scale or resource-constrained settings.  
   • While the theory highlights a novel regularizer, no direct comparisons against state-of-the-art stochastic label tricks are presented, leaving the real-world advantage ambiguous.

2. Reliance on linear assumptions  
   • Core proofs depend on linear predictors and MSE loss; extensions to nonlinear GNNs rest on limiting arguments (α→0,1) without rigorous bounds. It is not demonstrated empirically that the deterministic self-excluded predictor matches stochastic behavior in deep, non‐linear models such as multi-layer GCN or GAT.  

3. Experimental scope and ablations  
   • Although datasets like ogbn-arxiv and ogbn-products are included, the evaluation omits heterophily graphs and deeper GNN architectures.  
   • Sensitivity analyses on propagation depth, graph sparsity, and runtime overhead of computing high-order propagation matrices P and diag(P) are missing.  
   • The reported gains are often within one standard deviation, raising questions about statistical significance and stability across random seeds.

4. Computational cost and scalability  
   • The deterministic variant requires computing or approximating propagation operators and self-exclusion masks, which can be expensive on large graphs. The paper does not discuss wall-clock runtime, memory footprint, or sparse approximations needed for million-node scenarios.

5. Clarity and completeness  
   • Some notation (e.g., multiple mask matrices M_in, M_out) and dense matrix-derivation steps are deferred to the appendix, which may hamper readability for non-experts.  
   • A few important prior works on label propagation and graph diffusion (e.g., Szlam et al., 2008) are missing, and references to C&S should be clearly spelled out.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Provide direct head-to-head comparisons between the deterministic trick and standard stochastic label injection in end-to-end GNN training.  
– Include wall-clock runtime and memory benchmarks to clarify computational trade-offs.  
– Perform ablations on deeper nonlinear GNNs, heterophily graphs, and varying propagation steps to demonstrate generality.  
– Offer practical guidelines for choosing α, perhaps tied to graph density or validation performance.  
– Streamline notation and move critical proof sketches into the main text to improve accessibility.  
– Add the missing key references on graph-based diffusion and label propagation.

Decision  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold. While the theoretical contribution and unification of label-trick variants are valuable, the paper’s motivation for a deterministic alternative is underexplored, the nonlinear extension remains heuristic, and the experimental validation is not yet sufficiently comprehensive or convincing.

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In sum, the paper seems to contain potentially interesting results. 
The overall organization and presentation can be further improved to improve the readability of the paper.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper studies imitation learning for deterministic experts by reducing it to a single reinforcement-learning (RL) problem with a stationary intrinsic reward that marks expert state–action pairs. Under the assumption that the expert policy is deterministic and induces an irreducible, aperiodic (ergodic) Markov chain in a finite MDP, the authors prove high-probability, finite-sample bounds on (i) the total variation distance between the expert and learned policy and (ii) the recovery of any bounded extrinsic reward. They further draw a connection to adversarial imitation learning by showing that this reduction implicitly minimizes the same divergences as methods like GAIL. Finally, they demonstrate in continuous-control benchmarks (Hopper, Ant, Walker, HalfCheetah) that a practical implementation—using a soft-actor-critic solver on a relaxed continuous reward—matches or exceeds baselines such as behavioral cloning, GAIL, GMMIL, and SQIL, all while avoiding discriminator training and extra hyperparameters.

Recommendation  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Strengths  
1. Theoretical Guarantees and Conceptual Elegance  
   - The paper provides the first non-asymptotic total-variation bound for reducing imitation learning to a single RL call with a binary intrinsic reward.  
   - Proposition 1 cleanly links imitation performance to dataset size and the expert’s mixing time, unifying reductions like Random Expert Distillation and adversarial IL under one formal framework.  
   - The reward-recovery result is novel and offers clear insight into how the learner can replicate extrinsic objectives.

2. Simplicity and Practicality  
   - ILR requires exactly one RL invocation, no discriminator, and no additional hyperparameters beyond those of the base RL solver.  
   - Empirical results on four standard continuous-control tasks show competitive performance and often improved sample efficiency in low-data regimes.

3. Clear Link to Adversarial IL  
   - By framing the analysis in total variation, the authors reveal how stationary intrinsic rewards align with divergences minimized by GAIL, strengthening conceptual connections between imitation and adversarial methods.

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Strong Assumptions Limit Applicability  
   - The deterministic-expert assumption (Assumption 1) is restrictive: many real-world experts (humans, entropy-regularized policies) are inherently stochastic. Extending the analysis to stochastic experts or quantifying performance degradation when determinism is violated would broaden the method’s applicability.  
   - Reliance on ergodicity and mixing-time bounds raises questions in large or continuous state spaces: when τ_mix is large, the bound may become vacuous. A sensitivity analysis showing how performance degrades with slower mixing would be valuable.

2. Discounted vs. Average Reward Setting  
   - The paper exclusively treats average-reward MDPs, whereas discounted formulations are more common in modern RL practice. The authors should discuss whether and how their reduction and bounds extend to discounted settings.

3. Clarity and Presentation  
   - The definition of the total variation norm (‖·‖_TV) is assumed but not formally given; a precise statement would improve readability.  
   - The terms “intrinsic reward” and “extrinsic reward” are used without formal definitions in the main text; readers would benefit from explicit notation and discussion.  
   - Key components of Proposition 1—such as the constants hidden in big-O notation—should be clarified, and the range of RL solvers to which the proposition applies ought to be specified.  
   - Section 3’s description of how the imitation learner recovers the expert policy from the intrinsic reward is terse; a step-by-step exposition or pseudocode would help bridge the gap between theory and implementation.  
   - There are stray symbols (e.g., “(cid:80)”) and missing equation references that hinder the flow; a thorough proofreading is recommended.

4. Empirical Scope and Analysis  
   - The experiments rely on a single RL algorithm (SAC) and four Mujoco-style tasks. Including a broader set of environments or at least a comparison with alternative RL backbones would strengthen claims of generality.  
   - The continuous-state relaxation (histogram or kernel-based reward) is heuristic and lacks theoretical guarantees; an ablation over different relaxation kernels or bin sizes would help assess robustness.  
   - Comparative analysis details are thin: it is unclear how hyperparameters for baselines were tuned, whether runtime comparisons were performed, and how sensitive ILR is to its own hyperparameters.  
   - No empirical evaluation of the bound’s tightness is presented—reporting predicted vs. actual total-variation errors could validate the theoretical rates.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Discuss extensions to stochastic experts and discounted-reward MDPs, or clearly state the challenges and possible workarounds.  
2. Add formal definitions for ‖·‖_TV, intrinsic/extrinsic rewards, and specify the class of RL solvers covered by Proposition 1.  
3. Provide more detailed pseudocode or algorithmic steps for Section 3 to clarify the learning process under the intrinsic reward.  
4. Expand empirical evaluation: include sensitivity analyses (mixing time, relaxation parameters, hyperparameter robustness), additional RL solvers, and possibly image-based or higher-dimensional tasks.  
5. Tighten presentation: fix formatting issues, ensure all equations are referenced, and reorganize background material to improve reader guidance.

Overall Assessment  
This manuscript makes a noteworthy theoretical contribution by establishing finite-sample, high-probability bounds for a remarkably simple reduction from imitation to reinforcement learning. The conceptual link to adversarial methods and the practical advantage of avoiding discriminators and extra hyperparameters add to its appeal. However, strong assumptions (deterministic experts, ergodicity) and limits in empirical scope and clarity temper the impact. With revisions addressing the above points—especially those on definitions, proof applicability, and broader experiments—the paper will better serve both theorists and practitioners.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.
reviews.main_review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.
