One of the main contributions of the paper is addressing the major problems with RLSVI which are restricting its applications in deep RL. While addressing these limitations regarding computational costs of RLSVI is discussed extensively, providing a specific cost function to jointly optimize the feature extractor and the hypermodel might lead to stability and convergence problems. Hence, it would be beneficial if the authors provide further insights into this matter.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, the paper introduces a novel approach for randomized exploration in deep RL, is well written, and shows strong promise in terms of performance compared to other state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, it includes valuable theoretical and experimental discussions that provide further support for their contributions. I thus vote for an accept.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
The authors present HyperDQN, a randomized‐exploration method that extends randomized least‐squares value iteration (RLSVI) into deep reinforcement learning. HyperDQN augments a standard Q‐network with a probabilistic hypermodel: a learned mapping from a Gaussian noise vector to the final‐layer weights of the base network. Sampling once per episode yields an approximate posterior sample of the Q‐function without expensive covariance computations. A joint cost function combines a randomized temporal‐difference loss with a Bayesian regularization term, enabling end‐to‐end feature learning. The paper offers a linear‐case theoretical guarantee (Theorem 1) showing that the hypermodel recovers the exact Bayesian posterior in expectation, and demonstrates strong empirical performance on 49 Atari games (20M frames vs. 200M for DQN), SuperMarioBros levels, and the deep-sea benchmark.

Strengths  
• Novel and practical extension of RLSVI: By jointly training the final‐layer hypermodel and the feature extractor, HyperDQN overcomes the fixed‐feature limitation of prior RLSVI methods and scales randomized posterior sampling to high‐dimensional deep Q‐networks.  
• Theoretical backing: Theorem 1 rigorously establishes that, under a linear assumption, the hypermodel matches the Bayesian linear regression posterior in expectation—filling a gap left by earlier work.  
• Sample efficiency and empirical gains: Across discrete‐action benchmarks, HyperDQN substantially outperforms DQN, Bootstrapped DQN, NoisyNet, and bonus‐based methods, achieving comparable or superior scores in Atari with an order of magnitude fewer frames, and solving the deep-sea testbed more efficiently.  
• Computational efficiency: Sampling from the hypermodel is O(1) per episode, avoiding the overhead of recomputing large feature covariances and ensembles, which translates into practical speedups (although exact wall-clock comparisons are not yet reported).  
• Clear presentation and reproducibility: The paper is well written, with detailed algorithmic pseudocode, complete hyperparameter listings, and supplementary derivations that facilitate reproducibility.  

Weaknesses  
• Incremental novelty: While adapting RLSVI to deep RL is valuable, the core idea builds directly on existing work; extensions to other paradigms may be nontrivial.  
• Theoretical scope limited to linear last‐layer features: No formal analysis is provided for the joint optimization of nonlinear representation layers, leaving open questions about convergence and stability in the deep setting.  
• Restricted experimental domain: All RL experiments involve discrete actions; continuous‐control environments (e.g. MuJoCo or dm_control) are absent, and the single CartPole result is insufficient to judge generality.  
• Sparse‐reward challenges: HyperDQN fails on extremely sparse tasks like Montezuma’s Revenge, and the brief attribution to “poor feature learning” would benefit from deeper analysis or hybrid intrinsic‐motivation strategies.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity and ablations: Key design choices—noise scale, prior weight, hypermodel depth (linear vs. MLP)—are only lightly ablated. Insights into selecting these parameters in practice are limited.  
• Missing comparisons and compute budgets: The manuscript does not compare against intrinsic‐motivation methods (e.g. RND, RIDE), nor does it report GPU‐hour or FLOP‐based wall‐clock timing against ensemble‐based baselines.  
• Statistical reporting: Results are reported as maximum returns without confidence intervals or mean/standard‐error across random seeds, which may overstate gains.  
• Notational density: Some equations (e.g. err(ξ)) are hard to parse and the symbol-heavy presentation would benefit from a summary table.  

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
1. Generalization to other domains  
   – Discuss challenges and potential solutions for extending HyperDQN to continuous‐control tasks, offline RL, or environments with high‐dimensional observations (e.g. sparse‐reward 3D mazes). Preliminary MuJoCo experiments or even offline‐RL benchmarks would strengthen claims of broad applicability.  
2. Joint optimization stability  
   – Provide further insight into the convergence and stability of jointly training the hypermodel and feature extractor. Are there failure modes or oscillations when the two components co‐adapt? Empirical diagnostics (e.g. weight‐norm trajectories) or convergence plots would be informative.  
3. Hyperparameter ablations  
   – Expand ablation studies on hypermodel architecture (linear vs. shallow MLP), noise/prior scales, and number of posterior samples. Include guidance on choosing these hyperparameters and discuss any observed sensitivity.  
4. Compute‐cost analysis  
   – Report quantitative wall‐clock training times or FLOP counts comparing HyperDQN to Bootstrapped DQN and NoisyNet under similar hardware settings. This will clarify the practical efficiency advantages of the O(1) sampling.  
5. Sparse‐reward and Montezuma’s Revenge  
   – Analyze why HyperDQN fails in very sparse domains. Feature‐space visualizations, exploration‐trajectory statistics, or experiments combining intrinsic bonuses could identify remedies.  
6. Statistical rigor  
   – Present mean performance with error bars across multiple seeds and include statistical significance tests when comparing to strong baselines. This will ensure reported improvements are robust.  
7. Presentation improvements  
   – Streamline notation by providing a table of symbols, simplify dense equations (e.g. err(ξ)), and consolidate methodological details to enhance readability.  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
HyperDQN represents a solid, well‐motivated advance in scalable posterior sampling for deep Q‐learning, bringing RLSVI’s theoretical strengths into the deep RL regime with empirical sample‐efficiency gains. Although the theoretical analysis is confined to a linear setting and some empirical and ablation studies remain incomplete, the core contribution is significant and practical. I thus endorse the authors’ recommendation to accept this paper.  

Rating: 8: Accept, good paper

The authors further present a specific cost function to jointly optimize the hypermodel and the feature extractor that can efficiently address the limitation of the RLSVI approach in deep RL scenarios. Empirical study demonstrates significant improvement compared to the competitors on several benchmarks.

Strengths: 
+ The outcome of the paper which adapts the ideas from RLSVI to deep reinforcement learning settings is significant and practical. 
+ The paper is very well written, clear, and easy to understand. The supplementary materials provide most of the information needed for acquiring a better understanding of the paper. 
+ The authors provide theoretical analysis and proofs to support their main ideas and design choices. 
+ The experimental results show superior performance of HyperDQN compared to state-of-the-art algorithms. 
+ The results are reproducible as the implementation and experimental details are provided in the paper.

Weaknesses: 
- The proposed approach provides incremental contributions w.r.t. the existing ideas from the previous work.
- The contributions are very specific to a class of problems and algorithms.

Comments: 
- The main ideas of the paper has been previously introduced in the literature and is further extended to the deep RL scenarios which make the presented contributions only somewhat novel. 
- The paper can be improved by providing some information and\or intuition about the generalizability of the proposed approach to the other domains. Additionally, discussions about the challenges in extending the proposed idea to the areas such as continuous control, offline RL, etc. would be highly appreciated. 
- Could you elaborate about the challenges and potential problems that could arise when jointly optimizing the hypermodel and the feature extractor? One of the main contributions of the paper is addressing the major problems with RLSVI which are restricting its applications in deep RL. While addressing these limitations regarding computational costs of RLSVI is discussed extensively, providing a specific cost function to jointly optimize the feature extractor and the hypermodel might lead to stability and convergence problems. Hence, it would be beneficial if the authors provide further insights into this matter.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, the paper introduces a novel approach for randomized exploration in deep RL, is well written, and shows strong promise in terms of performance compared to other state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, it includes valuable theoretical and experimental discussions that provide further support for their contributions. I thus vote for an accept.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

Conceptually, *HyperDQN* is modified based on randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI) and it resolves the limitations of RLSVI on feature engineering and high computational complexity. Technically, *HyperDQN* extends the Hypermodel technique, which is only used for bandit learning in its original paper. The performance of *HyperDQN* is evaluated on Atari 2600 games, SuperMarioBros and deep sea environment.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths
Overall, I think it is a good trial to extend the idea of Hypermodel to the realm of deep reinforcement learning. Compared with BootDQN, *HyperDQN* will require much less computational power. Meanwhile, the paper provides a large amount of experiment results to show the superior performance of *HyperDQN*. Meanwhile, the paper is also written in a clear logical flow.

### Weaknesses
I apologize if I understand incorrectly. One of my major concern is on the novelty. Given the existence of Hypermodel, the *HyperDQN* seems to be a very straightforward application of it on deep reinforcement learning without more insights. Meanwhile, from my perspective, the Theorem 1 simply re-writes the motivation of Hypermodel in a mathematical way, using linear hypermodel to approximate the posterior distribution, which is already explained in [1].

Another concern I have is about the performance of *HyperDQN*. Specifically, although the experiments compare *HyperDQN* with BootDQN, it seems that this paper uses the version of BootDQN based on [2]. However, the version of BootDQN in [3] has much better performance than that in [2]. Further, it seems the BootDQN in [3] did not use $\epsilon$-greedy. Have you compred *HyperDQN* with the BootDQN in [3]?

**Honestly, I'm not very experienced in running experiments, so I apologize if the above comments on experiments contain some flaws.**

### Questions
- Does *HyperDQN* contain other insights that are not presented in [1]?
- Did the comparison experiments use BootDQN in [2] or [3]?
- Did you compare *HyperDQN* and BootDQN on deep sea?
- When you ran BootDQN, did you use number of ensembles significantly less than the one used in [3]?

### Suggestions on Writing
- In appendix C, it seems $b$ should be replaced by $\nu_b$ in equation C.6.
- At the bottom of page 4, it says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times N_a}$, but in the previous paragraph, it also says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^d$. This can potentially be confusing.
- In Theorem 1, it is may be better to replace "then $\theta:=\dots$ with" by "then $\theta:=\dots$ satisfies"

```
[1] Vikranth Dwaracherla, Xiuyuan Lu, Morteza Ibrahimi, Ian Osband, Zheng Wen, and Benjamin Van Roy. Hypermodels for exploration. In Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Learning Representations, 2020.

[2] Ian Osband, Charles Blundell, Alexander Pritzel, and Benjamin Van Roy. Deep exploration via bootstrapped DQN. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 29, pp. 4026–4034, 2016a.

[3] Ian Osband, John Aslanides, and Albin Cassirer. Randomized prior functions for deep reinforcement learning. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 31, pp. 8626–8638, 2018.
```

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a new posterior sampling-based exploration method, called *HyperDQN*, for deep reinforcement learning. Conceptually, *HyperDQN* is modified based on randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI) and it resolves the limitations of RLSVI on feature engineering and high computational complexity. Technically, *HyperDQN* extends the Hypermodel technique, which is only used for bandit learning in its original paper. The performance of *HyperDQN* is evaluated on Atari 2600 games, SuperMarioBros and deep sea environment.

Main Review:
### Strengths
Overall, I think it is a good trial to extend the idea of Hypermodel to the realm of deep reinforcement learning. Compared with BootDQN, *HyperDQN* will require much less computational power. Meanwhile, the paper provides a large amount of experiment results to show the superior performance of *HyperDQN*. Meanwhile, the paper is also written in a clear logical flow.

### Weaknesses
I apologize if I understand incorrectly. One of my major concern is on the novelty. Given the existence of Hypermodel, the *HyperDQN* seems to be a very straightforward application of it on deep reinforcement learning without more insights. Meanwhile, from my perspective, the Theorem 1 simply re-writes the motivation of Hypermodel in a mathematical way, using linear hypermodel to approximate the posterior distribution, which is already explained in [1].

Another concern I have is about the performance of *HyperDQN*. Specifically, although the experiments compare *HyperDQN* with BootDQN, it seems that this paper uses the version of BootDQN based on [2]. However, the version of BootDQN in [3] has much better performance than that in [2]. Further, it seems the BootDQN in [3] did not use $\epsilon$-greedy. Have you compred *HyperDQN* with the BootDQN in [3]?

**Honestly, I'm not very experienced in running experiments, so I apologize if the above comments on experiments contain some flaws.**

### Questions
- Does *HyperDQN* contain other insights that are not presented in [1]?
- Did the comparison experiments use BootDQN in [2] or [3]?
- Did you compare *HyperDQN* and BootDQN on deep sea?
- When you ran BootDQN, did you use number of ensembles significantly less than the one used in [3]?

### Suggestions on Writing
- In appendix C, it seems $b$ should be replaced by $\nu_b$ in equation C.6.
- At the bottom of page 4, it says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times N_a}$, but in the previous paragraph, it also says $\theta_{\mathrm{predict}}\in\mathbb{R}^d$. This can potentially be confusing.
- In Theorem 1, it is may be better to replace "then $\theta:=\dots$ with" by "then $\theta:=\dots$ satisfies"

```
[1] Vikranth Dwaracherla, Xiuyuan Lu, Morteza Ibrahimi, Ian Osband, Zheng Wen, and Benjamin Van Roy. Hypermodels for exploration. In Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Learning Representations, 2020.

[2] Ian Osband, Charles Blundell, Alexander Pritzel, and Benjamin Van Roy. Deep exploration via bootstrapped DQN. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 29, pp. 4026–4034, 2016a.

[3] Ian Osband, John Aslanides, and Albin Cassirer. Randomized prior functions for deep reinforcement learning. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 31, pp. 8626–8638, 2018.
```

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a computationally lighter method *HperDQN* for exploration in deep reinforcement learning and shows its superior performance in experiments. However, the formulation of *HyperDQN* is conceptually not novel and its performance compared to other methods is also questionable.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper introduces HyperDQN, a novel posterior‐sampling exploration method for deep reinforcement learning that extends the hypermodel idea—originally applied to bandits—to Q‐learning. HyperDQN jointly trains a base Q‐network and a probabilistic hypermodel that maps a Gaussian noise vector z to the final‐layer weights of the Q‐network. Sampling the hypermodel yields approximate posterior samples of Q‐functions for Thompson‐sampling‐style exploration, avoiding the heavy covariance recomputations of RLSVI and the large ensembles of Bootstrapped DQN. The authors prove (Theorem 1) that, under a linear‐regression assumption, this hypermodel recovers the exact Bayesian posterior in expectation. Empirically, HyperDQN (20 M frames) matches or surpasses DQN (200 M frames), BootDQN, NoisyNet, and exploration‐bonus methods on Atari 2600 games, SuperMarioBros, and the deep‐sea benchmark.

Decision  
Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold  
I find HyperDQN a well‐motivated and largely successful attempt to bring scalable posterior sampling to deep Q‐learning. While certain theoretical and empirical gaps remain, the paper’s clarity, scope of experiments, and sample‐efficiency gains justify a borderline acceptance.

Strengths  
1. Conceptual Contribution  
   - HyperDQN elegantly addresses RLSVI’s fixed‐feature limitation by jointly learning the final‐layer features and a hypermodel, preserving randomized exploration in a deep setting.  
   - Sampling from the hypermodel is O(1) per episode and yields full commitment to a single Q‐function, leading to deep exploration without maintaining large ensembles.  
2. Theoretical Grounding  
   - Theorem 1 rigorously shows that, in the linear last‐layer case, the learned hypermodel recovers the Bayesian posterior mean and covariance in expectation—bridging a key gap unaddressed by earlier RLSVI extensions.  
3. Empirical Evaluation  
   - Broad benchmarks: 49 Atari games, multiple SuperMarioBros levels, and the deep‐sea testbed demonstrate consistent sample‐efficiency improvements.  
   - HyperDQN achieves competitive scores with only 20 M frames versus DQN’s 200 M, and outperforms BootDQN, NoisyNet, and bonus‐based methods on both discrete benchmarks and a hard‐exploration environment.  
   - Ablations on ensemble size, noise/prior scales, and hypermodel architectures (linear vs. MLP) offer practical insight into design choices.

Weaknesses  
1. Novelty and Theoretical Scope  
   - The application of hypermodels to deep RL, while well executed, is a straightforward extension of prior work on bandits [1]. Theorem 1 essentially restates the original hypermodel motivation in a linear regression setting, without new theoretical insight into the nonlinear regime. Extending the guarantee to learned representations would strengthen the conceptual novelty.  
2. Empirical Comparisons  
   - Baselines: The paper compares mainly against the BootDQN variant from [2], but omits the stronger BootDQN in [3] (which forgoes ε-greedy and uses larger ensembles). A direct comparison to BootDQN [3] would clarify HyperDQN’s practical advantage.  
   - Missing intrinsic methods: There is no comparison to recent prediction‐error or intrinsic motivation approaches (e.g. RND, RIDE), making it difficult to position HyperDQN within the broader exploration literature.  
3. Ablations and Sensitivity  
   - Hyperparameter sensitivity (noise scale, prior regularization, hypermodel depth) is only briefly addressed. A more systematic ablation—especially on why linear hypermodels outperform MLP variants—would guide practitioners.  
   - Continuous control: Experiments are confined to discrete‐action tasks. Preliminary CartPole results exist, but scaling to MuJoCo or DM Control benchmarks would demonstrate generality.  
4. Compute and Statistical Reporting  
   - Runtime: Wall‐clock training time or FLOP comparisons versus BootDQN and NoisyNet are not reported, leaving the claimed efficiency advantage unquantified.  
   - Statistical rigor: The paper reports maximum scores but omits confidence intervals or average performance across random seeds. Including error bars would bolster empirical claims.  
5. Sparse‐Reward Failure  
   - Like many RL exploration methods, HyperDQN fails on Montezuma’s Revenge. The manuscript attributes this briefly to poor feature learning, but lacks deeper analysis or suggestions (e.g. auxiliary rewards, intrinsic bonuses) to mitigate this limitation.

Questions for the Authors  
- Which BootDQN implementation did you use—[2] or the stronger variant in [3]?  
- Have you compared HyperDQN to BootDQN [3] on the deep‐sea environment and Atari suite without ε-greedy?  
- Can you share preliminary continuous‐control results or plans to extend to MuJoCo?  
- How sensitive is HyperDQN to ensemble size and prior scale compared to BootDQN and NoisyNet?  

Suggestions on Writing and Presentation  
- In Appendix C, replace b with ν_b in equation C.6 for consistency.  
- Page 4: clarify whether θ_predict ∈ ℝ^d or ℝ^(d×N_a) to avoid confusion.  
- In Theorem 1, consider phrasing “then θ := … satisfies” rather than “then θ := … with” for clarity.  
- Add a notation table summarizing key symbols (e.g. err(ξ), ν_b) to improve readability.  
- Report performance averages and confidence intervals across seeds in plots and tables.

Overall Recommendation  
HyperDQN delivers a clear, scalable approach to posterior exploration in deep Q‐learning, backed by a linear‐case theoretical guarantee and strong empirical gains on standard discrete benchmarks. Despite some straightforward conceptual jumps from existing hypermodel work, unquantified compute benefits, and limited theoretical scope beyond linear layers, the manuscript is well written and offers valuable insights. I recommend marginal acceptance (6/10), contingent on addressing the empirical gaps—particularly baseline comparisons, compute‐cost reporting, and statistical rigor—either in revision or in supplementary material.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper presents HyperDQN as a practical algorithm of Randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI).  HyperDQN consists of two parametric models: first is a base model that is similar to that of a DQN agent and the second a meta model that parameterizes the last layer of the Q-network as a function of a latent variable. The purpose of this architecture is to generate posterior samples using the diverse Q-value functions represented through this architecture. The paper demonstrates this approach empirically across a variety of Atari games, SuperMarioBros and DeepSea.
reviews.main_review:
The paper begins with a strong motivation towards computationally implementing RLSVI in Deep RL using a base and meta models, and also points out reasons why this is non-trivial. Also, the main differences between BootDQN and HyperDQN are also discussed, which is important because BootDQN is a highly relevant prior work in this domain. Overall the paper is well-written and has strong motivations that are relevant to the RL community at large. 

The following are some of the questions based on my understanding of the paper and would be great if the authors could clarify some of them: 

1. The meta-model in HyperDQN is considered as a linear parameterized model and there is a theoretical justification for this. What would happen to the empirical/theoretical results if the meta-model is a non-linear function of the latent variable z? I am inclined to think that the representation power for the posterior distribution would be better and thus should produce improvements overall.

2. How are the sigma_w and sigma_p parameters chosen in the objective function? Have the authors considered a baseline where an agent uses an identical artificial noise term for modifying the Q_{target} (i.e., sigma_w z^{\top} \epsilon_i ) to the one used in HyperDQN? 

3. The Atari performance curves summarized in Fig 3 is not sufficient to demonstrate the utility of the presented approach. This curve is computed over all the Atari games (some of them require no exploration and some require a complex strategy for exploration). It would be more appropriate to show how the agent performs on “hard-exploration” and “easy-exploration” games (this classification is presented in Bellemare et al. 2016) rather than present a summary across all games. 

Also, why are the agents only trained for 20M frames? It is conventional in Atari to run the agents for 200M frames (Machado et al. 2018). This makes it easier to compare the performance of HyperDQN wrto the prior methods in Atari. 

A minor comment: why do some curves in Fig 3 start at 0 and another starts at a value lower than 0? 

Bellemare, M., Srinivasan, S., Ostrovski, G., Schaul, T., Saxton, D., and Munos, R. Unifying count-based exploration and intrinsic motivation. In NeurIPS, 2016.

Machado, M. C., Bellemare, M. G., Talvitie, E., Veness, J., Hausknecht, M., & Bowling, M. (2018). Revisiting the arcade learning environment: Evaluation protocols and open problems for general agents. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 61, 523-562.

4. How many random seeds were used to produce the learning curves in all the experiments (Atari, Mario and DeepSea)? It would be useful to add this information in the main text. Also, are the error bars reported in each of the presented learning curves?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I think the contribution would be important to the RL community. My main concerns with the paper is that the presented approach claims to be helpful for exploration but the empirical results are not demonstrating this: One specific way to demonstrate this would be to show and emphasize that HyperDQN is able to achieve better returns on Atari games that are classified to be hard-exploration games. This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper presents HyperDQN as a practical algorithm of Randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI).  HyperDQN consists of two parametric models: first is a base model that is similar to that of a DQN agent and the second a meta model that parameterizes the last layer of the Q-network as a function of a latent variable. The purpose of this architecture is to generate posterior samples using the diverse Q-value functions represented through this architecture. The paper demonstrates this approach empirically across a variety of Atari games, SuperMarioBros and DeepSea.

Main Review:
The paper begins with a strong motivation towards computationally implementing RLSVI in Deep RL using a base and meta models, and also points out reasons why this is non-trivial. Also, the main differences between BootDQN and HyperDQN are also discussed, which is important because BootDQN is a highly relevant prior work in this domain. Overall the paper is well-written and has strong motivations that are relevant to the RL community at large. 

The following are some of the questions based on my understanding of the paper and would be great if the authors could clarify some of them: 

1. The meta-model in HyperDQN is considered as a linear parameterized model and there is a theoretical justification for this. What would happen to the empirical/theoretical results if the meta-model is a non-linear function of the latent variable z? I am inclined to think that the representation power for the posterior distribution would be better and thus should produce improvements overall.

2. How are the sigma_w and sigma_p parameters chosen in the objective function? Have the authors considered a baseline where an agent uses an identical artificial noise term for modifying the Q_{target} (i.e., sigma_w z^{\top} \epsilon_i ) to the one used in HyperDQN? 

3. The Atari performance curves summarized in Fig 3 is not sufficient to demonstrate the utility of the presented approach. This curve is computed over all the Atari games (some of them require no exploration and some require a complex strategy for exploration). It would be more appropriate to show how the agent performs on “hard-exploration” and “easy-exploration” games (this classification is presented in Bellemare et al. 2016) rather than present a summary across all games. 

Also, why are the agents only trained for 20M frames? It is conventional in Atari to run the agents for 200M frames (Machado et al. 2018). This makes it easier to compare the performance of HyperDQN wrto the prior methods in Atari. 

A minor comment: why do some curves in Fig 3 start at 0 and another starts at a value lower than 0? 

Bellemare, M., Srinivasan, S., Ostrovski, G., Schaul, T., Saxton, D., and Munos, R. Unifying count-based exploration and intrinsic motivation. In NeurIPS, 2016.

Machado, M. C., Bellemare, M. G., Talvitie, E., Veness, J., Hausknecht, M., & Bowling, M. (2018). Revisiting the arcade learning environment: Evaluation protocols and open problems for general agents. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 61, 523-562.

4. How many random seeds were used to produce the learning curves in all the experiments (Atari, Mario and DeepSea)? It would be useful to add this information in the main text. Also, are the error bars reported in each of the presented learning curves?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I think the contribution would be important to the RL community. My main concerns with the paper is that the presented approach claims to be helpful for exploration but the empirical results are not demonstrating this: One specific way to demonstrate this would be to show and emphasize that HyperDQN is able to achieve better returns on Atari games that are classified to be hard-exploration games. This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes a new randomized exploration method termed HyperDQN. 

It aims to improve the popular Randomized Least Square Value Iteration (RLSVI) method by addressing RLSVI's major limitations of computational burden and its requirement of known good features in advance to adopt the linear setting. 

The principal of HyperDQN is to combine DQN with a probabilistic hypermodel which could generate posterior samples for the parameter values of the weights at the last linear layer of the Q-function. Both the hypermodel and the Q-function could be jointly optimized during training. By sampling the Q-function parameters, it helps the model to select exploratory action sequences.

 Empirical evaluation results are provided in Atari 2600 domain, SuperMarioBros and deep sea. Overall, the proposed method could outperform Bootstrapped DQN, Double DQN and another recent bootstrapping baseline termed OB2I in majority of the testified domains.
reviews.main_review:
$\textbf{Strength}$: 
- This work tackles an important problem of exploiting randomized exploration methods in deep reinforcement learning problems.
- This paper is very well written and easy to follow.
- Overall the method of combining hypermodel with Q-network is quite sound and very general to be applied on deep RL problems.
- The empirical evaluation results are very extensive where relevant baselines are considered for comparison.

$\textbf{Weakness}$:
- Very limited novelty where the idea is from the work [1]. 
- During comparison, only results at intermediate training stage  (i.e., 20M frames)  is presented while the detailed scores for the full training is missing. So it is unclear if the method could really be the next state-of-the-art method for Atari 2600 test suite.
-Only limited number of baselines are considered. There is not decent exploration methods, such as UCB-type exploration or ramdomized exploration with noisy nets, being considered for comparison.  


$\textbf{Detailed comments}$:

1. My primary concern is about the novelty of this paper. I found that both the formulation for hypermodel and the loss function for optimizing the hypermodel is inherited from the existing work [1]. In [1], a more general formulation for the hypermodel has been discussed which includes linear models, neural models and additive prior models. In HyperDQN, a linear hypermodel is adopted where there is nothing new for the method. Thus the contribution of this paper is not in terms of introducing a new method but is to show additional experimental results for an existing method on several deep RL domains. I feel such contribution might not be significant enough to be published as a full paper in ICLR.

2. This paper tackles the policy learning problem from a direction of considering randomized exploration method. It claims the randomized exploration method O2BI as a SOTA exploration method but I do not think so. Generally, the O2BI as well as HyperDQN is only compared with limited baselines when the training is incomplete. I feel that many other variants of UCB-type algorithms, such as noisy net [2] might be able to outperform O2BI or HyperDQN greatly with 200M training budget. For instance, prediction-error based exploration methods could effectively progress on the extremely hard exploration task Montezuma's Revenge while O2BI and HyperDQN make no progress on it. Therefore, a more inclusive exploration methods for discussion/comparison is desired. 

3. The training curves presented in Fig 12 come with no error bars and/or O2BI is missing from the curves.

4. I feel noisy net is also a related randomized exploration model which should be considered for discussion and/or experimental comparison.

[1] HYPERMODELS FOR EXPLORATION
[2] NOISY NETWORKS FOR EXPLORATION

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a new randomized exploration method termed HyperDQN. 

It aims to improve the popular Randomized Least Square Value Iteration (RLSVI) method by addressing RLSVI's major limitations of computational burden and its requirement of known good features in advance to adopt the linear setting. 

The principal of HyperDQN is to combine DQN with a probabilistic hypermodel which could generate posterior samples for the parameter values of the weights at the last linear layer of the Q-function. Both the hypermodel and the Q-function could be jointly optimized during training. By sampling the Q-function parameters, it helps the model to select exploratory action sequences.

 Empirical evaluation results are provided in Atari 2600 domain, SuperMarioBros and deep sea. Overall, the proposed method could outperform Bootstrapped DQN, Double DQN and another recent bootstrapping baseline termed OB2I in majority of the testified domains.

Main Review:
$\textbf{Strength}$: 
- This work tackles an important problem of exploiting randomized exploration methods in deep reinforcement learning problems.
- This paper is very well written and easy to follow.
- Overall the method of combining hypermodel with Q-network is quite sound and very general to be applied on deep RL problems.
- The empirical evaluation results are very extensive where relevant baselines are considered for comparison.

$\textbf{Weakness}$:
- Very limited novelty where the idea is from the work [1]. 
- During comparison, only results at intermediate training stage  (i.e., 20M frames)  is presented while the detailed scores for the full training is missing. So it is unclear if the method could really be the next state-of-the-art method for Atari 2600 test suite.
-Only limited number of baselines are considered. There is not decent exploration methods, such as UCB-type exploration or ramdomized exploration with noisy nets, being considered for comparison.  


$\textbf{Detailed comments}$:

1. My primary concern is about the novelty of this paper. I found that both the formulation for hypermodel and the loss function for optimizing the hypermodel is inherited from the existing work [1]. In [1], a more general formulation for the hypermodel has been discussed which includes linear models, neural models and additive prior models. In HyperDQN, a linear hypermodel is adopted where there is nothing new for the method. Thus the contribution of this paper is not in terms of introducing a new method but is to show additional experimental results for an existing method on several deep RL domains. I feel such contribution might not be significant enough to be published as a full paper in ICLR.

2. This paper tackles the policy learning problem from a direction of considering randomized exploration method. It claims the randomized exploration method O2BI as a SOTA exploration method but I do not think so. Generally, the O2BI as well as HyperDQN is only compared with limited baselines when the training is incomplete. I feel that many other variants of UCB-type algorithms, such as noisy net [2] might be able to outperform O2BI or HyperDQN greatly with 200M training budget. For instance, prediction-error based exploration methods could effectively progress on the extremely hard exploration task Montezuma's Revenge while O2BI and HyperDQN make no progress on it. Therefore, a more inclusive exploration methods for discussion/comparison is desired. 

3. The training curves presented in Fig 12 come with no error bars and/or O2BI is missing from the curves.

4. I feel noisy net is also a related randomized exploration model which should be considered for discussion and/or experimental comparison.

[1] HYPERMODELS FOR EXPLORATION
[2] NOISY NETWORKS FOR EXPLORATION

title: Learning Synthetic Environments and Reward Networks for Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces Synthetic Environments and Synthetic Rewards as a bi-level optimization problem. The main idea is to find SEs and REs such that an RL agent trained under them will do well in a given, known target environment, without further training.
reviews.main_review:
Results:
- In Figure 2, the performance seems very unstable: In particular, the reward for individual agents seems to fluctuate widely and even the average starts drooping after the 50th iteration in the arcobot task. It would be great to know what the source of this instability is. 

Conceptual:
- There are repeated claims in the paper that the synthetic environment "focuses the learning" on the relevant states or learns "representations" of the target environment ("an informed representation of the target environment by
modifying the state distributions to bias agents towards relevant states"). This claim is entirely unclear to me at the moment. In particular, there simply doesn't seem to be any reason that the SE should have much in common with the ground truth environment. The only condition is that _optimal policies_ for the two environments match. In particular, there will be many environments in which the optimal policy is likely much easier to learn than in the original Env. 
For example, you can imagine a state transition dynamic that is entirely random but provides a large reward for the optimal action in each of the given states. Another option is to learn environment dynamics where the optimal action in each state deterministically transitions to an arbitrary next state, such that the agent will see all required states during training. 
It would be great to provide any type of evidence that there is actually semantic meaning in the SE that matches the underlying ground truth environment.  

Other comments:
- In Figure 2, it seems like the legend is switched? I.e. dashed line should be the solved threshold?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
A very interesting paper with early results in a potentially interesting direction. It is currently unclear what this method allows to do that was previously impossible from a practical point of view, but there seems to be promise. 
I'd be open to raising my score if my concerns are addressed by the authors. 



Update: 
Based on the discussion phase and the rebuttal I have updated the score to a 6. I believe this paper has enough merit to be published at ICLR, if there is space. It is an intriguing piece of work, even though the practical utility is very unclear at this point.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes learning Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Reward Networks (RNs) as neural proxies for full environment dynamics and reward shaping via a bi-level optimization framework. An inner-loop RL agent trains on the proxy, while an outer-loop evolution strategies (ES) procedure updates the proxy parameters to maximize performance in the true target environment. Once learned, SEs can train new agents without further real-environment interactions, and RNs can accelerate and shape reward signals. Empirical evaluation on classic control benchmarks (CartPole, Acrobot, CliffWalking, MountainCarContinuous, and HalfCheetah) shows substantial sample-efficiency gains, robustness to hyperparameter variation, and transfer to unseen agents. The authors open-source code and models to support reproducibility.

Strengths  
- Novelty & Scope: The idea of end-to-end, performance-driven proxy learning for both dynamics and rewards is conceptually fresh. By avoiding unstable meta-gradients and using ES, the method remains agent-agnostic and compatible with a variety of inner-loop RL algorithms (value-based and actor-critic).  
- Empirical Breadth: The paper evaluates SEs and RNs across five well-known control tasks, multiple RL algorithms, and transfer scenarios. Quantitatively, agents trained on SEs or shaped by RNs match or exceed baseline performance while requiring 30–65% fewer real-environment steps.  
- Reproducibility & Analysis: Detailed ablations (e.g., RNs vs. full SEs), hyperparameter-sensitivity studies, and qualitative visualizations of state and reward distributions enrich the evaluation. Code and trained models are released.

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Result Stability & Clarity (Main Review Point + Alt Reviews 2,3): Figure 2 shows pronounced instability, particularly in the Acrobot task where performance fluctuations and a drop after the 50th iteration raise questions about ES noise, proxy overfitting, or inner-loop RL instability. A more thorough analysis of these variance sources—and perhaps comparisons to alternative outer-loop updates (e.g., meta-gradients)—is warranted.  
2. Semantic Meaning of SEs (Main Review Point + Alt Review 1 & 4): The paper repeatedly claims that SEs “focus the learning” on relevant states and capture “informed representations” of the true environment. Yet, by construction, many arbitrary SEs with equivalent optimal policies could exist without preserving any semantic structure. Providing empirical evidence—such as visualizing learned dynamics in relation to ground truth, or measuring representation distances—would support the claim of meaningful semantic alignment.  
3. Limited Domain & Scalability (Alt Reviews 1–4): All experiments are confined to low-dimensional, fully observable control tasks. Attempts to scale to pixel-based or high-dimensional environments (e.g., vision-based Atari or complex continuous control like Humanoid) remain unreported or unsuccessful under modest ES budgets. It is unclear how proxy learning would fare under partial observability, longer horizons, or safety-critical constraints.  
4. Computational Cost & Practicality (Alt Reviews 1–4): The bi-level ES process requires thousands of real-environment interactions and dozens of parallel workers, potentially offsetting its sample-efficiency gains in expensive simulators or robotics applications. Reporting wall-clock training times, ES population sizes, and resource-use trade-offs would help contextualize the real-world applicability.  
5. Comparison to Model-Based Baselines (Alt Reviews 1–4): While a brief supervised dynamics baseline is mentioned, dedicated comparisons to state-of-the-art model-based RL (e.g., PETS, World Models) or curriculum-based approaches are limited. Such baselines would clarify whether the primary benefit derives from the agent-aware bi-level loss or simply from having any proxy.  
6. Hyperparameter & Ablation Depth (Alt Reviews 2–4): Although some RN variants and ES hyperparameters are explored, a systematic ablation on critical choices—such as ES population size, score transformations, proxy network capacity, and early-stopping criteria for RNs—would increase confidence in robustness and guide practitioners.

Other Minor Points  
- In Figure 2, the legend appears switched: the dashed line seems to correspond to the solved threshold.  
- The manuscript lacks any theoretical discussion of SE convergence guarantees or conditions under which proxy learning may mislead agent training.

Recommendation  
Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
This work advances a promising direction in agent-aware proxy learning for RL and delivers compelling preliminary gains on classic benchmarks. However, the concerns around stability, semantic interpretability, scalability, and baseline comparisons must be addressed to fully assess practical impact. I would be willing to raise my score if the authors provide deeper analyses of instability sources, stronger empirical evidence of semantic alignment, scalability studies to higher-dimensional tasks, wall-clock cost reporting, and thorough comparisons to model-based RL.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.
reviews.main_review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript.

1. The writing is extremely clear & detailed, and makes the paper a pleasure to read. I also found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations.

2. The authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was setup in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. Truly commendable, and a great example of what good ML research looks like (I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop).

3. The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective; it honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning syntethic environment models.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses:

4. The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have a feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process.

5. Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine):

>Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**.

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). 

In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effecitveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis.


### Nits / Additional comments

Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).

Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, good research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.

Main Review:

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative).

My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).

This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, good research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This manuscript introduces Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Reward Networks (RNs) as learned neural proxies for typically non-learned components of a reinforcement learning (RL) loop—namely transition dynamics and reward functions. Through a bi-level optimization scheme, an inner loop trains an arbitrary RL agent on the proxy, while an outer loop uses evolution strategies (ES) on real-environment performance to update proxy parameters. The authors demonstrate across classic control tasks (CartPole, Acrobot, CliffWalking, MountainCarContinuous, HalfCheetah) that SEs can train new agents with fewer real-environment steps, RNs accelerate agent learning, and both transfer to unseen algorithms. The code and detailed appendices are released to support reproducibility.

Strengths:  
1. Clarity and Writing: The prose is exemplary—extremely clear, logically structured, and anticipates reader questions. Complex methodological details and hyperparameter choices are explained thoroughly, making the work a pleasure to read.  
2. Novelty and Agent-Agnostic Design: Framing environment and reward proxies as performance-driven meta-learning objectives is an elegant extension of prior reward learning and model-based RL literature. The use of ES in the outer loop sidesteps unstable meta-gradients and is compatible with both value-based and actor-critic inner-loop algorithms.  
3. Empirical Thoroughness and Reproducibility: Experiments span five benchmarks, multiple RL algorithms, analyses of hyperparameter robustness, and cross-agent transfer. The open-source release of code, models, and detailed appendices—including ablations on RN variants—sets a strong reproducibility standard.  
4. Effective Sample Efficiency Gains: Agents trained on SEs or augmented by RNs achieve 30–65% faster convergence while matching or exceeding baseline performance, demonstrating the practical utility of the approach on toy control domains.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Scope and Scalability: All tasks are low-dimensional and fully observable. It remains unclear how SEs and RNs scale to high-dimensional or pixel-based domains, partial observability, or real-world robotics. A mid-scale benchmark (e.g., full HalfCheetah with larger ES budgets or vision tasks) or at least preliminary results on image inputs would strengthen claims of generality.  
2. Computational Cost: The ES outer loop requires thousands of real-environment interactions and parallel workers, which may offset benefits in expensive simulators. Reporting wall-clock runtimes, sample-vs-compute trade-offs, and sensitivity to ES hyperparameters (population size, noise scale) would contextualize resource requirements.  
3. Comparison to Model-Based Baselines: While a supervised dynamics model is briefly mentioned, explicit comparisons to state-of-the-art model-based RL methods (e.g., PETS, World Models) or curriculum generators are limited. Such baselines would clarify the advantage of the bi-level, agent-aware loss.  
4. Hypothesis Clarity and Theoretical Insight: Some explanatory hypotheses—for example, that optimizing SEs yields “efficient synthetic state dynamics” leading to fast agent training—feel qualitative. More diagnostic analyses, theoretical discussion of when proxy biases help or hinder, and exploration of potential failure modes would deepen understanding.  
5. Paper Density and Organization: Methodology and experimental sections are dense; some ablations (e.g., ES design choices, score transformations) are deferred to the appendix. Consider reorganizing or splitting content (e.g., separate ES vs. RN focus) to improve readability.

Nits:  
– Figure 2’s legend inverts threshold labels (dotted vs. solid).  
– The claim that “learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions” would benefit from a citation or a more nuanced discussion, as stochastic rewards can be more challenging in some MDPs.

Decision and Rating:  
I am very enthusiastic about this work’s clarity, reproducibility, and empirical contributions. While questions about scalability and computational cost remain, they do not detract from the paper’s core novelty and strong results on the benchmarks studied. I recommend acceptance (8: good paper).
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.

Main Review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript.

1. The writing is extremely clear & detailed, and makes the paper a pleasure to read. I also found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations.

2. The authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was setup in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. Truly commendable, and a great example of what good ML research looks like (I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop).

3. The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective; it honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning syntethic environment models.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses:

4. The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have a feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process.

5. Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine):

>Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**.

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). 

In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effecitveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis.


### Nits / Additional comments

Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).

Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).

Summary Of The Review:
This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, good research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes to learn neural environment models they term Synthetic Environments, such that planning in the model with model-free RL results in good performance in the real environment.
The authors study learning full models as well as reward-only models they call Reward Networks, investigating the feasibility of learning these models as well as their robustness to different inner-loop algorithms and hyperparameters.
reviews.main_review:
This is an interesting line of work and initial investigation. The exposition of the method and description of experiments are quite clear and complete. However, there are a few weaknesses.

The motivation for this work deserves more attention. The authors suggest that SE’s could be used for AutoML, iterating on algorithms in expensive environments like robotics, agent or task analysis, or agent pre-training. However, I’m not sure these are particularly well aligned with the authors’ statement of the problem, implementation, or experiments.
Most applications are predicated on the relative performance of various algorithms, when trained in the SE, being correlated with performance in the true environment.
However, the problem formulation in eqn (1) describes maximising performance absolutely.
This is approximated in Alg. 1 with a generic TrainAgent function that approximates the argmax_theta. But it seems the specific TrainAgent functions chosen are highly relevant to potential use cases – it is not a generic stand-in for an argmax.
The authors certainly understand the relevance of this, as they study generalisation of SE’s to different algorithms and their hyperparameters, and even address generalisation algorithmically by varying hyperparameters during training of the SEs. However, this is introduced as a brief comment in the experiment descriptions, rather than being central to the method.
It feels that to use SE’s for the potential applications discussed, it would make more sense to reformulate the problem statement to explicitly account for different inner-loop algorithms.
Further, simply maximising the final performance of all inner-loop algorithms seems quite limited when applications involve comparing different algorithms.
It might be interesting to algorithmically address this use-case by changing the fitness function to account for the relative performance of different algorithms.
However, it would also be useful to address this empirically given the current formulation of the objective, and the authors likely already have the data to do so, at least partially: does the performance of different HPs/algorithms *in the SEs* correlate with their performance on the true environment?

It may also be possible to motivate the work from a more traditional model-based RL point of view, which might be more closely aligned to the problem statement given in the paper. A closely related work is that of Nikishin et al [1] (who use implicit differentiation to address the bi-level optimisation). NB I don’t fault the authors for not citing this, I believe it is only available as preprint. They argue that models learned only to further optimisation of a policy (as in this work's problem statement) may perform better than traditional models when capacity is limited. However, the ES-based bi-level optimisation is expensive, so it may be difficult to make a strong case for this approach.

Another weakness of the study is the simplicity of the environments used. I understand the authors may have limited computational resources, but it is difficult to know how highly to weight an empirical feasibility study carried out on such small-scale tasks. In the absence of more compelling empirical findings, it is more critical to explore the algorithmic choices and motivations.

In this vein, I did appreciate the investigation of which inductive biases might help during reward learning. Overall, I find the case for RNs is perhaps more intuitive: dense-ifying rewards once in an expensive optimisation to allow fast iteration later. Using an additive potential form even guarantees that this will not change the optimal behaviour – sadly, it seems this type of RN did not outperform the baseline much in the experiments.

In the RN case optimising for the speed of training makes sense, but I still find the chosen objective strange. Why not optimise the area-under-curve returns for the full inner-loop? Clipping at a somewhat arbitrary solution threshold seems like it would result in suboptimal RNs (this appears to have occurred in the half-cheetah experiment in the appendix).

Minor comments:
 - In the SE experiments, it would be good to show the performance of the ‘fixed’ hyperparams evaluated with the same fixed hyperparams: this would show the ‘generalisation gap’ compared to evaluating with varied hyperparms as is currently shown.
 - Overall, many figures are a bit hard to parse. Try using more smoothing or subsampling for curves. For the barplots, maybe re-arrange them so the comparable bars (i.e. measuring the same metric, ‘steps’ or ‘episodes’, are next to each other).

[1] “Control-Oriented Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Differentiation” Nikishin et al 2021

----------------------

Update: the authors have addressed many of my concerns, ran some additional useful experiments, and clarified their reasoning on several points in the overall discussion with reviewers. I still think there is a bit of a gap between the big-picture motivation for the work and the instantiation we see studied empirically; the authors are working on an empirical investigation to help make that connection. However, even in its absence, I am sufficiently positive about the paper overall now to lean towards acceptance -- I found the study interesting and creative.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained at not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation. The empirical study is fairly clear but limited by the simplicity of the domains and the restriction largely to assessing feasibility rather than giving clearer indications of how the method could be used in practice.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper introduces Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Reward Networks (RNs) as neural‐network proxies for RL environments and reward functions. SEs jointly learn state dynamics and rewards via a bi‐level optimization: an inner loop trains an arbitrary RL agent on the proxy, and an outer loop uses evolution strategies (ES) to optimize the proxy parameters for performance in the real environment. RNs restrict the proxy to reward shaping only. Experiments on CartPole, Acrobot, CliffWalking, MountainCarContinuous and HalfCheetah demonstrate that once an SE or RN is learned, new agents can be trained with significantly fewer real‐environment interactions, are robust to hyperparameter variations, and transfer to unseen algorithms. The authors also provide qualitative analyses of learned state representations and open‐source their code and models for reproducibility.

Strengths  
1. Novel, Agent‐Agnostic Framework: Building on a clear bi‐level formulation, the paper presents a performance‐driven way to learn entire MDP proxies (SEs) or reward modifiers (RNs) without requiring meta‐gradient backpropagation. This allows compatibility with a broad class of inner‐loop RL algorithms, from Q‐learning to actor‐critic methods.  
2. Thorough Empirical Study: The authors evaluate across five classic control tasks, multiple RL algorithms, and both SE and RN variants. They investigate hyperparameter sensitivity, transfer to unseen agents, and even qualitative biases in the learned representations. These experiments convincingly show 30–65% speedups in sample efficiency while matching or exceeding baseline performance.  
3. Clear Exposition & Reproducibility: The method is presented in detail, with pseudocode, objective derivations, and variant descriptions. The release of code, trained models, and hyperparameter settings supports reproducibility and community adoption.  
4. Investigation of Inductive Biases: The paper’s study of Reward Network forms (e.g., additive potential‐based shaping) is insightful, showing that density‐adjusted rewards can accelerate inner‐loop learning without changing optimal policies.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Motivation Alignment and Problem Formulation  
   • The high‐level motivations—AutoML for robotics, algorithm analysis, pre‐training—rest on the assumption that relative performance in SEs correlates with that in the real environment. However, the core problem statement (Eqn. 1) and Algorithm 1 optimize absolute performance of a single inner‐loop agent, rather than preserving rank correlations among multiple algorithms. To bridge this gap, it would help to reformulate the outer‐loop fitness (or introduce a multi‐algorithm objective) that explicitly captures relative performance, or at minimum present empirical correlations between hyperparameter/algorithm rankings in SEs versus the real environment.  
   • A more direct connection to control‐oriented model‐based RL (e.g., Nikishin et al.’s implicit‐differentiation approach) could ground the work in existing literature and highlight when a purely performance‐driven proxy outperforms traditional supervised dynamics models under capacity constraints.

2. Limited Domain Scope and Scalability  
   • All experiments are confined to low‐dimensional, fully observable control tasks. As noted in the appendix, modest ES budgets failed to scale SEs to higher‐dimensional benchmarks (e.g., Humanoid). It remains unclear whether SEs and RNs can handle pixel‐based, partially observable, or procedurally generated domains. Adding at least one mid‐scale continuous control or image‐based experiment—or presenting a more detailed failure analysis—would strengthen the paper’s claims of generality.  
   • The method’s reliance on ES with large populations and many parallel workers translates to high computational cost: thousands of real‐environment interactions and heavy wall‐clock time. Reporting actual runtimes for SE and RN training, and contrasting them with standard model‐based RL baselines (e.g., PETS, World Models), would contextualize resource requirements and practical applicability.

3. Comparisons and Ablations  
   • Comparison to supervised dynamics models is currently limited to a single ablation with poor performance. A more systematic baseline—training a dynamics model on the same agent trajectories and evaluating planning or rollout‐based policy optimization—would clarify the benefit of the bi‐level, agent‐aware loss.  
   • Key hyperparameters of the ES outer loop (population size, noise scale, score transformations) and RN objective choices (threshold clipping versus area‐under‐curve) warrant ablation. For instance, why clip returns at an arbitrary solution threshold when optimizing the full learning curve might yield better RNs?  
   • To assess generalization gaps, it would be helpful to compare SE performance under fixed versus varied inner‐loop hyperparameters, as suggested. Showing how the learned proxy generalizes across different algorithm families and learning budgets would make the case for robustness.

4. Theoretical and Algorithmic Insights  
   • The paper would benefit from brief theoretical discussion or guarantees on when a learned SE or RN can faithfully preserve policy optima, especially under model capacity limits.  
   • Exploring alternative outer‐loop optimizers (e.g., meta‐gradients) could reduce sample complexity or improve proxy fidelity, and comparing them to ES in a small study would be informative.

Minor Comments  
– Several plots are dense and noisy. Consider additional smoothing or subsampling for learning curves, and re‐arranging bar charts so that comparable metrics (‘steps’ vs. ‘episodes’) are adjacent.  
– In Experiment 3, reporting the generalization gap for fixed hyperparameters as well as varied ones would clarify the benefits of hyperparameter randomization during SE training.  
– A table summarizing wall‐clock time and number of environment steps per method would aid readers in evaluating trade‐offs.

Decision and Rating  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
Despite the noted gaps between high‐level motivation and empirical instantiation, the paper offers a novel, well‐evaluated framework for learning performance‐driven environment and reward proxies. The strengths in empirical design, agent‐agnostic applicability, and reproducibility justify a marginal acceptance, provided the above clarifications and additional analyses can be addressed.
