The memory behavior of sampled methods is vastly different than non-sampled GNNs when dealing with distributed execution or on graphs that are substantially larger than single-device memory capacity.  Or the focus on O(V)/O(E), which glosses over the fact the average degree is small (between 2 and 13 on their chosen OGB datasets) and ignores other 'constants' on the same order. This is technically correct but misleading. None of these invalidate the authors' methods, but it's somewhat disingenuous.

Also, it very much felt like parts of the paper were aimed at different goals. The intro and background lean heavily into hardware efficiency, while other parts of the paper (S3 outside of the last paragraph; S5.1-5.3) seem to ignore it.

Summary Of The Review:
The authors take a different tact with their proposed GNN, which is welcome. They explain it clearly, and the paper's writing and organization are solid. The results support their claim that they can achieve competitive accuracy, but the quantitative computation and memory results are somewhat underwhelming. In my view, the contribution of this paper is its approach and challenge to convention, which feels good enough to publish, even if it left me wanting more substantive.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces Efficient Graph Convolution (EGC), an isotropic graph neural network that challenges the prevailing assumption that anisotropic message functions are required for high accuracy. EGC achieves spatially varying adaptive filtering by combining a small set of shared basis weight matrices with per-node combination coefficients, reducing memory and latency complexity from O(E) to O(V). The authors present two variants—EGC-S (single symmetric aggregator) and EGC-M (multiple aggregators)—and offer both spatial and spectral interpretations. Extensive experiments on six large-scale benchmarks (including ZINC, CIFAR-10 Superpixels, MolHIV, Code-V2, Arxiv, and OGB-MAG) demonstrate that EGC matches or outperforms popular anisotropic methods such as GAT, GATv2, and PNA, while consuming less GPU memory and inference time. The paper is well organized, with especially clear rationale in Appendix C, and the authors release code and pretrained models to facilitate reproducibility.

Strengths  
•  Challenging folk wisdom The core contribution is a direct challenge to the community’s belief that anisotropy is essential for strong GNN performance. By showing that an isotropic model can match or exceed anisotropic baselines, the work opens new avenues for model design.  
•  Hardware efficiency focus Section 3.2’s “aggregator fusion” optimization is a well-motivated, practical trick that leverages sparse matrix multiplication to reduce memory-bound overhead. The paper’s profiling on both CPU and GPU adds valuable evidence for real-world deployments.  
•  Clear model exposition The layer formulations are explained with intuitive spatial and spectral perspectives. Readers may find the appendices—particularly the detailed pseudocode in Appendix C—more accessible than the main text’s aggregator discussion.  
•  Thorough empirical evaluation Results span both inductive and transductive settings, parameter-normalized baselines, and ablations over heads, bases, and activation choices. EGC-S and EGC-M consistently perform on par with, or slightly better than, established anisotropic GNNs across diverse tasks.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1.  Under-emphasized quantitative support for efficiency Although the paper claims significant memory and latency gains, the reported speedups (e.g., accuracy-normalized runtime) are modest. Moreover, focusing solely on high-watermark GPU memory overlooks parallelism effects and the behavior of sampling-based methods on large graphs or in distributed settings. A more comprehensive hardware analysis—possibly including CPU wall-clock times, energy measurements, or end-to-end accelerator benchmarks—would strengthen the efficiency argument.  
2.  Manual aggregator selection in EGC-M The choice of multiple aggregators is based on heuristics rather than an automated or learned procedure. Developing a principled strategy or incorporating differentiable aggregator selection could improve both generality and ease of adoption.  
3.  Limited evaluation on heterophilous and edge-rich graphs All primary benchmarks exhibit high homophily, leaving open questions about EGC’s behavior on strongly heterophilous datasets (e.g., Chameleon, Cornell) or tasks with rich edge attributes. Including at least one non-homophilous benchmark and exploring simple edge-feature extensions would broaden the paper’s scope.  
4.  Theoretical expressivity and formal analysis While the spatial and spectral interpretations are insightful, the paper stops short of formally characterizing EGC’s expressive power relative to anisotropic MPNNs (e.g., via the Weisfeiler–Leman hierarchy). Adding sharper theoretical bounds or conditions under which isotropic filters suffice would lend greater rigor.  
5.  Baseline tuning and presentation details The manuscript would benefit from clearer documentation of hyperparameter search budgets for baselines to ensure fair comparison. A concise layer summary table or high-level diagram early in the paper would also help readers navigate the dense notation in Sections 3–4.

Overall Assessment  
EGC represents a valuable, well-motivated departure from conventional anisotropic GNN designs, combining elegant architectural ideas with practical hardware considerations. The proposed model is clearly described, and the extensive experiments underscore its competitive accuracy and resource footprint. At the same time, the paper’s central claims around efficiency would be more convincing with deeper quantitative hardware analysis, automated aggregator strategies, and broader benchmark coverage.  

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a different formulation for a graph neural network, focused on achieving accuracy equal to anisotropic GNNs without anisotropic mechanisms.

Main Review:
This paper is a mixed bag. On one hand, I appreciate the authors' vision: take some things we've learned about GNNs (heads, aggregators), revisit first principles (isotropic GNNs), try to come up with something new that blends them. In addition, I appreciate their take on computational efficiency: they correctly seize upon the notion that a well-understood algorithmic kernel can have meaningful computational impact, and they build on its advantages (e.g., section 3.2-aggregator fusion). On the other hand, many of the conclusions leave something to be desired. The analyses and descriptions sometimes gloss over important factors, and I wish the results delivered a stronger takeaway to match the ambition. For all the talk about computation and memory efficiency, the actual numbers are not all that substantial.

Strengths:

- The authors' direct challenge to accepted folk knowledge about anisotropic GNNs is healthy and welcome. As models evolve over time, it is important for the community to revisit fundamental positions on model components. The paper opens a lot of areas for other researchers to build off it, which I see as a major strength.
- The proposed model is clearly explained and fairly easy to follow. The intrepretations are useful and provide good intuition for how to place their proposed model in the context of other current work.
- The appendices actually provide a lot of useful content, sometimes more than the paper itself. The rationale in appendix C, for instance, was far clearer than the paper's 'aggregator fusion' section in 3.2 (even without the algorithm).

Weaknesses:

- Some of the evaluation results are weak. The accuracy-normalized results in seem to demonstrate that EGC-S  is not all that much faster or smaller than GCN. It also seems to indicate that parameter-normalized results do not correlate particularly well with memory footprint across models, which weakens the argument for O(V) vs. O(E).
- Despite a nominally primary argument of the paper being about memory efficiency and computational performance, very little quantitative results support that argument. I agree that there is definitely a lot of potential for this approach to be efficient, but I wish the paper had strong support for it.

- The authors fail to disambiguate different types of memory effects. For instance, while high-watermark memory footprint (which is mostly what is measured in their experiments) is important, they overlook the effects of parallelism. This leads them, amongst other things, to dismiss sampling methods almost out of hand. The memory behavior of sampled methods is vastly different than non-sampled GNNs when dealing with distributed execution or on graphs that are substantially larger than single-device memory capacity.  Or the focus on O(V)/O(E), which glosses over the fact the average degree is small (between 2 and 13 on their chosen OGB datasets) and ignores other 'constants' on the same order. This is technically correct but misleading. None of these invalidate the authors' methods, but it's somewhat disingenuous.

Also, it very much felt like parts of the paper were aimed at different goals. The intro and background lean heavily into hardware efficiency, while other parts of the paper (S3 outside of the last paragraph; S5.1-5.3) seem to ignore it.

Summary Of The Review:
The authors take a different tact with their proposed GNN, which is welcome. They explain it clearly, and the paper's writing and organization are solid. The results support their claim that they can achieve competitive accuracy, but the quantitative computation and memory results are somewhat underwhelming. In my view, the contribution of this paper is its approach and challenge to convention, which feels good enough to publish, even if it left me wanting more substantive.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper claimed they designed a new GNN architecture that achieves state-of-the-art performance with lower memory consumption and latency. More specifically, the proposed model uses memory proportional to the number of vertices in the graph $O(V)$, in contrast to competing methods which require memory proportional to the number of edges $O(E)$. The paper claimed that the new architecture enabled each vertex to have its own weight matrix, thus following a novel adaptive filtering approach. The experiments found that the proposed efficient model could achieve higher accuracy than competing approaches across six large and varied datasets against strong baselines. Moreover, the experiments demonstrated that the proposed method achieves lower latency and memory consumption for the same accuracy compared to competing approaches.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:
1. This paper applied the idea of "basis weights" and thus effectively uses different weight matrices for different nodes.
2. This paper has provided a long section to help interpret the proposed architecture and compared it with GCN, GAT, and PNA.

### Questions:
1. Since it is noted that different aggregators might result in outputs with different means and variances, for the experiments in Table 1, did you normalize the combination weights $w$ or the different aggregated outputs?
2. I am curious how you implemented the "weighting of bases" per node, i.e., multiplication between the combination weighting coefficients $w$ (different for different nodes) and the aggregated outputs corresponding to different basis weight matrices? Is this step before or after the aggregation? Although the theoretical complexity of this step is $O(V)$, I think the choice of actual implementation might significantly affect the practical time efficiency.

### Weaknesses:
1. The major weakness of this paper, in my opinion, is that this paper seems to have misunderstood the real bottleneck to scale-up GNNs, and the proposed architecture may not be suitable for large graphs (i.e., size over a million).
   - The paper has compared with many existing approaches to solve the scalability issues of GNNs on large graphs. However, this paper did not tackle this problem. Firstly, the theoretical complexities of the proposed architecture are still $O(V)$. Thus on a graph with more than a million nodes (which happens in some node classification or link prediction benchmarks), the memory consumption is still too large for conventional GPUs. Secondly, the main experiments (Table 1) are conducted on four graph regression/classification benchmarks and only one node classification benchmark. However, the graphs in the four graph-level benchmarks are small. The experimental exploration and evidence on large graphs are insufficient.
   - For graph sampling approaches, the paper claimed that `We evaluated a variety of sampling approaches and observed that even modest sampling levels, which provide little benefit to memory or latency, cause model performance to decline noticeably.` The corresponding experimental results are shown in Table 8 in the appendix. However, all four benchmarks used in Table 8 are graph-level tasks (graph regression or classification), where the sizes of input graphs are small. For example, ZINC and MolHIV are molecule graphs, and CIFAR consists of super-pixel sampled graphs whose sizes are at most 150 (Dwivedi et al., 2020). Applying sampling strategies to these small input graphs is not reasonable, and it is not strange to observe significant performance degradation. Actually, the performance of some sampling algorithms on relatively large graphs for node classification is strong, e.g., GraphSAINT (SAGE aggregator) can outperform the "Full-batch" GraphSAGE on *ogbn-products* (see [leaderboard](https://ogb.stanford.edu/docs/leader_nodeprop/)). The quoted sentence above is not appropriate.
   - To sum up, I think the architecture proposed in the paper does not solve the scalability problem on large graphs. Thus the authors should make this point clearer in the related work and experiment sections.
2. Another potential problem, in my opinion, is the spectral interpretation in Section 4.2. The paper claims that `Our approach corresponds to learning multiple filters and computing a linear combination of the resulting filters with weights depending on the attributes of each node locally.` The paper compared two formulas: (1) the ideal linear combination of filters: $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot g_{\theta_b}(\mathbf{L})\mathbf{X}$; and (2) the filtering of proposed model (correspond to EGC-S, Eq.(2)) $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot (\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})\mathbf{X}\Theta_b$.  However, I think the latter formula is not a combination of multiple filters. There is only one filter $(\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})$ (which is left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$) and the weight matrix $\Theta_b$ right multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ should not change the relative magnitude of signals of different frequencies. It is not the same if changing $g(\mathbf{L})$ in the former formula, where the matrix left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ changes.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposed a novel architecture to linearly combine the aggregated outputs using different weight matrices for each node. The model is memory efficient since the memory consumption is $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$. However, the proposed method did not solve the scalability problem of GNNs when applied to considerably large graphs. And it is unclear when the provided memory efficiency is necessary, and thus the proposed method is the first choice. In terms of performance, the improvements compared with PNA are also not very convincing. The significance of this paper might be limited if the efficiency/performance improvements are marginal. I would encourage the author to explore the theoretical understanding of the proposed architecture further. Currently, section 4 is well-written, but the conclusions are limited and may have some flaws. In general, I could not recommend the current manuscript for acceptance.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper claimed they designed a new GNN architecture that achieves state-of-the-art performance with lower memory consumption and latency. More specifically, the proposed model uses memory proportional to the number of vertices in the graph $O(V)$, in contrast to competing methods which require memory proportional to the number of edges $O(E)$. The paper claimed that the new architecture enabled each vertex to have its own weight matrix, thus following a novel adaptive filtering approach. The experiments found that the proposed efficient model could achieve higher accuracy than competing approaches across six large and varied datasets against strong baselines. Moreover, the experiments demonstrated that the proposed method achieves lower latency and memory consumption for the same accuracy compared to competing approaches.

Main Review:
### Strengths:
1. This paper applied the idea of "basis weights" and thus effectively uses different weight matrices for different nodes.
2. This paper has provided a long section to help interpret the proposed architecture and compared it with GCN, GAT, and PNA.

### Questions:
1. Since it is noted that different aggregators might result in outputs with different means and variances, for the experiments in Table 1, did you normalize the combination weights $w$ or the different aggregated outputs?
2. I am curious how you implemented the "weighting of bases" per node, i.e., multiplication between the combination weighting coefficients $w$ (different for different nodes) and the aggregated outputs corresponding to different basis weight matrices? Is this step before or after the aggregation? Although the theoretical complexity of this step is $O(V)$, I think the choice of actual implementation might significantly affect the practical time efficiency.

### Weaknesses:
1. The major weakness of this paper, in my opinion, is that this paper seems to have misunderstood the real bottleneck to scale-up GNNs, and the proposed architecture may not be suitable for large graphs (i.e., size over a million).
   - The paper has compared with many existing approaches to solve the scalability issues of GNNs on large graphs. However, this paper did not tackle this problem. Firstly, the theoretical complexities of the proposed architecture are still $O(V)$. Thus on a graph with more than a million nodes (which happens in some node classification or link prediction benchmarks), the memory consumption is still too large for conventional GPUs. Secondly, the main experiments (Table 1) are conducted on four graph regression/classification benchmarks and only one node classification benchmark. However, the graphs in the four graph-level benchmarks are small. The experimental exploration and evidence on large graphs are insufficient.
   - For graph sampling approaches, the paper claimed that `We evaluated a variety of sampling approaches and observed that even modest sampling levels, which provide little benefit to memory or latency, cause model performance to decline noticeably.` The corresponding experimental results are shown in Table 8 in the appendix. However, all four benchmarks used in Table 8 are graph-level tasks (graph regression or classification), where the sizes of input graphs are small. For example, ZINC and MolHIV are molecule graphs, and CIFAR consists of super-pixel sampled graphs whose sizes are at most 150 (Dwivedi et al., 2020). Applying sampling strategies to these small input graphs is not reasonable, and it is not strange to observe significant performance degradation. Actually, the performance of some sampling algorithms on relatively large graphs for node classification is strong, e.g., GraphSAINT (SAGE aggregator) can outperform the "Full-batch" GraphSAGE on *ogbn-products* (see [leaderboard](https://ogb.stanford.edu/docs/leader_nodeprop/)). The quoted sentence above is not appropriate.
   - To sum up, I think the architecture proposed in the paper does not solve the scalability problem on large graphs. Thus the authors should make this point clearer in the related work and experiment sections.
2. Another potential problem, in my opinion, is the spectral interpretation in Section 4.2. The paper claims that `Our approach corresponds to learning multiple filters and computing a linear combination of the resulting filters with weights depending on the attributes of each node locally.` The paper compared two formulas: (1) the ideal linear combination of filters: $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot g_{\theta_b}(\mathbf{L})\mathbf{X}$; and (2) the filtering of proposed model (correspond to EGC-S, Eq.(2)) $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot (\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})\mathbf{X}\Theta_b$.  However, I think the latter formula is not a combination of multiple filters. There is only one filter $(\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})$ (which is left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$) and the weight matrix $\Theta_b$ right multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ should not change the relative magnitude of signals of different frequencies. It is not the same if changing $g(\mathbf{L})$ in the former formula, where the matrix left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ changes.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a novel architecture to linearly combine the aggregated outputs using different weight matrices for each node. The model is memory efficient since the memory consumption is $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$. However, the proposed method did not solve the scalability problem of GNNs when applied to considerably large graphs. And it is unclear when the provided memory efficiency is necessary, and thus the proposed method is the first choice. In terms of performance, the improvements compared with PNA are also not very convincing. The significance of this paper might be limited if the efficiency/performance improvements are marginal. I would encourage the author to explore the theoretical understanding of the proposed architecture further. Currently, section 4 is well-written, but the conclusions are limited and may have some flaws. In general, I could not recommend the current manuscript for acceptance.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces Efficient Graph Convolution (EGC), an isotropic GNN architecture that adaptively combines a small bank of shared basis weight matrices per node, reducing memory from O(E) to O(V) and enabling sparse-matrix implementations. Two variants are proposed—EGC-S (single aggregator) and EGC-M (multiple aggregators)—and the authors provide both spatial and spectral interpretations, an “aggregator fusion” optimization for inference, and extensive empirical evaluation on six benchmarks. The core claim is that, despite its isotropic form, EGC matches or outperforms anisotropic methods (GAT, GATv2, PNA) in accuracy while offering lower latency and memory consumption.

Strengths  
1. Novel Adaptive Filtering Mechanism  
   • By using per-node combination weights over a shared basis of filters, the paper effectively implements spatially varying filters without materializing messages on every edge, addressing a key hardware bottleneck.  
   • This basis-weight idea is clearly explained and compared against GCN, GAT, and PNA, with both spatial and spectral interpretations that aid conceptual understanding.  

2. Hardware-Oriented Efficiency  
   • The reduction of memory complexity from O(E) to O(V) is a meaningful contribution for moderate-scale graphs, and the “aggregator fusion” trick further optimizes inference by minimizing memory-bound operations.  
   • Empirical measurements on both CPU and GPU demonstrate lower latency and peak memory usage compared to strong baselines under matched parameter budgets.  

3. Thorough Empirical Study  
   • Experiments cover six diverse benchmarks (ZINC, CIFAR-10 Superpixels, MolHIV, Code-V2, Arxiv, OGB-MAG), including both transductive and inductive settings, and ablations on number of heads, bases, aggregator choices, and activation functions.  
   • Public release of code and pretrained models enhances reproducibility and practical impact.  

Questions  
1. Normalization and Aggregation Details  
   The paper notes that different aggregators can produce outputs with varying means and variances. For the results in Table 1, were the combination weights w or the aggregated outputs normalized before weighting?  
2. Implementation of Per-Node Weighting  
   How is the multiplication between node-specific combination coefficients and basis-filtered outputs implemented in practice? Is it applied before or after neighbor aggregation? Given the theoretical O(V) complexity, implementation choices could materially impact empirical latency.  
3. Heterophily and Edge Features  
   Can EGC handle strongly heterophilous graphs (e.g., Chameleon or Cornell) or tasks with rich edge attributes? The current evaluation focuses on homophilous datasets and omits edge features, limiting generality.  
4. Automated Aggregator Selection  
   EGC-M relies on manual heuristics to pick aggregators. Is there a principled or automated approach to select or learn these aggregators for new datasets?  

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Scalability on Very Large Graphs  
   Although EGC’s memory scales as O(V), graphs with millions of nodes still pose significant GPU memory challenges. The paper’s claim of improved scalability is not validated on truly large node-classification benchmarks (e.g., ogbn-products, ogbn-papers100M). Without such evidence, it remains unclear whether O(V) memory is sufficiently hardware-friendly at scale.  
2. Inappropriate Sampling Comparison  
   The critique of graph sampling methods (Table 8) is based on small graph-level tasks (ZINC, MolHIV), where sampling is ill-suited. This does not justify dismissing powerful sampling techniques like GraphSAINT on large node-classification graphs, nor does it support the claim that sampling invariably degrades performance.  
3. Flawed Spectral Interpretation  
   The spectral analogy in Section 4.2 equates EGC’s per-node linear combination of feature transformations with combining distinct filters g₍ᵦ₎(L). However, since EGC applies a single normalization matrix Ã and right-multiplies by Θ₍ᵦ₎, it does not actually learn multiple graph filters in the spectral sense. This undermines the theoretical framing of adaptive filtering.  
4. Manual Heuristics for Aggregator Fusion  
   While aggregator fusion is well motivated, EGC-M’s choice of which aggregators to fuse is based on hand-tuned heuristics rather than a systematic or learned procedure. This may limit generality and reproducibility of the best-performing configurations.  
5. Missing Analysis of Expressivity and Edge Cases  
   Although spatial and spectral intuitions are provided, there is no formal comparison of expressive power relative to anisotropic GNNs (e.g., in the WL hierarchy). Similarly, the sensitivity of EGC to graph heterophily, dynamic graphs, and the inclusion of edge features remains unexamined.  
6. Marginal Performance Gains  
   The accuracy improvements over strong baselines such as PNA are modest, and the significance of these gains is unclear given the extra architectural complexity. Without stronger empirical or theoretical justification, the benefits may not outweigh the limitations.  

Recommendation  
3: Reject—Not Good Enough  
While EGC offers an elegant isotropic alternative with concrete hardware optimizations and a range of empirical results, it falls short in critical areas: true scalability on very large graphs remains unverified, the spectral interpretation has theoretical flaws, and practical issues such as heterophily, edge features, and automated design choices are left unaddressed. The performance improvements, though consistent, are marginal, and the core scalability claim requires validation on larger benchmarks. I encourage the authors to strengthen theoretical analyses—especially around expressive power and filter interpretation—and to extend experiments to heterophilous, large-scale, and edge-rich graph tasks before resubmission.

title: Distributionally Robust Fair Principal Components via Geodesic Descents

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers the fair PCA with distribution ally robust optimization algorithm. The problem is of great interest. The key idea of the proposed method is to consider a penalized loss function with the penalty on the fairness criterion. The distributionally robust optimization and its reformulation is developed for parameter estimation.
reviews.main_review:
The strength of the paper is the rigorous optimization framework to solve the proposed fair PCA, with a nice reformulation and theoretical property. 

The weakness of this paper is in the following folds. First, it is not very clear on the significance of the novelty. The authors cast the PCA problem as the minimization of the penalized reconstructed error. Such a formulation is widely common in the machine learning community. Because of the L2-type loss function, the use of the distributionally robust optimization is not that surprising with the known complication on the penalty part. Second, the proposed method involves the selection of tuning parameter. While the authors seem to avoid the discussion tuning parameter selection. Moreover, the numerical experiments just consider the use of fixed value of these tuning parameters, which makes reader hard to judge the quality of the numerical comparison. Third, due the use of the penalty to engage the fairness, it is not how good the proposed method enables the fairness, especially there is no criterion on the selection of the tuning parameter \lambda. Fourth, under the context of fairness, the authors also avoid the discussion on how the choice of k, the number of PCs, would affect the fairness.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Based on the aforementioned strengths and weakness, I would consider the proposed method contain interest merits, with the conservation of the sufficiency on the methodology novelty, framework rigor, and the applicability in practice.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers the fair PCA with distribution ally robust optimization algorithm. The problem is of great interest. The key idea of the proposed method is to consider a penalized loss function with the penalty on the fairness criterion. The distributionally robust optimization and its reformulation is developed for parameter estimation.

Main Review:
The strength of the paper is the rigorous optimization framework to solve the proposed fair PCA, with a nice reformulation and theoretical property. 

The weakness of this paper is in the following folds. First, it is not very clear on the significance of the novelty. The authors cast the PCA problem as the minimization of the penalized reconstructed error. Such a formulation is widely common in the machine learning community. Because of the L2-type loss function, the use of the distributionally robust optimization is not that surprising with the known complication on the penalty part. Second, the proposed method involves the selection of tuning parameter. While the authors seem to avoid the discussion tuning parameter selection. Moreover, the numerical experiments just consider the use of fixed value of these tuning parameters, which makes reader hard to judge the quality of the numerical comparison. Third, due the use of the penalty to engage the fairness, it is not how good the proposed method enables the fairness, especially there is no criterion on the selection of the tuning parameter \lambda. Fourth, under the context of fairness, the authors also avoid the discussion on how the choice of k, the number of PCs, would affect the fairness.

Summary Of The Review:
Based on the aforementioned strengths and weakness, I would consider the proposed method contain interest merits, with the conservation of the sufficiency on the methodology novelty, framework rigor, and the applicability in practice.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper addresses the problem of fair principal component analysis (PCA) by casting it as a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) problem with a penalty on subgroup reconstruction error disparity. Building on a penalized L₂ reconstruction loss, the authors define moment-based Wasserstein‐type ambiguity sets over each sensitive subgroup and derive a finite-dimensional reformulation of the infinite-dimensional min–max program on the Stiefel manifold. They provide explicit expressions for the robustified objective, propose a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm (with QR‐ or polar‐retractions), and prove a sublinear convergence rate. Empirical evaluations on a diverse collection of real-world datasets demonstrate favorable trade‐offs between overall reconstruction error and subgroup fairness, as well as improved out-of-sample robustness compared to two state-of-the-art fair PCA baselines.

Strengths  
1. Rigorous theoretical framework: The reformulation of the infinite-dimensional DRO into a tractable finite saddle-point problem over orthogonal projections is elegant and technically solid. The derivation of closed-form worst-case terms and the convergence analysis for a nonconvex Riemannian subgradient method represent a noteworthy contribution to robust and fair unsupervised learning.  
2. Clear algorithmic development: The paper presents detailed steps for performing subgradient updates on the Stiefel manifold, leveraging recent advances in manifold optimization. The inclusion of both QR- and polar-based retractions, along with provable sublinear convergence, strengthens confidence in the proposed solver.  
3. Empirical validation: Extensive experiments across fifteen real-world datasets illustrate consistent Pareto improvements over FairPCA and CFPCA baselines in terms of reconstruction accuracy versus subgroup error gap. The authors also report out-of-sample generalization under distributional perturbations, highlighting the robustness benefits of the DRO formulation.  
4. Applicability and extensions: The approach naturally extends to nonbinary sensitive attributes (sketched in the paper) and could be adapted to other ambiguity-set divergences, suggesting potential for broader impact in fairness-aware representation learning.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Novelty and significance of DRO for L₂‐loss: Casting PCA with an L₂ penalty into a DRO framework, while methodologically sound, is a relatively straightforward step given prior work on robust regression and unsupervised DRO. The paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of how its formulation advances beyond existing penalized fair PCA approaches and why the specific Wasserstein moment sets offer a distinct advantage.  
2. Hyperparameter selection (λ, ε) and tuning guidance: Both the fairness penalty λ and ambiguity radii ε play a critical role in balancing accuracy, fairness, and robustness. However, the manuscript omits systematic guidance or automated strategies (e.g., cross-validation heuristics) for choosing these parameters. I recommend adding ablation studies that sweep λ and ε across regimes, and including a validation-based procedure to ground the empirical comparisons.  
3. Computational cost and scalability: While the Riemannian subgradient method is elegant, its runtime and wall-clock performance—especially relative to semidefinite-programming baselines or other robust PCA solvers—are not reported. Please provide runtime per iteration, total training time on large-scale datasets, and discuss complexity with respect to data dimension and sample size.  
4. Sensitivity analyses and assumptions: The theoretical results rest on eigenvalue gap assumptions and marginal probability bounds (Theorem 3.2). It would strengthen the paper to empirically assess how often these conditions hold in practice. Additionally, sensitivity to the choice of retraction (QR vs. polar) and step-size schedule could be evaluated via an ablation study.  
5. Fairness scope and choice of k: The current experiments focus on binary protected groups and fixed numbers of principal components k. Real-world fairness applications often involve multiple subgroups, and the choice of k can markedly affect error disparities. I suggest extending experiments to m > 2 sensitive categories and reporting how varying k influences both reconstruction error and subgroup fairness.  
6. Comparison to broader baselines: The empirical evaluation is limited to two prior fair PCA methods. To better position this work within the landscape of fairness and robustness, consider including recent adversarial-fair PCA approaches or representation-learning baselines (e.g., deep fairness methods), and discuss when the proposed DRO-based method is preferable.  
7. Clarity and presentation: Sections 3 and 4 contain dense notation and heavy proofs. A schematic overview of the reformulation pipeline, along with concise pseudocode for the algorithm, would improve readability. Fixing occasional formatting inconsistencies and adding intuitive commentary around key equations will help a broader audience follow the technical narrative.

Decision  
Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
The paper presents a well‐motivated, theoretically grounded method for distributionally robust fair PCA, supported by solid convergence analysis and extensive empirical results. However, to fully establish its significance and practical impact, the authors should augment the manuscript with hyperparameter selection strategies, runtime and scalability assessments, sensitivity and ablation studies, and broader baseline comparisons. With these revisions, the work would make a valuable contribution to the intersection of fairness, robustness, and unsupervised learning.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the formulation, reformulation and algorithm for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA. The reformulation exploits techniques from distributionally robust optimization, and the algorithm is based on reimannian sub-gradient descent. The theory is tested on UCI datasets.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

1. The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear and the problem formulation is novel.

2. Results on reformulation and riemannian gradient descent render tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors.

3. Numerical experiments are based on real data set and are comprehensive.

Weakness:

1. The formulation simply combines distributioanlly robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks.

2. The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological, but just applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

3. The dimensions of variables in UCI datasets are still relatively low (<=40 according to Table 2). The results can be strengthened by testing on datasets of larger dimensions.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposes a new formulation for PCA and derives a complete set of results with regard to the computational issues of the new results. My only reservation is that most theory are relatively straightforward compared to existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the formulation, reformulation and algorithm for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA. The reformulation exploits techniques from distributionally robust optimization, and the algorithm is based on reimannian sub-gradient descent. The theory is tested on UCI datasets.

Main Review:
Strength:

1. The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear and the problem formulation is novel.

2. Results on reformulation and riemannian gradient descent render tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors.

3. Numerical experiments are based on real data set and are comprehensive.

Weakness:

1. The formulation simply combines distributioanlly robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks.

2. The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological, but just applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

3. The dimensions of variables in UCI datasets are still relatively low (<=40 according to Table 2). The results can be strengthened by testing on datasets of larger dimensions.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes a new formulation for PCA and derives a complete set of results with regard to the computational issues of the new results. My only reservation is that most theory are relatively straightforward compared to existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper studies a distributionally robust, fairness‐aware variant of Principal Component Analysis (PCA). The authors formulate a min–max optimization over projection matrices on the Stiefel manifold that balances total reconstruction error against subgroup error disparity under moment‐based Wasserstein‐type ambiguity sets. By exploiting results from distributionally robust optimization, they reformulate the infinite‐dimensional problem into a finite‐dimensional saddle‐point program with closed‐form worst‐case functions, and develop a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm with provable sublinear convergence. Experiments on several UCI and other real‐world datasets demonstrate improved Pareto trade‐offs between reconstruction accuracy and fairness compared to established FairPCA and CFPCA baselines.

Strengths:
1. Clarity and Motivation: The paper is well written, with a clear motivation for combining distributional robustness and subgroup fairness in unsupervised learning. The problem formulation is novel in its joint treatment of ambiguity sets and fairness penalties.
2. Rigorous Reformulation: The reduction of an infinite‐dimensional DRO to a tractable finite minimax program on the Stiefel manifold is elegant. The authors derive explicit expressions for the robustified objective and its Riemannian subgradient, leveraging recent advances in nonconvex optimization.
3. Convergence Guarantees: The proposed Riemannian subgradient descent is equipped with a sublinear convergence analysis under mild eigenvalue‐gap and probability‐mass assumptions.
4. Empirical Validation: Extensive experiments across fifteen datasets show consistent Pareto improvements in the fairness–accuracy trade‐off, as well as better out‐of‐sample generalization under distribution shifts. The method also compares favorably to state‐of‐the-art baselines like FairPCA and CFPCA.

Weaknesses:
1. Incremental Novelty: Although the integration of DRO and fairness is timely, the theoretical tools (moment‐based ambiguity sets, Stiefel‐manifold subgradients) are adaptations of existing techniques. The methodological novelty is largely in their combination rather than fundamentally new theory.
2. Dimensionality and Scalability: The datasets used have relatively low ambient dimension (≤40). Scalability to high‐dimensional data remains untested, and wall‐clock runtimes or per‐iteration costs versus SDP‐based methods are not reported.
3. Hyperparameter Selection: The choice of ambiguity radius ε and fairness penalty λ is critical yet appears to rely on ad‐hoc tuning. More guidance—such as cross‐validation heuristics or automated selection strategies—would enhance practical applicability.
4. Binary Attribute Limitation: The focus on binary sensitive attributes limits real‐world applicability. While an extension to multiple groups is discussed, no empirical results support that claim.
5. Notational Density and Presentation: Sections 3 and 4 contain dense notation and heavy use of subscripts, which can hinder readability. A schematic diagram or pseudocode box could clarify the algorithmic flow.
6. Baseline Comparisons: The evaluation omits comparison to other robust or adversarial‐fair PCA and unsupervised representation‐learning methods. Including such baselines would strengthen claims of superiority.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Report wall‐clock runtimes, scalability experiments on higher‐dimensional datasets, and sensitivity of convergence to retraction choice and step‐size.
- Provide ablation studies on λ and ε, ideally with guidance on their selection (e.g., cross‐validation).
- Include experiments with more than two protected groups to demonstrate multi‐class fairness.
- Explore alternative ambiguity sets (e.g., KL or φ‐divergences) or discuss their potential impact.
- Improve exposition by adding pseudocode, a flowchart of the algorithm, and consolidating notation.
- Discuss ethical considerations and practical limitations of the moment‐based robustness formulation.

Overall, this work offers a coherent and well‐executed integration of distributional robustness and fairness into PCA, backed by solid theory and encouraging empirical results. The contributions justify a marginal acceptance, contingent on addressing the aforementioned points.

Recommendation:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper aims to improve fairness and distributional robustness in dimensionality reduction techniques. More specifically, it proposes a regularization term to principal compoenent analysis (PCA) such that the expected reconstruction errors for each groups (conditioned on a binary sensitive variable) are similar, and relaxes constraint on the empirical probability distribution by maximizing over a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. This is optimized with subgradient descent on the Stiefel manifold, and numerical experiments comparing the proposed method to previous fair PCA are conducted.
reviews.main_review:
This paper tackles an interesting problem, and is mostly well-written and easy to follow. The problem formulation and re-formulation steps are not trivial.

However, there are places in this paper that needs more explanation and justification. 

- There needs to be a discussion on the assumptions for Theorem 3.2. How restrictive are they? Were they satisfied in the experiments? For example, (i) implies that the regularization parameter is less than 0.5, which will restrict the flexibility shown in Figure 1. 
- The ambiguity set B(P) is a central part of the proposed method. What is the justification for using Wasserstein-type divergence as opposed to others? Also, the second line of equation (5) needs to be explained more carefully.
- In defining $gap_\mu(U)$, is $||U'-U||_F$ the best choice to measure the difference between two orthonormal matrices? One can think of many examples with rotations where $U$ and $U'$ span the same subspace but will have very large frobenius norm difference. A more typical choice in the literature would be $||UU^T - U' U' ^T||$. 
- Is the RHS of Theorem 4.3. necessarily decreasing with $t$? It's not obvious to me why this guarantees convergence.
- Experiments and discussions on the robustness of the proposed method are lacking, compared to the fairness aspect. Are there any comparisons to other robust PCA methods, or simulation experiments?
- In the experiments, how were the sensitive attributes chosen? Were they based on what are considered sensitive attributes in the real world, e.g. race, gender, or were they chosen randomly?
- This paper is lacking a conclusion section. It would have made it stronger if the authors mentioned how they would extend this work to non-binary sensitive attributes.
- On a very high level, it's not *as* obvious why reconstruction error should be balanced compared to, for example, in supervised learning. The toy example in Figure 1 is helpful but I'm wondering if the authors can describe a real-world example where bias in reconstruction error will be harmful - where PCA is the end result rather than a preprocessing step for supervised learning problem.

Other minor problems that can be fixed with careful editing, such as:
- Page 2: "signal process" -> signal processing
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..."
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." sentence is hard to parse.
- Page 9: "In Each dataset, ..." "other features is..." "the the generalization..."
reviews.summary_of_the_review: It's a good paper, but is missing some interpretation of its theoretical results and justifications. It is likely that I will change my recommendation after the discussion period, if the authors provide more details.

reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper aims to improve fairness and distributional robustness in dimensionality reduction techniques. More specifically, it proposes a regularization term to principal compoenent analysis (PCA) such that the expected reconstruction errors for each groups (conditioned on a binary sensitive variable) are similar, and relaxes constraint on the empirical probability distribution by maximizing over a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. This is optimized with subgradient descent on the Stiefel manifold, and numerical experiments comparing the proposed method to previous fair PCA are conducted.

Main Review:
This paper tackles an interesting problem, and is mostly well-written and easy to follow. The problem formulation and re-formulation steps are not trivial.

However, there are places in this paper that needs more explanation and justification. 

- There needs to be a discussion on the assumptions for Theorem 3.2. How restrictive are they? Were they satisfied in the experiments? For example, (i) implies that the regularization parameter is less than 0.5, which will restrict the flexibility shown in Figure 1. 
- The ambiguity set B(P) is a central part of the proposed method. What is the justification for using Wasserstein-type divergence as opposed to others? Also, the second line of equation (5) needs to be explained more carefully.
- In defining $gap_\mu(U)$, is $||U'-U||_F$ the best choice to measure the difference between two orthonormal matrices? One can think of many examples with rotations where $U$ and $U'$ span the same subspace but will have very large frobenius norm difference. A more typical choice in the literature would be $||UU^T - U' U' ^T||$. 
- Is the RHS of Theorem 4.3. necessarily decreasing with $t$? It's not obvious to me why this guarantees convergence.
- Experiments and discussions on the robustness of the proposed method are lacking, compared to the fairness aspect. Are there any comparisons to other robust PCA methods, or simulation experiments?
- In the experiments, how were the sensitive attributes chosen? Were they based on what are considered sensitive attributes in the real world, e.g. race, gender, or were they chosen randomly?
- This paper is lacking a conclusion section. It would have made it stronger if the authors mentioned how they would extend this work to non-binary sensitive attributes.
- On a very high level, it's not *as* obvious why reconstruction error should be balanced compared to, for example, in supervised learning. The toy example in Figure 1 is helpful but I'm wondering if the authors can describe a real-world example where bias in reconstruction error will be harmful - where PCA is the end result rather than a preprocessing step for supervised learning problem.

Other minor problems that can be fixed with careful editing, such as:
- Page 2: "signal process" -> signal processing
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..."
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." sentence is hard to parse.
- Page 9: "In Each dataset, ..." "other features is..." "the the generalization..."

Summary Of The Review:
It's a good paper, but is missing some interpretation of its theoretical results and justifications. It is likely that I will change my recommendation after the discussion period, if the authors provide more details.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper addresses the important and timely problem of incorporating both subgroup fairness and distributional robustness into unsupervised dimensionality reduction. Concretely, the authors propose a regularized PCA formulation that penalizes differences in reconstruction error between two sensitive subgroups and simultaneously hedges against worst‐case distributions within a Wasserstein‐type ambiguity set. Through a careful reformulation, the infinite‐dimensional distributional robust, fair PCA problem is reduced to a finite‐dimensional minimax program over the Stiefel manifold. The paper derives closed‐form expressions for the robustified objective, develops a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm with QR/polar retractions, and establishes sublinear convergence guarantees. Numerical experiments on multiple real‐world datasets demonstrate Pareto improvements in the trade‐off between overall reconstruction error and subgroup error gap relative to prior fair PCA methods.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of the formulation. By integrating distributionally robust optimization with subgroup fairness in an unsupervised setting, the paper advances beyond existing fair PCA work (e.g., Samadi et al., Olfat & Aswani) and contributes to a growing literature on robust fairness.  
2. Rigorous theoretical development. The reformulation of the infinite‐dimensional DRO into a finite Stiefel‐manifold minimax problem is elegant, and the derivation of explicit Riemannian subgradients is technically sound. The proof of sublinear convergence for the nonconvex Riemannian subgradient descent fills an important gap in the analysis of robust fair PCA algorithms.  
3. Empirical validation. Experiments on 15 real‐world datasets show consistent reductions in subgroup error gap while maintaining competitive overall reconstruction error. The method demonstrates improved out‐of‐sample performance under mild distribution shifts, suggesting practical robustness advantages over baseline fair PCA algorithms.  
4. Clarity of writing. Overall, the presentation is mostly clear and accessible, with a logical flow from problem formulation through algorithm design and experiments. The toy example in Figure 1 aptly illustrates the motivation for balancing reconstruction errors, and the connection to robustness is well highlighted.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Assumptions in Theorem 3.2. The paper should discuss the restrictiveness of the eigenvalue and marginal‐probability bounds required for the theoretical results. In particular, the constraint that the regularization parameter λ < 0.5 may limit the flexibility observed in Figure 1. It would strengthen the work to report whether these conditions hold in the real‐dataset experiments and, if not, how violations affect performance.  
2. Justification of the ambiguity set choice. While a moment‐based Wasserstein‐type divergence is a natural choice, the authors should motivate this selection over alternative discrepancy measures (e.g., KL or φ‐divergences). Moreover, the second line of Equation (5) deserves a more careful derivation or intuitive explanation.  
3. Distance metric for subspaces. In defining gap_\mu(U), the Frobenius‐norm difference ||U′−U||_F may be a suboptimal measure, since two projections spanning the same subspace can differ by an arbitrary rotation yet incur a large Frobenius distance. A canonical alternative is ||UU^T − U′U′^T||_F, which directly measures subspace deviation. The authors should comment on this choice or evaluate its impact.  
4. Convergence guarantee clarity. The statement that the right‐hand side of Theorem 4.3 is necessarily decreasing in t is not immediately obvious. A brief argument or reference to known Riemannian subgradient results would help justify the monotonicity and thus the convergence claim.  
5. Hyperparameter selection and sensitivity. Both the ambiguity radii εₐ and the fairness penalty λ critically influence outcomes, yet the paper offers little guidance on tuning. Incorporating automatic selection heuristics (e.g., cross‐validation) or presenting ablation studies over a grid of ε and λ values would greatly improve practical applicability.  
6. Computational complexity and scalability. The runtime cost of the Riemannian subgradient method—especially with multiple random restarts—is not quantified. Wall‐clock time comparisons against SDP‐based fair PCA or other robust PCA methods, as well as scalability tests on higher dimensions, would better demonstrate efficiency.  
7. Scope of fairness attributes. The current formulation and experiments restrict to binary sensitive attributes. Given that real‐world applications often involve multiple protected groups, an empirical or at least conceptual extension to m>2 categories would broaden the paper’s impact.  
8. Robustness‐focused evaluations. Although robustness is part of the title, most experiments emphasize fairness. It would be valuable to compare against established robust PCA techniques or conduct simulation studies with synthetic distribution shifts to isolate robustness benefits.  
9. Experimental details. Clarify how sensitive attributes were chosen in each dataset—whether they correspond to real‐world categories (e.g., race, gender) or are synthetically assigned. Also ensure that hyperparameter tuning does not inadvertently leak test‐set information.  
10. Paper structure and minor edits. The manuscript lacks a dedicated conclusion section summarizing contributions and future directions (e.g., nonbinary extension). Additionally, several typos and phrasing issues remain (e.g., “signal process”→“signal processing,” “By the definition the ambiguity…,” and grammatical edits on pages 7 and 9).

Decision  
Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold  

This work makes a substantive contribution by unifying distributional robustness and subgroup fairness in PCA, with solid theoretical reformulations and promising empirical results. Addressing the points above—particularly around theoretical assumptions, hyperparameter sensitivity, computational scalability, and broader fairness/generalization experiments—will further strengthen the manuscript.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper presents an optimization based formulation of fair principal components analysis problem with a particular focus on the robustness of the optimal models. In the case when the desired robustness is specified by Gaussian distributions, the authors show how to adapt a manifold subgradient descent to solve a non-convex reformulation of the proposed fair pca formulation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength: + Robustness criterion for linear transformation of fairness can be formulated as a single optimization problem as long as the ambiguity set is defined using a single normal distribution.
+ A smooth reformulation is presented which can be solved using standard riemannian gradient descent algorithm.

Weakness: - Generalization to multiclass sensitive attribute, and other fairness metrics is missing.
- Figure 1 illustrating different approaches is nice, although it fails to capture the distributional robustness aspect of the formulation presented in the paper.
- It is hard to follow the paper since the notations are not standard.
- Experimental results are preliminary and conclusions are not clear.

While the approach here may not be directly extendable to other metrics, the paper completely misses on discussing when the chosen formulation is appropriate for practitioners.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper presents an optimization based formulation of fair principal components analysis problem with a particular focus on the robustness of the optimal models. In the case when the desired robustness is specified by Gaussian distributions, the authors show how to adapt a manifold subgradient descent to solve a non-convex reformulation of the proposed fair pca formulation.

Main Review:
Strength: + Robustness criterion for linear transformation of fairness can be formulated as a single optimization problem as long as the ambiguity set is defined using a single normal distribution.
+ A smooth reformulation is presented which can be solved using standard riemannian gradient descent algorithm.

Weakness: - Generalization to multiclass sensitive attribute, and other fairness metrics is missing.
- Figure 1 illustrating different approaches is nice, although it fails to capture the distributional robustness aspect of the formulation presented in the paper.
- It is hard to follow the paper since the notations are not standard.
- Experimental results are preliminary and conclusions are not clear.

This is important in the context of the paper's contribution because we know that standard SDP solvers do not scale to settings with big or high dimensional data. reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the important problem of integrating distributional robustness with subgroup fairness in unsupervised dimensionality reduction, formulating a distributionally robust fair PCA via a min–max program over projection matrices on the Stiefel manifold. By defining moment‐based ambiguity sets (Gaussian) for each sensitive subgroup, the authors convert the infinite‐dimensional DRO into a finite‐dimensional nonconvex problem. They derive closed‐form expressions for the robustified objective, propose a Riemannian subgradient descent (with QR‐ or polar‐retractions) on the Stiefel manifold, and establish sublinear convergence guarantees. Initial experiments on binary‐attribute datasets suggest improvements in the trade‐off between overall reconstruction error and subgroup error gap compared to FairPCA and CFPCA baselines.

Strengths  
1. Novel formulation: The paper elegantly unifies robustness against distributional shifts with fairness constraints in an unsupervised setting, extending classical PCA to account for worst‐case subgroup disparities.  
2. Finite reformulation: The reduction of an infinite‐dimensional moment‐based DRO to a tractable minimax problem on the Stiefel manifold is technically interesting, leveraging closed‐form worst‐case functions.  
3. Riemannian optimization: Adapting manifold subgradient (and gradient) descent with explicit retraction schemes is well motivated, and the convergence analysis for this nonconvex setting is a valuable theoretical contribution.  
4. Empirical promise: On several real‐world, binary‐attribute datasets, the proposed method yields Pareto improvements in reconstructed variance versus subgroup error gap, hinting at practical viability.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited fairness scope: The method and experiments focus exclusively on binary sensitive attributes. As noted in both the main and alternative reviews, generalization to multi‐class or continuous sensitive features is not developed nor empirically tested, yet is critical for real‐world deployment.  
2. Notational density: Throughout Sections 3–4, nonstandard notation (e.g., undefined \hat pₐ, Q(dx×da)) and terse definitions hinder readability. A consistent schematic or glossary would reduce cognitive load for readers encountering the Stiefel‐manifold terminology for the first time.  
3. Experimental preliminarity and scope: The empirical study omits runtime or wall‐clock comparisons to SDP‐based fair PCA and alternative robust or adversarial representation‐learning baselines. Moreover, sensitivity analyses over the key hyperparameters (ambiguity radii εₐ, fairness penalty λ, step‐size, and retraction choice) are lacking.  
4. Hyperparameter selection: There is no systematic guidance for choosing εₐ and λ. As raised in the alternative reviews, automatic strategies (e.g., cross‐validation heuristics) or ablation studies are needed to demonstrate robust performance and avoid optimistic tuning on test splits.  
5. Assumption validation: The convergence proof relies on eigenvalue and marginal‐probability bounds (Theorem 3.2), but empirical verification of these conditions is absent. It remains unclear how often these assumptions hold in practice, especially on high‐dimensional datasets.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Extend to multi‐class sensitive attributes: Even a sketch or preliminary experiment on three or more groups would greatly strengthen the paper’s practical relevance. Discuss how the number of worst‐case terms grows and whether the algorithmic complexity remains manageable.  
• Clarify notation and presentation: Introduce a notation table or a running example to make the manifold formulation more accessible. Include pseudocode summarizing the Riemannian subgradient descent, specifying retraction and step‐size rules.  
• Hyperparameter and sensitivity studies: Provide ablations on εₐ and λ across multiple datasets, report wall‐clock runtime and per‐iteration costs relative to FairPCA/CFPCA, and analyze sensitivity to retraction scheme (QR vs. polar) and initialization.  
• Broader baseline comparisons: Compare against additional robustness‐ or adversarial‐fairness methods (e.g., adversarially trained embeddings) to contextualize the gains.  
• Empirical validation of assumptions: Test key assumptions from the convergence theorem (e.g., eigenvalue gaps, marginal‐probability bounds) on real datasets, and discuss any violations or required safeguards.

Decision  
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold.  
While the paper offers a promising unification of distributional robustness and fairness in PCA with solid theoretical underpinnings, its current form falls short in readability, empirical scope, and practical guidance. Addressing the above concerns would be necessary to justify acceptance.

title: Meta Learning Low Rank Covariance Factors for Energy Based Deterministic Uncertainty

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The authors explore the uncertainty calibration problem in meta-learning few-shot classification, which is a relatively new topic that was seldom discussed before. 
To solve the challenges that the few samples in few-shot learning are usually insufficient for providing reliable estimation to the covariance matrices, the authors introduce meta-learning covariance matrices parametrized by a set encoder (implemented as set-transformer). And the authors further introduce scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution for improving the uncertainty calibration of the softmax classification layer.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
The overall presentation of this paper is both clear and straightforward.

The figures and equations precisely facilitate the reading. 

No noticeable typos were spotted. 

The topic that the authors selected is important and largely overlooked by the research of meta-learning and few-shot learning. The effectiveness of the proposed method is reflected by the quantitative comparisons. 

Weakness:
My major concern to this paper mainly arises from the use of an additional meta-learned set encoder to predict the task-specific covariance. 
It has been shown in previous work that some meta-learners have natural advantages in quantifying uncertainty. 

For example, relying on kernel methods, Gaussian processes based meta-learners [1,2] have demonstrated strong uncertainty calibration without leveraging any auxiliary components. 

Additionally, I might be wrong, but I'm curious that if this class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information so that an additional set encoder can be removed. 
Please refer to Eq.6 to Eq. 9 of [3] for an example. Although the motivations are different, I believe there is some shared wisdom. 

[1] Deep kernel transfer in gaussian processes for few-shot learning, NeurIPS 2020

[2] Bayesian Few-Shot Classiﬁcation with One-vs-Each Pólya-Gamma Augmented Gaussian Processes, ICLR 2021

[3] Amortized Bayesian Prototype Meta-learning: A New Probabilistic Meta-learning Approach to Few-shot Image Classification, AISTAT 2021
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall this paper attacks a very important problem of meta learning and few-shot learning. And considering the quantitative results, the proposed method does achieve better uncertainty calibration. 
I expect further discussion on justifying the necessity of adopting an additional set encoder to achieve this capability.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the important and under-explored problem of uncertainty calibration in meta-learning for few-shot classification. The authors observe that empirical covariance estimation in the feature space is unreliable when support sets are tiny, and propose to meta-learn class-wise covariance factors (diagonal or diagonal-plus-low-rank) via an attentive set encoder (implemented with a Set Transformer). To further mitigate softmax overconfidence resulting from shift invariance, they introduce a scaled-energy logit-normal softmax, which inflates variance in proportion to the minimum energy per class. Bi-Lipschitz regularization through spectral normalization ensures the feature extractor remains expressive and well-conditioned. Experiments on synthetic tasks, Omniglot/Omniglot-C, MiniImageNet/MiniImageNet-C, and other few-shot benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in expected calibration error (ECE) and out-of-distribution detection, while maintaining competitive classification accuracy.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation  
   – The manuscript is well-written, with clear motivation, precise equations, and helpful figures. No notable typos were found.  
   – The use of matrix determinant lemma and Sherman-Morrison inversion is well explained, making the inference procedure transparent and efficient.  

2. Novelty and Technical Soundness  
   – Meta-learning low-rank covariance factors per class in few-shot tasks is an original contribution that directly addresses the limitations of empirical covariance under data scarcity.  
   – Employing a Set Transformer to encode covariance structure captures pairwise interactions in the support set and is more expressive than purely diagonal estimators.  
   – The scaled-energy logit-normal softmax is a principled mechanism to adjust predictive variance based on model confidence, tackling softmax shift-invariance in a simple yet effective way.  

3. Empirical Validation  
   – Extensive experiments on both toy and real-world few-shot benchmarks show marked ECE and negative log-likelihood gains under distributional shift, without sacrificing in-distribution accuracy.  
   – Ablations on covariance rank and comparisons between diagonal vs. low-rank variants convincingly illustrate the utility of the proposed factors.  
   – The paper evaluates both in-distribution calibration and out-of-distribution detection, demonstrating robustness across multiple corruption intensities and datasets.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Justification for the Meta-Learned Set Encoder  
   – While the additional set encoder yields richer covariance estimates, prior meta-learners based on Gaussian processes (e.g., Deep Kernel Transfer, NeurIPS 2020; Bayesian Few-Shot Classification, ICLR 2021) naturally quantify uncertainty without auxiliary modules. The authors should more directly compare to or discuss these approaches to clarify the unique benefits of their encoder.  
   – It may be possible to aggregate sample-wise statistics (see Eq.6–9 in Amortized Bayesian Prototype Meta-learning, AISTATS 2021) to obtain class covariances without a separate Set Transformer. Discussion or small experiments in this direction would strengthen the justification.  

2. Comparison to Recent Baselines  
   – Important recent uncertainty-aware meta-learning methods, including kernel-based and Bayesian posterior approximations, are only mentioned briefly. A quantitative comparison (e.g., ECE and accuracy) would better position this work relative to the state of the art.  

3. Temperature Tuning Procedure  
   – The scaled‐energy temperature scheduling is described at a high level, but its sensitivity to the initial temperature, increment step, and stopping criterion is unclear. A step-by-step pseudocode or algorithm box would aid reproducibility. Additionally, include an ablation on how calibration performance varies with the tuning schedule.  

4. Rank Selection and Hyperparameter Sensitivity  
   – The choice of low-rank dimension r is treated as a fixed hyperparameter. More extensive ablations over r (e.g., r ∈ {0,1,2,5}) and discussion of the trade-off between calibration improvement and computational overhead would guide practitioners.  
   – Similarly, report sensitivity to the set encoder’s architecture (number of blocks, attention heads) and support set size or class imbalance scenarios.  

5. Scalability and Computational Cost  
   – Although low-rank inversion is more efficient than full covariance, runtime and memory profiling for higher ranks and deeper backbones (e.g., ResNet) are not provided. Tables summarizing inference latency and GPU memory usage would clarify practical scalability.  

6. Broader OOD Evaluation  
   – The paper focuses on few-shot corruption benchmarks, but standard OOD splits (e.g., CIFAR-10 vs. SVHN) and detection metrics such as AUROC/AUPR would further demonstrate generalization beyond small-scale tasks.  
   – Consider reporting additional metrics (e.g., FPR at 95% TPR) to complement ECE and highlight separation between in- and out-of-distribution samples.  

7. Theoretical Insight  
   – A formal analysis or intuitive bound on why a set encoder can generalize task-specific covariance structure would strengthen the theoretical foundation. At minimum, a discussion of potential failure modes when class distributions are highly anisotropic or multimodal would be valuable.

Overall Assessment  
This paper presents a clear, technically sound, and empirically strong contribution to uncertainty estimation in few-shot meta-learning. The hybrid use of a Set Transformer for covariance prediction and a scaled‐energy softmax addresses overconfidence in a principled manner, yielding consistent calibration benefits under distributional shift. While several aspects—such as comparisons to Gaussian process meta-learners, hyperparameter sensitivity, temperature tuning clarity, and scalability profiling—require deeper analysis, these are addressable and do not detract from the core novelty and empirical gains.  

Recommendation  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors proposed methodologies to estimate the covariance matrix of embedded features under the meta-learning setting. The authors first showed that the empirical covariance matrix is inappropriate in this setting and that due to the shift invariant property of softmax function, ProtoNet could produce overly confident predictions. 

The authors instead propose to learn the covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component. The authors used scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to make the methodology more performant. 

Numerical studies are performed to evaluate the calibration performance as well as the eigenvalue distribution of several methods.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is well written and easy to follow. Some specific comments / suggestions:
1. It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
2. What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda_c to be zero?
3. Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
4. In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is well-written with clear motivations and easy to follow presentations of the methodology. The proposal is relatively novel, and could be a meaningful contribution to the meta-learning community.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

The authors instead propose to learn the covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component. The authors used scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to make the methodology more performant.
