reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This submission focus on improving the performance of uncertainty quantification and OOD detection using bi-Lipschitz regularized neural networks. There are two involved challenges here: (i) we are required to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrix of the latent features. This estimation is challenging under low sample size. (ii) generative classifiers using softmax activation function suffer from shift invariance, and thus may become over-confident for OOD samples.

To resolve these issues, the paper focus on two innovations:

(i) a meta-learning model to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrices,
(ii) a modification of the inference procedure with the energy function to detect OOD samples.

The paper concludes with numerical results showing the performance of the proposed solution approach.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is empirical: the paper is well-motivated, and the paper adapts existing, popular techniques to engineer a solution to the standing problem (using quadratic discriminant analysis, modeling the covariance matrix as a sum of a diagonal matrix and a low-rank psd matrix, etc.).

On the downside, there are several theoretical questions that are left unanswered:
1. Why is the Set Transformer a good choice? On which ground can we justify that the Set Transformer can generate sensible values of Lambda and Phi to construct the covariance matrices?
2. There are a lot of methods from statistics to estimate covariance matrices from low sample size dataset (linear/nonlinear shrinkage, sparse, etc.). Why can't they be used in this application? Is it possible to show that the Set Transformer is better than these traditional approach (for any possible criteria)?
3. In page 8, why should the eigenvalue distribution be diversified? It is well known that the eigenvalue can be sensitive to small perturbations of the off-diagonal entries of the matrix. Thus, what we observed (diversity of the eigenvalues) can be just an artifact of the numerical errors from the Set transformer network. Diverse eigenvalues also imply that the condition number is large, which leads to higher numerical errors to the log-determinant term. Thus, I am not fully convinced that a diverse set of eigenvalues is of any advantage.


Minor:
- What is f(E) and min(E) after equation (10)?
- The abstract and intro focus heavily on bi-Lipschitz regularized NN, however, the exposition in section 3 (except for Section 3.4 which is 10 lines long) relies minimally on this architecture. This emphasis may be misleading in the first read.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In my opinion, the paper is a good engineering attempt to solve a specific problem. It lacks, unfortunately, concrete scientific contributions.


======== Post-rebuttal ========
The authors have provided further empirical results to justify the use of the ST to estimate the covariance matrix. I have raised my evaluation from 3 to 5.

Summary Of The Paper:
This submission focus on improving the performance of uncertainty quantification and OOD detection using bi-Lipschitz regularized neural networks. There are two involved challenges here: (i) we are required to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrix of the latent features. This estimation is challenging under low sample size. (ii) generative classifiers using softmax activation function suffer from shift invariance, and thus may become over-confident for OOD samples.

To resolve these issues, the paper focus on two innovations:

(i) a meta-learning model to estimate the class-conditional covariance matrices,
(ii) a modification of the inference procedure with the energy function to detect OOD samples.

The paper concludes with numerical results showing the performance of the proposed solution approach.

Main Review:
This submission is empirical: the paper is well-motivated, and the paper adapts existing, popular techniques to engineer a solution to the standing problem (using quadratic discriminant analysis, modeling the covariance matrix as a sum of a diagonal matrix and a low-rank psd matrix, etc.).

On the downside, there are several theoretical questions that are left unanswered:
1. Why is the Set Transformer a good choice? On which ground can we justify that the Set Transformer can generate sensible values of Lambda and Phi to construct the covariance matrices?
2. There are a lot of methods from statistics to estimate covariance matrices from low sample size dataset (linear/nonlinear shrinkage, sparse, etc.). Why can't they be used in this application? Is it possible to show that the Set Transformer is better than these traditional approach (for any possible criteria)?
3. In page 8, why should the eigenvalue distribution be diversified? It is well known that the eigenvalue can be sensitive to small perturbations of the off-diagonal entries of the matrix. Thus, what we observed (diversity of the eigenvalues) can be just an artifact of the numerical errors from the Set transformer network. Diverse eigenvalues also imply that the condition number is large, which leads to higher numerical errors to the log-determinant term. Thus, I am not fully convinced that a diverse set of eigenvalues is of any advantage.


Minor:
- What is f(E) and min(E) after equation (10)?
- The abstract and intro focus heavily on bi-Lipschitz regularized NN, however, the exposition in section 3 (except for Section 3.4 which is 10 lines long) relies minimally on this architecture. This emphasis may be misleading in the first read.

Summary Of The Review:
In my opinion, the paper is a good engineering attempt to solve a specific problem. It lacks, unfortunately, concrete scientific contributions.


======== Post-rebuttal ========
The authors have provided further empirical results to justify the use of the ST to estimate the covariance matrix. I have raised my evaluation from 3 to 5.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper tackles uncertainty quantification and out‐of‐distribution (OOD) detection in few‐shot classification by meta‐learning class‐conditional covariance matrices and refining inference with an energy‐based correction. Specifically, the authors (i) train an attentive set encoder (Set Transformer) to predict either diagonal or diagonal-plus-low-rank factors for each class covariance in the embedding space, and (ii) introduce a scaled energy logit-normal softmax to mitigate softmax’s shift-invariance overconfidence. Bi-Lipschitz regularization via spectral normalization ensures well-behaved feature representations. Empirical evaluations on synthetic tasks, Omniglot, MiniImageNet (and their corrupted variants) demonstrate improved expected calibration error (ECE) and competitive classification accuracy, particularly under distribution shift.

Strengths  
1. Clear Motivation and Novelty  
   - The paper identifies a genuine limitation of empirical covariance estimation in few-shot settings and proposes meta-learning low-rank covariance factors, an idea that has not been widely explored in the meta-learning literature.  
   - Integrating an energy-based inference procedure addresses a well-known softmax overconfidence issue under OOD shifts, improving calibration without sacrificing accuracy.  

2. Technical Soundness and Efficient Implementation  
   - The use of the Set Transformer to encode support-set statistics, combined with Sherman–Morrison inversion and the matrix determinant lemma, yields a computationally efficient inference that scales better than naive covariance estimation.  
   - Bi-Lipschitz regularization via spectral normalization is a principled choice to preserve feature distances and prevent degenerate embeddings.  

3. Empirical Evidence  
   - Across toy experiments and standard few-shot benchmarks (including corruption variants), the proposed method consistently reduces ECE and negative log‐likelihood compared to ProtoNet, MAML/Reptile, SNGP, and DDU baselines.  
   - Ablations on covariance rank and analyses of eigenvalue distributions provide insight into how low-rank factors enhance expressiveness over purely diagonal models.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Theoretical Justification for Set Transformer  
   - It remains unclear why a Set Transformer is the “right” inductive bias for predicting covariance factors. The paper would benefit from either theoretical insight or comparisons to classical shrinkage and sparsity‐based covariance estimators (e.g., linear/nonlinear shrinkage, graphical lasso) to show when and why the Set Transformer yields superior covariance estimates.  
   - Relatedly, the observed diversity in eigenvalue spectra might be an artifact of numerical perturbations rather than a meaningful modeling choice; a deeper investigation or controlled experiment is needed to substantiate the claimed benefits of eigenvalue diversification.

2. Energy‐Scaling and Temperature Tuning  
   - The scaled energy logit‐normal softmax is introduced heuristically, and the tuning procedure (incremental temperature search until a calibration criterion is met) appears ad hoc. A clearer derivation, pseudocode, or a more principled alternative (e.g., learned mapping) would improve reproducibility and generalization to new datasets.  
   - Sensitivity analyses to initial temperature, tuning schedule, and the choice of scaling function h(E) should be reported to demonstrate robustness under varying shifts.

3. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Rank Selection  
   - While low-rank variants (r=1,2) are evaluated, the paper lacks guidance on selecting the rank hyperparameter in practice. Additional ablations over a wider range of ranks, with trade-off curves of calibration improvement versus computational/memory overhead, would aid users in balancing performance and efficiency.  
   - An explicit runtime and memory profiling—especially on higher‐dimensional backbones (e.g., ResNet variants) and larger tasks—would clarify the practical scalability of the approach.

4. Baselines and OOD Evaluation  
   - Important recent meta-learning uncertainty approaches (e.g., deep kernel transfer methods, normalizing flows) are only briefly mentioned. Including direct quantitative comparisons would strengthen claims of novelty and empirical superiority.  
   - OOD experiments are confined to corrupted variants of the same dataset family. Evaluations against truly out‐of‐domain datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10 vs. SVHN) and additional metrics such as AUROC/AUPR for OOD detection would paint a more comprehensive picture of the method’s robustness.

5. Clarity and Notation  
   - Notational inconsistencies (e.g., definitions of f(E) and min(E) after Eq. (10)) and sparse exposition in key sections (e.g., bi-Lipschitz regularization coverage limited to Section 3.4) can confuse readers. A more thorough explanation of each component, along with consistent notation and well‐labeled figures/tables, will improve readability.  

Minor Comments  
- The abstract and introduction emphasize bi-Lipschitz regularized networks, yet Sections 3.1–3.3 focus primarily on covariance encoding and energy‐based inference. Consider rebalancing the narrative to reflect the actual content proportion.  
- Please append pseudocode for the temperature tuning loop and clarify the architecture details of the Set Transformer (number of blocks, heads, embedding size).

Conclusion and Rating  
Overall, this work presents a compelling engineering solution with clear empirical gains in calibration and OOD detection for few‐shot tasks. However, significant gaps remain in theoretical justification, hyperparameter analysis, baseline coverage, and broader OOD evaluation. These issues must be addressed to substantiate the claimed scientific contributions. In its current form, I recommend a score of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

title: ZeroFL: Efficient On-Device Training for  Federated Learning with Local Sparsity

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach each node uses only the top-K weights for computations in the forward and backward pass of training (and only top-K activations for computations in the backward pass). The authors empirically show that adding sparsity in this way, while speeding up local training, significantly degrades the accuracy when directly used in federated learning. To mitigate this issue the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation since their empirical study suggests that the accuracy drop may be due to dilution of weights that are active in only a few nodes. Experiments on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model show some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed schemes.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

1. The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.
reviews.correctness: 2: Several of the paper’s claims are incorrect or not well-supported.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the problem of adding sparsity to local training in federated learning. Under the approach each node uses only the top-K weights for computations in the forward and backward pass of training (and only top-K activations for computations in the backward pass). The authors empirically show that adding sparsity in this way, while speeding up local training, significantly degrades the accuracy when directly used in federated learning. To mitigate this issue the authors propose to combine sparse training with sparse aggregation since their empirical study suggests that the accuracy drop may be due to dilution of weights that are active in only a few nodes. Experiments on CIFAR10 with non-IID splits using ResNet-18 as the model show some gain in accuracy along with a drop in communication cost when using the proposed schemes.

Main Review:
Strengths:

1. The authors introduce a new problem of adding sparse local training to federated learning. The problem appears well motivated (sparse training can lead to significant power savings at the edge) and challenging (directly adding sparse training to conventional aggregation methods like FedAvg leads to significant accuracy drops).

2. The proposed solutions are good initial approaches backed by the empirical study and do appear to lead to some improvement in accuracy over the naive approach.

Weaknesses:

1. For a paper that relies entirely on empirical evidence for its claims, the empirical study appears a bit limited as it considers only a single dataset (CIFAR10) and a single model architecture (ResNet18). Without seeing results for some other image/text datasets and other model architectures (MLP/LSTM) it is difficult to completely believe the claims especially since the proposed solutions rely heavily on the claims that the positions of the top-K weights do not vary significantly across models and across rounds.

2. The experimental results are also a bit limited. In particular:

a) It is misleading to highlight both max accuracy and max comms savings values since the two correspond to different settings and thus cannot be achieved simultaneously. I would suggest removing the bold font for the max comms savings model and just reporting the comms savings for the different mask ratios. An interesting future direction would be to think of a metric that can combine the two since it is currently unclear if the communication saving (and local computation cost reduction) is significant enough to compensate for the drop in accuracy w.r.t conventional FL (black line in Fig. 1)

b) Since the authors acknowledge that accuracy is expected to first increase and then decrease on increasing mask ratio, it would be worth including results for all mask ratios in the appendix at least to see the trend clearly.

c) The results with i.i.d data partitioning are not presented. Since from Fig 1 it seems like accuracy drops even for the i.i.d case, it is important to show if the proposed schemes alleviate the issue in that case as well.

d) Lastly, following point 1, in my opinion all experiments should be performed with some other datasets and model architectures before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

3. (Minor) There doesn't seem to be any real difference between Top-K-Weights and the Diff on Top-K-weights in that the weight updates will be exactly the same in both cases as far as I can tell. If that is so then there are essentially 2 (and not 3) new schemes being proposed.

Summary Of The Review:
The authors introduce an interesting and important problem but given that the paper relies purely on empirical evidence for its claims the current evaluation is quite limited and not enough for acceptance. I find the claims fairly intuitive, however, and so would be willing to increase my score if more empirical evidence (in line with my comments above) is presented to back them.

Comments after rebutal : I appreciate the added experiments which certainly demonstrate that the proposed schemes are able to improve performance across different datasets. I have increased my score to reflect the same.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the problem of incorporating sparsity into local training within federated learning (FL) to reduce on-device computation and communication costs. Each client applies unstructured sparse training—retaining only its top-K weights (and top-K activations during backpropagation)—and transmits only these sparse updates to the server. The authors empirically observe that naive sparse training degrades global accuracy in non-IID settings, likely due to dilution of rare but important weight updates across heterogeneous clients. To address this, they propose combining sparse local training with sparse aggregation via three masking strategies (Top-K-Weights, Diff on Top-K-Weights, and Top-K-Weights Diff). Experiments on CIFAR-10 (and, after rebuttal, on FEMNIST and SpeechCommands) under IID and non-IID partitions using ResNet-18 (and a small CNN for FEMNIST) demonstrate modest accuracy gains (up to +2.3%) and substantial uplink communication savings (1.6×–7.4×) at 90–95% sparsity.

Strengths  
1. Novel Problem Formulation: The paper is among the first to examine training-time sparsity in federated settings, a compelling and timely direction given the resource constraints of edge devices.  
2. Insightful Empirical Analysis: The finding that clients’ top-K weight positions overlap significantly and remain stable across rounds motivates the ZeroFL framework and its masking strategies.  
3. Simple, Clear Algorithms: The three proposed sparsification schemes are straightforward to implement and integrate seamlessly with standard FL workflows like FedAvg and FedAdam.  
4. Multi-Dataset Evaluation: Beyond the initial CIFAR-10 experiments, the authors include results on FEMNIST and SpeechCommands, as well as both IID and non-IID splits, improving the empirical support for their claims.  
5. Communication vs. Accuracy Trade-off: Demonstrating up to 7.4× uplink savings with only minor accuracy degradation (and even improvements over sparse baselines) highlights the practical potential of the approach.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Model and Task Diversity: Although the extended experiments cover three benchmarks, all architectures are small to medium-scale CNNs. It remains unclear whether ZeroFL generalizes to other modalities (e.g., text via LSTMs/transformers) or larger models.  
2. Absence of Real-Device Benchmarks: The paper reports FLOPs and upload size reductions but lacks wall-clock latency or energy consumption measurements on representative mobile/IoT hardware. Given the reliance on unstructured sparsity, readers need confirmation that the theoretical speedups translate to real speedups.  
3. Mask-Ratio Sensitivity and Metric Ambiguity: The choice of mask ratio rmask lacks theoretical guidance, and only a few discrete values are evaluated. Moreover, reporting max accuracy and max communication savings for different configurations can be misleading—these trade-offs should be unified under a single composite metric or curve.  
4. Downlink and Server-Side Costs Omitted: The focus is on uplink compression, but the impact on downlink communication and server-side aggregation overhead is not analyzed.  
5. Presentation and Reproducibility Issues: Some pseudocode contains inconsistent notation (e.g., dt vs. wt), figures suffer from low resolution or unlabeled axes, and the communication-savings formula in the text does not always match the tables. Details on code availability and hyperparameter settings (e.g., learning-rate schedules) are sparse.

Detailed Suggestions  
• Expand Model/Task Coverage: Include at least one non-CNN architecture (e.g., an MLP or a small transformer) to strengthen claims of generality.  
• Real-Device Profiling: Provide latency and energy benchmarks on a commodity mobile CPU/GPU with sparse kernels to validate practical gains.  
• Ablation on Mask Ratio: Present a fine-grained sweep of mask ratios in the appendix, along with a discussion or guideline on selecting rmask for different resource budgets.  
• Unified Performance Metric: Rather than separate “max accuracy” and “max comms savings,” report a Pareto front or an aggregate utility score that balances accuracy and communication/computation cost.  
• Include Downlink Analysis: Discuss how sparse updates affect server broadcast size and whether similar masking could be applied on the downlink.  
• Improve Clarity and Reproducibility:  
  – Revise pseudocode with consistent variable names and indexing.  
  – Enhance figure quality, add explicit legends and axis labels, and ensure equation numbering aligns with references in the text.  
  – Release code and detailed experiment configurations to facilitate replication.

Decision  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold  
While the paper presents a novel and well-motivated framework for sparse training in federated learning, its empirical scope and practical validation need further strengthening. I recommend acceptance contingent on addressing the above weaknesses—particularly the inclusion of real-device benchmarks, deeper mask-ratio analysis, and improved presentation—to fully realize the impact of this promising work.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method (SWAT) and analyzes its limitations. Then, the method proposes three strategies to improve upon SWAT. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 dataset and show some improvements compared to SWAT.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has following strengths:
1.	Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important.
2.	The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT.
3.	Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting.

The paper has following weaknesses:
1.	The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses).
2.	One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Even though the reviewer is quite happy about the problem addressed in this paper, it lacks a solid experimental section. I would be willing to increase the score if the authors can present a more comprehensive results section.

Post rebuttal:
I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
The paper tackles the important problem of accelerating on-device training in federated learning by adapting sparse training methods. Starting from the off-the-shelf SWAT algorithm, the authors identify that naively applying centralized sparse training in FL dilutes critical weight updates under data heterogeneity. To address these limitations, they propose three local sparsification strategies—Top-K-Weights, Diff on Top-K-Weights, and Top-K-Weights Diff—that selectively prune or compress client updates before aggregation. Experiments on CIFAR-10 demonstrate modest accuracy improvements over SWAT at high sparsity (90–95%) and substantial uplink communication savings.

Strengths  
1. Problem Significance  
   • The focus on reducing on-device training cost, rather than just communication compression or inference acceleration, is timely and important for practical FL deployments.  
2. Insightful Analysis  
   • The paper provides a novel empirical study of sparse training dynamics in federated settings, showing that clients’ top-K weight patterns overlap significantly and remain stable across rounds. This insight clearly motivates the proposed local sparsification strategies.  
3. Clear Algorithmic Contributions  
   • The three sparsification schemes are well motivated, algorithmically transparent, and integrate seamlessly with standard FL workflows (FedAvg and FedAdam).  
4. Demonstrated Communication Savings  
   • At 90–95% sparsity, the methods recover or even improve global accuracy (by up to +2.3% on CIFAR-10) while reducing uplink payloads by 1.6×–7.4× compared to the SWAT-based baseline.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Experimental Scope  
   • All reported results are on CIFAR-10, whereas standard federated benchmarks (e.g., LEAF, FEMNIST, SpeechCommands) and diverse model architectures (transformers, larger CNNs) are missing. Reviewing works such as FedProx and FedMAX would help guide a more comprehensive evaluation suite.  
2. Lack of Real-Device Benchmarks  
   • Claims of on-device acceleration are unverified in the absence of wall-clock speedup, latency, or energy measurements on representative mobile or IoT hardware. Unstructured sparsity may not translate to practical runtime gains without specialized kernels.  
3. Hyperparameter and Ablation Analysis  
   • The selection and sensitivity of the mask ratio parameter (rmask) is only briefly explored with coarse values. A more systematic ablation—including dynamic or fine-grained schedules—would better support practitioners.  
4. Comparisons to Alternative FL Methods  
   • The manuscript omits comparisons to non-sparse, fast-converging FL optimizers (e.g., FedProx, FedAdam variants, momentum-based methods) and to other model-compression approaches (structured pruning, quantization). Such baselines are needed to contextualize the trade-offs between sparsity and convergence.  
5. Presentation and Reproducibility Concerns  
   • Notational inconsistencies in the pseudocode (e.g., variable superscripts, dt vs. wt), low-resolution or unlabeled figures, and an unclear communication-cost formula reduce clarity. The absence of code release or reproducibility details further hampers follow-up efforts.  
6. Downlink and Server-Side Costs  
   • While uplink savings are emphasized, the paper does not discuss the impact on downlink communication or any additional server-side aggregation overhead introduced by sparse updates.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Extend the empirical evaluation to additional FL benchmarks (LEAF suite, FEMNIST, SpeechCommands) and model types to validate generality.  
• Include real-device profiling (latency, energy) on commodity hardware to substantiate on-device acceleration claims.  
• Provide systematic ablations on mask-ratio selection and local epoch counts, and analyze sensitivity to data heterogeneity and client compute constraints.  
• Compare against non-sparse convergence accelerators (FedProx, FedMAX, FedAdam) and structured compression techniques to clarify when sparsity is most beneficial.  
• Refine pseudocode notation, enhance figure clarity (high-resolution plots, labeled axes, consistent legends), and correct any mismatches between formulas and table entries.  
• Discuss downlink communication costs, server-side handling of sparse updates, and include or reference a code repository for reproducibility.

Decision  
Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
This paper addresses a salient challenge in federated learning and contributes valuable empirical insights and simple yet effective sparsification strategies. However, the limited experimental scope, lack of real-device benchmarks, and presentation issues temper its impact. With a more comprehensive evaluation and clearer exposition, the work would make a stronger case for practical deployment. Given its solid foundation and the promise of the ideas, I recommend marginal acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper investigates sparse training to accelerate on-device federated learning. It begins by taking an off-the-shelf sparse training method (SWAT) and analyzes its limitations. Then, the method proposes three strategies to improve upon SWAT. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10 dataset and show some improvements compared to SWAT.

Main Review:
The paper has following strengths:
1.	Indeed, for more realistic deployment and to move federated learning towards practical systems, it is important to accelerate the on-device training (and not just the inference which is mainly the current practice). So, the problem the authors have considered is important.
2.	The proposed strategies clearly indicate the improvements over existing centralized sparse training technique like SWAT.
3.	Some of the insights shared (like the non-sparse weights staying the same throughout FL) were interesting.

The paper has following weaknesses:
1.	The main weakness of this paper is the experiments section. The results are presented only on CIFAR-10 dataset and do not consider many other datasets from Federated learning benchmarks (e.g., LEAF https://leaf.cmu.edu/). The authors should see relevant works like (FedProx https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.06127) and (FedMAX, https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.03657 (ECML 2020)) for details on different datasets and model types. If the experimental evaluation was comprehensive enough, this would be a very good paper (given the interesting and important problem it addresses).
2.	One other thing (although this is not the main focus of this paper), the authors should provide comparisons between strategies that result in fast convergence (without sparsity) vs. sparse methods? For example, do non-sparse, fast convergence methods (like FedProx, FedMAX, and others) result in small enough number of epochs compared to sparse methods? Can the fast convergence methods be augmented with sparisity ideas successfully without resulting in significant loss of accuracy? Some discussion and possibly performance numbers are needed here.

Summary Of The Review:
Even though the reviewer is quite happy about the problem addressed in this paper, it lacks a solid experimental section. I would be willing to increase the score if the authors can present a more comprehensive results section.

Post rebuttal:
I have read the author response and other reviews. Thanks to the authors for a detailed rebuttal and new experiments. It addressed my concerns and so I have increased the rating from 5 to 6.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.

Main Review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces ZeroFL, a method extending the SWAT unstructured sparse-training approach to federated learning (FL). Each client maintains a target sparsity level sp during both forward and backward passes, then uploads a slightly larger fraction of weights (1 – sp + r_mask) to the server. This “slack” is motivated by an ablation showing that naive SWAT application in FL degrades global accuracy. The authors propose three server-update variants—using client top-k weights, client–server weight differences, or top-k of those differences—and evaluate ZeroFL on CIFAR-10 with 100 clients under IID and non-IID splits.

Strengths  
• Builds on a simple, well-understood prior (SWAT) and adapts it cleanly for FL, making implementation straightforward.  
• Introduces a novel “slack” parameter r_mask to mitigate dilution of sparse updates during aggregation.  
• Proposes three complementary sparsification schemes, each clearly described in pseudocode.  
• Presents an empirical analysis of why high sparsity hampers FL—clients’ top-k weight patterns overlap yet get “corrupted” in aggregation.  
• Demonstrates communication savings (up to 7.4×) with modest accuracy recovery or gains versus a sparse SWAT baseline.  
• Evaluates both IID and non-IID settings, offering initial evidence of ZeroFL’s robustness to data heterogeneity.

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Missing Comparisons to Key Baselines  
   - No evaluation against federated dropout (weight-level or unit-level), structured pruning, or quantization-based FL methods, which also reduce local computation and uplink size.  
   - Few alternative FL optimizers are considered; comparisons to FedAvgM, FedAdam, or momentum/adaptive schemes could clarify interactions with sparsification.  

2. Limited Experimental Scope  
   - Only CIFAR-10 is reported in the main text with 100 clients; results on other common FL benchmarks (EMNIST/FEMNIST, SpeechCommands) would bolster generality.  
   - Single-seed runs make it hard to assess stability; multi-seed experiments and error bars are needed.  

3. Incremental Contribution  
   - ZeroFL’s core idea—a small mask-overhead slack—appears to be a minor tweak to SWAT rather than a fundamentally new algorithm. Activation sparsification, noted in SWAT, is not utilized, making the contribution narrower.  

4. Missing Practical Validation  
   - No wall-clock runtime, latency, or energy consumption measurements on real edge devices, yet on-device acceleration is a key motivator. It is unclear if unstructured sparse convolutions yield speedups on commodity mobile CPUs/GPUs.  
   - Hardware/software support requirements for unstructured sparsity are under-discussed; reported FLOPs reductions may not translate directly to device-level gains.  

5. Communication Cost Details  
   - The upload cost of transmitting sparse indices (e.g., via CSC) is only briefly mentioned in a table caption but not in the main text. It is unclear if the (r_mask – sp)/2 savings formula accounts for index overhead.  
   - Downlink compression is not explored, though it could further reduce overall communication.  

6. Algorithmic and Presentation Issues  
   - The pseudocode could be clearer (e.g., consistent if-statements for the three update modes, unified notation of top-k vs. top-k%).  
   - Figures (e.g., Figure 3) suffer from low resolution and ambiguous color schemes; axes and legends should be clarified.  
   - Equation numbering is inconsistent, and some footnotes appear misplaced.  

7. Privacy and Reproducibility  
   - The claim that client-specific sparsity patterns improve privacy is not substantiated; varying masks may leak structural information compared to a shared mask.  
   - No mention of code release or detailed reproducibility instructions.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Include comparisons to federated dropout, structured pruning/quantization methods, and momentum/adaptive FL optimizers.  
• Expand experiments to EMNIST/FEMNIST and SpeechCommands with multi-seed runs and report variance.  
• Provide real-device benchmarks for runtime and energy on representative edge hardware.  
• Conduct a deeper ablation on mask ratio (r_mask), local epoch count, and sparsity schedules to guide practitioners.  
• Clarify the derivation of communication-saving formulas, explicitly accounting for index transmission costs.  
• Refine pseudocode for clarity, standardize notation, and improve figure quality with better color palettes and higher resolution.  
• Discuss downlink compression, structured sparsity alternatives, and potential privacy implications of per-client masks.  
• Release code and detailed experimental settings for reproducibility.

Decision  
While ZeroFL offers a clear extension of SWAT to FL and introduces useful insights about sparse-update overlap, the contribution is incremental and the evaluation lacks critical baselines, statistical rigor, and practical validation. I therefore recommend marginal rejection.

Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Complex Locomotion Skill Learning via Differentiable Physics

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes to learn locomotion skills on soft robots made of springs. It proposes to leverage differentiable physics along with a NN controller based on SIREN, which allows to directly learn policies by minimizing loss functions defined on trajectories. The methods allows to learn locomotion and jump behaviors on a various set of 2D and 3D mass-spring systems.
reviews.main_review:
## Strengths

* The loss function design is clear and well explained, it is specifically tailored for cyclic motion which is the main task addressed by the paper.
* The use of SIREN to process the input and output a periodical signal is quite interesting. It suits well to tasks requiring cyclic motion.
* The comparison to PPO seems fair and thorough, I appreciate that the authors put efforts into having a well-tuned baseline, something which is often overlooked.
* The control of complex mass spring system is impressive

## Weaknesses

* The claim: "To the best of our knowledge, we proposed the first differentiable physics based learning frame-
work for complex locomotion tasks using single network." is a bit fishy.
Many RL algorithms handle locomotion tasks of humanoid-like robots in simulation with a single neural network. I understand the current framework use differentiable physics for mass-spring systems, and thus is not directly applicable to classical RL benchmarks without a differentiable physics simulator. Such a claim could be made if it solved classical RL locomotion tasks + harder ones for example.

* The experiment section is not really convincing and hard to follow. 
** Table 1 is barely commented, there is only a bold number in one column. Only tanh activation function is compared to sin, while relu, gelu, sigmoid could be used for hidden layers with an activation function outputting a value in [-1, 1] for the final layer of the network. Given that using SIREN seems to be an important component, the paper would benefit from a better ablation study. 
** The importance of features section is really unclear. 
There are sentences like "The results show that each of the three parts serves as a necessary
component for a converged training. The periodic control signal serves as an important role. The
agent can hardly move without this signal" without reference to a table or a figure, so I do not know what to look to confirm the statement.
Figure 6 is also hard to read and I am not sure if the section is super insightful. To my understanding it validates the design choice of the input feature which is a bit minor compared to other components of the model.
* In the abstract, a modified Adam optimizer is mentioned "we find our adoption of batching and modified Adam optimizer effective in training complex locomotion tasks". It seems to be important according to the authors but there is no experiment on vanilla Adam vs the proposed one with reduced momentum so we cannot asses how useful it is.

## Other comments

* The abbreviations of Table 1 could be put as caption rather than footnote for readability. The back-and-forth between table and footnote is not great for the reader.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is interesting, proposing a clean framework to perform locomotion tasks on mass-spring systems. The benefit of using differentiable physics is clearly highlighted and the use of SIREN to learn periodic motions is on point. However the experimental section is weak. For this reason I only grade it as weak-reject but would be inclined to bump it if this part is strengthened.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.

* The control of complex mass spring system is impressive

## Weaknesses

* The claim: "To the best of our knowledge, we proposed the first differentiable physics based learning frame-
work for complex locomotion tasks using single network." is a bit fishy.
Many RL algorithms handle locomotion tasks of humanoid-like robots in simulation with a single neural network. I understand the current framework use differentiable physics for mass-spring systems, and thus is not directly applicable to classical RL benchmarks without a differentiable physics simulator. Such a claim could be made if it solved classical RL locomotion tasks + harder ones for example.

* The experiment section is not really convincing and hard to follow. 
** Table 1 is barely commented, there is only a bold number in one column. Only tanh activation function is compared to sin, while relu, gelu, sigmoid could be used for hidden layers with an activation function outputting a value in [-1, 1] for the final layer of the network. Given that using SIREN seems to be an important component, the paper would benefit from a better ablation study. 
** The importance of features section is really unclear. 
There are sentences like "The results show that each of the three parts serves as a necessary
component for a converged training. The periodic control signal serves as an important role. The
agent can hardly move without this signal" without reference to a table or a figure, so I do not know what to look to confirm the statement.
Figure 6 is also hard to read and I am not sure if the section is super insightful. To my understanding it validates the design choice of the input feature which is a bit minor compared to other components of the model.
* In the abstract, a modified Adam optimizer is mentioned "we find our adoption of batching and modified Adam optimizer effective in training complex locomotion tasks". It seems to be important according to the authors but there is no experiment on vanilla Adam vs the proposed one with reduced momentum so we cannot asses how useful it is.

## Other comments

* The abbreviations of Table 1 could be put as caption rather than footnote for readability. The back-and-forth between table and footnote is not great for the reader.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is interesting, proposing a clean framework to perform locomotion tasks on mass-spring systems. The benefit of using differentiable physics is clearly highlighted and the use of SIREN to learn periodic motions is on point. However the experimental section is weak. For this reason I only grade it as weak-reject but would be inclined to bump it if this part is strengthened.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.
reviews.main_review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?

This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Main Review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a practical, end-to-end differentiable physics framework for training unified neural network controllers capable of multiple locomotion skills (running, jumping, crawling) on both 2D and 3D soft-body agents. Building on existing physics engines, the authors propose staging long-horizon tasks into manual motion primitives, injecting periodic sine activations and phase signals into the network inputs, and employing batching and a “modified Adam” optimizer to accelerate and stabilize convergence. Extensive ablation studies and comparisons to a PPO baseline demonstrate faster convergence and lower validation loss, and an interactive video showcases real-time control of complex skills. The main contribution lies in distilling empirical recipes—periodic activations, tailored per-phase loss functions, batch simulation, and optimizer choices—that together yield robust locomotion controllers trained via gradient-based methods.

Strengths  
1. Practical Guidance for Differentiable Physics.  By combining intuitive techniques—staging, sine activation layers, periodic control signals, and regularization—the paper offers a clear recipe for leveraging differentiable simulators. This empirical guide will aid researchers attempting to scale physics gradients to complex, long-horizon locomotion tasks.  
2. Comprehensive Ablation Studies.  The authors systematically isolate the contributions of optimizer hyperparameters, activation functions, loss designs (velocity, height, contact), and batch size. These studies convincingly show the roles of periodic signals and a tuned Adam variant in stabilizing gradients over hundreds of time steps.  
3. Broad Experimental Coverage.  Experiments span both mass–spring and material point method simulators, multiple agent morphologies, and 2D/3D scenarios. Comparisons against a PPO baseline highlight the data-efficiency advantage of gradient-based training, and the interactive control demos underscore the framework’s practical relevance.  
4. Real-Time Interactive Control.  The supplementary video demonstrating smooth interpolation among velocity, height, and orientation goals in real time adds to the paper’s appeal and suggests potential for user-in-the-loop applications.

Weaknesses and Questions  
1. Simulation-Only Validation.  While the framework shows strong performance in simulation, there is no real-robot or hardware-in-the-loop evaluation. Ablation results would be more compelling if accompanied by robustness tests under model error, noise, or domain randomization.  
2. Limited Baselines.  The paper compares only to a PPO-based RL method. Including additional baselines—such as model-based RL, other differentiable physics controllers (e.g., ChainQueen), or state-of-the-art soft-robot learning approaches—would strengthen the empirical claims and contextualize the efficiency gains.  
3. Computational Cost and Scalability.  Details on wall-clock training time, GPU utilization, memory footprint, and per-iteration throughput (steps/sec) are missing. Such metrics are critical for reproducibility and practical adoption, especially given the batched simulator’s resource demands.  
4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Clarity.  Key components—the modified Adam optimizer, loss weights (λv, λh, λc), and sine activation parameters—appear crucial to success but are only briefly described. It would be valuable to report sensitivity analyses or provide guidelines for tuning these in new tasks.  
5. Presentation and Notation Issues.  Several formulas (e.g., Equation 5’s subtraction of g_v(n) from A(t)) and the description of the “modified Adam” lack clarity—dimensions, equation numbering, and typo corrections are needed. Figure captions and tables would benefit from clearer units and references.  
6. Question on Iteration Definition.  In Figure 4, it is unclear whether one iteration corresponds to a single network update or a simulation step. Clarifying this would aid interpretation of convergence plots.

Detailed Suggestions  
- Elaborate on how the Adam optimizer is modified (reduced momentum? parameter schedules?) and justify those changes with empirical or theoretical insight.  
- Clarify the dimensionality and mathematical operations in Equations 4 and 5—specifically, how the target velocity term is incorporated and why height objectives are omitted.  
- Report wall-clock and throughput metrics alongside convergence curves, and discuss memory requirements for batched simulations.  
- Include robustness experiments (noise injection, perturbations, partial model mismatch) to assess real-world applicability and identify failure modes.  
- Broaden baseline comparisons to other differentiable physics frameworks or model-based RL methods to contextualize performance claims.  
- Improve notation consistency, fix typos (e.g., “dfferentiable”), and ensure all equations and loss definitions have clear numbering and prose explanations.

Conclusion  
This paper offers a valuable, empirically grounded recipe for training powerful locomotion controllers via differentiable physics. Its combination of periodic activations, staged loss functions, batch simulation, and optimizer tuning yields clear benefits over standard RL baselines, and the interactive demos are compelling. However, the lack of real-world validation, limited baseline scope, and missing runtime and hyperparameter details temper its current impact. After addressing these concerns, the work would make a strong contribution to the differentiable physics and soft-robotics communities.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.
