reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents an end-to-end differentiable physics framework for training unified neural network controllers to perform multiple soft-body locomotion skills. Building on Taichi’s existing differentiable simulator, the authors implement both mass–spring and material-point‐method backends and explore a rich combination of design choices—periodic (SIREN‐style) activations, tailored reward and loss functions for running, jumping, and turning, batched simulation instances, and a modified Adam optimizer with reduced momentum. Through extensive ablation studies, they demonstrate that with the right mixture of activation function, reward shaping, optimizer hyperparameters, batch size, and input features, a single policy can learn diverse skills on flat ground more effectively than a PPO baseline.

Strengths  
1. Practical Ablation and Design Guidance  
   – The paper’s systematic ablation study isolates the impact of optimizer settings (e.g., Adam vs. modified Adam), activation functions (sine vs. ReLU), loss weightings for velocity/height/orientation, and batch size. These experiments go beyond prior work by quantifying how each component contributes to stable gradient propagation over long horizons.  
   – By covering both 2D (mass–spring) and 3D (MPM) simulations and providing interactive real-time control demos, the work offers practitioners clear, evidence-backed recommendations for building differentiable locomotion pipelines.  

2. Empirical Efficiency Gains over PPO  
   – Across multiple soft-robot geometries and motion tasks (running, jumping, turning), the differentiable-physics approach consistently converges faster and achieves lower validation loss than PPO. This highlights the advantage of gradient‐based training in scenarios where sample efficiency and convergence speed matter.  
   – The inclusion of real-time, interactive demonstrations underscores the practical viability of the learned controllers for applications such as rapid motion prototyping or interactive robotics research.

3. Unified Multi-Skill Controller  
   – Unlike many prior works that focus on a single skill or target velocity, the authors show that a single network can smoothly interpolate among goals for velocity, height, and orientation. This unified control capability represents a step toward more general soft-robot controllers.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Novelty and Scope of Technical Contributions  
   – The paper leans heavily on the existing Taichi differentiable simulator, and the “end-to-end differentiable environment” contribution (mass–spring and MPM support) is described only at a high level. Clarifying which parts of the simulator were extended or optimized for this study—perhaps by providing pseudocode or architecture diagrams—would strengthen the technical contribution.  
   – Claims about applicability to “any physically-based deformable object simulator” appear overstated, as non-differentiable simulators cannot plug into the framework without modification. A brief discussion on the necessity of differentiability and how one might adapt other engines would resolve confusion.

2. Empirical Breadth and Realism  
   – All results remain in idealized simulation on flat terrain. Demonstrating performance on uneven or cluttered terrains, or on tasks beyond locomotion (e.g., soft-body manipulation or object pushing), would substantiate the framework’s generality.  
   – No hardware-in-the-loop or real-robot experiments are presented. Even a small proof-of-concept on a soft robotic prototype, or a domain-randomization study to assess robustness to modeling errors, would greatly enhance real-world relevance.

3. Baselines and Comparative Analysis  
   – While PPO is a reasonable baseline, comparisons to other differentiable-physics controllers (e.g., ChainQueen) or model-based RL methods (SAC, TRPO) would position this work more clearly relative to state of the art.  
   – Ablations on contact-rich behaviors (climbing, grasping) are missing; discussing failure modes under discontinuous contact dynamics would guide future extensions.

4. Computational Cost and Reproducibility  
   – The manuscript omits wall-clock training times, GPU/CPU utilization, memory footprints, and throughput metrics (steps per second). Reporting these would help readers assess practical deployment costs.  
   – Detailed hyperparameter settings—especially the loss weights (λv, λh, λc), periodic signal frequencies, and modified Adam schedules—should be consolidated in a table or appendix. Guidance on how sensitive performance is to these choices would aid new users.

5. Presentation and Clarity  
   – Several typographical and formatting issues (e.g., stray symbols in Equation 4, inconsistent notation for summations and goals, missing units in tables and figure captions) detract from readability. A careful proofreading pass and consistent equation numbering would address these concerns.  
   – Some figures and tables are overly dense. Streamlining captions and adding callouts to key trends (e.g., percent speedup over PPO) would improve clarity.

Additional Questions  
– How does performance degrade if the simulator’s material parameters are perturbed?  
– Could the framework handle longer horizons (e.g., multi-second tasks) without gradient instability?  
– What are the limits on batch size given memory constraints, and how does batch size trade off with convergence speed?

Conclusion and Recommendation  
This paper provides a valuable, practitioner-oriented study of how differentiable simulators can be leveraged—through careful design of activations, losses, batching, and optimizers—to learn versatile soft-body locomotion controllers. Despite relatively modest technical novelty in the underlying simulator engine, the extensive empirical analysis and clear performance gains over PPO make this work a solid contribution to the reinforcement learning and soft-robotics communities. I therefore recommend acceptance at the marginal threshold.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance bar)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.
reviews.main_review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents an end-to-end differentiable physics framework for training unified neural network controllers that perform multiple soft-robot locomotion skills—running, jumping, crawling in 2D and running plus rotation in 3D—within a Taichi-based simulator. The authors integrate periodic SIREN-style activations, tailored loss terms for different motion goals (velocity, height, orientation), batched simulation instances, and a modified Adam optimizer to improve robustness and data efficiency. Extensive ablation studies isolate the impact of each component, and empirical comparisons against a PPO baseline demonstrate faster convergence and lower validation losses. The system supports real-time interactive control and smooth interpolation among skills for a variety of mass–spring and MPM soft-robot morphologies.

Strengths  
1. Multi-skill capability and interactive control: The framework convincingly scales differentiable simulation beyond single-goal tasks, allowing one network to switch among diverse locomotion behaviors in real time.  
2. Comprehensive ablation studies: By systematically varying activation functions, loss components, input features, batch sizes, and optimizer settings, the paper provides clear evidence for the utility of periodic activations and period-aware losses.  
3. Empirical gains over PPO: Across 2D and 3D soft agents, gradient-based training yields faster convergence and lower validation losses than a PPO baseline, supporting the claim that differentiable physics can be a more data-efficient alternative for these tasks.  
4. Support for multiple simulation backends and agent geometries: Demonstrating on both mass–spring and MLS-MPM simulators and on various robot designs enhances the paper’s practical relevance.  
5. System-level contributions: The batched simulator execution and the modified Adam optimizer (with reduced momentum) yield notable speedups in gradient evaluation and model training.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited technical novelty and generalization: While the combination of existing techniques is solid, the specific loss designs and optimizer tweaks are tailored to the presented locomotion tasks. It remains unclear whether these ideas extend to other robot morphologies, contact-rich behaviors (e.g., climbing or manipulation), or real-world hardware [1, 2].  
2. Inconsistent support for periodic activations: Table 1 shows only marginal gains of sine over tanh, and Appendix Table 5 even suggests tanh can outperform sine in some cases. The paper would benefit from a broader comparison—including ReLU or other modern activations—and a deeper analysis of when and why SIREN-style activations help.  
3. Baseline selection: The empirical evaluation is limited to a PPO baseline. Including comparisons to other model-based RL methods, differentiable simulators such as ChainQueen, or state-of-the-art soft-robot learning frameworks would strengthen the claims.  
4. Missing robustness and real-world validation: All experiments are in idealized simulation. There is no hardware-in-the-loop test, domain randomization, or noise sensitivity study to assess robustness to modeling errors or sim-to-real transfer.  
5. Lack of computational cost metrics: The paper omits details on wall-clock training time, GPU/CPU utilization, memory footprint, and per-step throughput (e.g., simulation steps per second). Reporting these would greatly aid reproducibility and adoption.  
6. Hyperparameter sensitivity: Key design choices—loss weights (λv, λh, λc), batch size, and modified Adam hyperparameters—appear critical but are only briefly described. Guidance on tuning these parameters for new tasks is missing.  
7. Presentation and clarity issues:  
   • The modification to the Adam optimizer is not clearly described or compared to vanilla Adam.  
   • Agent names are introduced in Fig. 7 but referenced earlier, creating confusion; these should be defined early.  
   • Figure and table references are sometimes incorrect (e.g., Fig. 5 is cited for SGD vs. Adam but shows no such comparison).  
   • Equations (e.g., Eq. 4) contain stray symbols and inconsistent notation; loss definitions lack clear numbering and prose explanations.  
   • Captions in Table 1 and elsewhere omit units and statistical significance tests.  
   • Typos (e.g., “dfferentible”) and formatting inconsistencies distract from the core contributions.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Broaden the empirical evaluation by adding:  
   – Comparisons to additional baselines (model-based RL, SAC/TRPO, other differentiable simulators).  
   – Robustness tests under perturbations, domain randomization, or simulated noise.  
   – A real-robot or hardware-in-the-loop demonstration, even on a simplified platform.  
2. Provide computational metrics: wall-clock training times, GPU memory usage, throughput in steps-per-second, and solver performance details.  
3. Expand activation function study: include ReLU, leaky ReLU, and discuss failure modes when sequences grow long.  
4. Clarify optimizer modifications: state the exact changes to Adam’s momentum or learning rate schedule, and present ablations against the standard Adam.  
5. Detail hyperparameter tuning: include a sensitivity analysis for loss weights and periodic activation frequencies, and offer heuristics for practitioners.  
6. Improve presentation: correct figure/table references, unify notation throughout, number and explain all equations, define agents’ names early, and include units and statistical significance in result tables.  
7. Release code and simulation scripts to facilitate reproduction and further extension by the community.

Conclusion  
This work offers a compelling demonstration that differentiable physics can train unified controllers for multiple soft-robot locomotion skills with data efficiency gains over PPO and real-time interactive capabilities. However, the paper’s technical contributions are somewhat incremental, and its scope is limited by simulation-only experiments, narrow baselines, and missing computational and robustness analyses. The presentation requires substantial polishing to ensure clarity and reproducibility. Under the current form, I rate this submission a 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold) but would be willing to revisit if the authors address the outlined concerns.

Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:
P1 last paragraph : I’m not sure if being unaware to the task is a bad thing necessarily.  isn't it better to do imputation in a task agnostic fashion?

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?

Weaknesses:

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper addresses the problem of training graph neural networks when node features are partially missing by proposing a diffusion-based preprocessing step dubbed Feature Propagation (FP). The method is derived from minimizing the Dirichlet energy on the graph, leading to a heat-diffusion PDE whose explicit Euler discretization yields a simple iterative algorithm. At each iteration, features diffuse via the normalized adjacency and known entries are reset as Dirichlet boundary conditions. FP is scalable (e.g., reconstructing on a 2.5 M-node graph in seconds) and can be used with any downstream GNN. Empirical results on six standard benchmarks and two large OGBN datasets show that FP+GNN outperforms prior missing-feature methods (GCNMF, PaGNN) and simple baselines (label propagation, neighbor averaging), sustaining only ~4% relative drop even at 99% missing rates.

Strengths:
1. Theoretical grounding. Deriving FP from Dirichlet energy minimization and connecting it to classical harmonic interpolation provides clear conceptual motivation and guarantees convergence to the unique solution.
2. Simplicity and modularity. Algorithm 1 requires only sparse–dense matrix multiplications and boundary resets; it is easy to implement and integrates seamlessly as a preprocessing step for any GNN.
3. Scalability and empirical performance. FP scales linearly in |E| and runs in seconds on million-node graphs. Extensive experiments across small and large datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy under very high missing rates, outperforming both graph-aware and feature-agnostic baselines.
4. Robustness analysis. The authors systematically report accuracy drops at 50%, 90%, and 99% missing, and include a synthetic homophily study to clarify FP’s domain of applicability.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Relation to prior diffusion methods. While FP is positioned as distinct from label propagation (due to continuous features and different operators), a more detailed comparison to classical graph signal interpolation and continuous GNNs (e.g. GRAND) would strengthen the novelty claim.
2. Hyperparameter ablations. The choice of step size h and fixed iteration count (e.g., 40 steps) appears arbitrary. A quantitative study on convergence rates, sensitivity to h, and a complexity analysis (runtime vs. |V|, |E|, iterations) would clarify practical trade-offs.
3. Feature dimensionality. It remains unclear how FP extends to vector-valued or correlated features. Does one run independent diffusions per channel, or could inter-channel correlations be exploited? A brief discussion or experiment in this direction would be valuable.
4. Low-homophily and heterogeneous graphs. FP’s assumption of feature homophily may limit performance on heterophilic or multi-modal graphs. Evaluating on real low-homophily datasets or exploring learnable diffusion weights could broaden applicability.
5. Additional baselines and tasks. Comparisons to recent learned imputation methods (e.g., VAE-based, GAIN, SAT) and demonstrations on tasks beyond node classification (e.g., link prediction) would further validate generality.
6. Presentation improvements. The pseudocode could explicitly highlight the reset step; figure text (especially in Fig. 3) should be enlarged; and formatting inconsistencies (e.g., equation alignment, figure numbering) merit cleanup.

Overall, this paper presents a well-motivated, elegant, and scalable solution to missing-feature imputation in GNNs, and its strong empirical gains justify acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: A graph neural network (GNN) pre-processing step for completing missing node features is presented: feature propagation (FP). The method is based on minimisation of Dirichlet energy. A more efficient iterative update aproach is derived avoiding matrix inversion (cubic operation) to a series of sparse-to-dense matrix multiplications. The method acts similar to a low-pass filter for missing features, which complements the GNN learning algorithm. Relevant work is covered and similarities and differences mentioned in many cases. Experiments on seven publicly available datasets show that the method is effective and performs especially well when a large proportion of the feature values are missing (above 90%). A run-time analysis of wall-clock performance compares their method to two state-of-the-art methods for handling missing node features in GNNs and their method is 3 x faster in these comparison. A weakness of their method, FP, is analysed; FP assumes the graphs are homophlic, that nearby nodes have similar node features but if this is not the case then the method does not perform well. This is some interesting future work.
reviews.main_review:
This paper addresses a very interesting problem that is relevant to real-world applications; dealing with missing node features for graph neural networks (GNNs). The approach is based on minimising Dirichlet energy, which is a emthod previously used for graph regularisation and has relations with graph Laplcians, so is well suited to working with data on graph topology. The author(s) derive an efficient iterative algorithm which makes the approach scalable to large graphs which makes it more practical for real-world applications. Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

Many public datasets were used for benchmarking that show very promising results.

In the experiments, it's surprising how well the nearest-neigbour averaging worked. In the comparison method's own papers SAT paper has a 'NeighAggre' baseline that seems to do something similar but gets worse scores than their method and GCNMF compares with kNN with k=5, and this also performs worse than their method. Do you have some insights into why neighbourhood averaging performs so well?

This method is a pre-processing step, not integrated into the GNN, as such many other methods for estimating the missing values could be used. One justification of this choice of approach is that is acts as a low-pass filter, and the GNN mainly learns from the lower frequency components.  This work is very close to matrix completion with graph side information.In this domain there are other methods that are highly scalable and are able to accurately estimate missing values when large amounts of the data (>99%) are missing [1, 2, 4]. Furthermore, one method uses a similar optimisation approach of iterative dense-sparse matrix mulitplication [1]. Worth note is that an extension to [1] will also remove edges in the graph that do not have high-homophily that could improve results for low-himopholy results. Also [3] is a kernel method for matrix completion that allows for a choice of covariance functions, including a diffusion kernel that is therefore similar to this approach. I think it is worth at mentioning this in comparison to this approach and even adding emprical evaluation against these methods, especially [1].

Other small comments:

You report using V100 GPUs (16 or 32Gb?), how much memory did GCNMF take before ran out of memory.

It wasn't crystal clear how the approach works for vector node attributes, do you simply repeat the algorithm for each dimension separately?

"Proposition 1: inverse of Laplacian depends on a connected graph, as eigen values are 0 if graph has clusters." How true is this assumption in real graphs, and what can be done in the case where a graph is not connected?

How do you know 40 iterations is enough, is there a easy guide to this and what happens if you keep running the algorithm, will it eventually make all missing featues almost the same if run for a large number of iterations?

"For a fair comparison, we use the same standard hyperparameters for all methods across all experiments." Does this mean that the downstream 2-layer GCN model has the same hyperparameters? 

Edits:
    A_u should be A_uu ?
    
    Zhu and Ghahramani reference is incomplete

    donwstream task -> downstream task


1. Rao, Nikhil, et al. "Collaborative Filtering with Graph Information: Consistency and Scalable Methods." NIPS. Vol. 2. No. 4. 2015.

2. Strahl, Jonathan, et al. "Scalable probabilistic matrix factorization with graph-based priors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 34. No. 04. 2020.

3. Zhou, Tinghui, et al. "Kernelized probabilistic matrix factorization: Exploiting graphs and side information." Proceedings of the 2012 SIAM international Conference on Data mining. Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, 2012.

4. Monti, Federico, Michael M. Bronstein, and Xavier Bresson. "Geometric matrix completion with recurrent multi-graph neural networks." arXiv preprint arXiv:1704.06803 (2017).

Summary Of The Review:
As this is a preprocessing step that essentially is similar to matrix completion with graph side information, then I believe that comparison with this literature is missing. Otherwise this is a very good paper. The approach they have for the problem, which could be seen as matrix completion with graph side information, is I believe novel in that domain, as well as in this framework for graph neural networks. Therefore, comparison with this domain is important here, as other methods in this domain could also be well justified as pre-processing steps. One such method that both scalable (using similar final equation, but dervied from a different approach, conjugate gradients), must be compared both in the paper and experiments in my opinion. Otherwise, it feels like the work is trying to avoid comparison with similar methods by claiming this work is related to GNN, while really it feels like this is a matrix completion with graph side information method, that is then used for GNNs.

After feedback:

I still feel that the motivation for using this particular method of estimating the missing values is not clearly better than other approaches. Also, the link to GNN seems weak as essentially this is a method for completing missing data and with this seems better suited to the domain for estimating missing values, where a stronger comparison with other missing values estimating methods can be compared - for example in comparison to recommender system approaches like collaborative filtering with graph side information. The link to GNN could be a small subsection in that style of paper. I don't see why running GNN is such a large part of this work when it's a stand alone preprocessing step. Therefore I've lowered my score from 6 to 5.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.
reviews.main_review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a fast way to do node feature imputation in graph learning settings. In a nutshell, the idea is to consider the graph topology in the imputation by doing a feature by feature minimization of the Laplacian quadratic form. However, since the exact solution to this requires a matrix inversion, this quantity is minimized by a gradient descent procedure that relies on sparse matrix multiplications.

Main Review:
Through numerical experiments the authors illustrate the benefit of this simple approach. Nonetheless, my main concern has to do with the novelty of this. Using the quadratic form of the graph Laplacian (Dirichlet energy) for label propagation or graph signal interpolation has been used several times over the last decade (as referenced in this paper). Moreover, using a gradient descent approach instead of inverting the Laplacian has also been used quite often. E.g., see the tutorial "Signal processing on higher-order networks: Livin’on the edge... and beyond" where in Section 2.4.2 the authors review some typical formulations of signals on graphs before going into higher-order networks. Notice that Equation (4) contains (I-L)^k, which corresponds to several applications of the (normalized) adjacency matrix. In fact, it is mentioned that "This may be interpreted in terms of k gradient descent steps of the cost function (Dirichlet energy)".

From the perspective of graph signal processing, the true solution with the inverse of the Laplacian can be seen as an IIR filter and this is an FIR approximation of the filter. This idea of graph filter design has also been studied quite a bit over the last 7 years.

Summary Of The Review:
Under this perspective, I regard that the novelty in this paper is limited.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the problem of imputing missing node features in graph‐based learning by minimizing the Dirichlet energy (Laplacian quadratic form) feature‐by‐feature. Rather than computing the closed‐form inverse of the Laplacian, the authors propose a simple explicit Euler (gradient descent) scheme that relies solely on sparse matrix multiplications and resets known values at each iteration. Empirically, the method—termed Feature Propagation (FP)—is evaluated across six standard benchmarks and two large‐scale OGBN datasets, demonstrating robustness to extreme missing rates (up to 99% missing with only ~4% relative drop in accuracy) and scalability to millions of nodes.

Strengths  
1. Theoretical grounding and simplicity: As several reviewers note, the derivation from minimizing Dirichlet energy yields a clear diffusion/PDE interpretation and guarantees convergence to the harmonic interpolant. The explicit Euler discretization is straightforward to implement, requiring only sparse‐dense multiplications and boundary resets.  
2. Scalability: Experiments on a 2.5 M‐node, 123 M‐edge graph complete in seconds on a single GPU, illustrating practical efficiency for real‐world, large‐scale applications.  
3. Empirical performance: Across small benchmarks and OGBN datasets, FP combined with standard GNNs (e.g., GCN, GraphSAGE) consistently outperforms prior missing‐feature methods (GCNMF, PaGNN) and simple baselines (neighbor mean, label propagation), particularly at high missing rates.  
4. Reproducibility and clarity of experiments: Public release of code, detailed hyperparameter settings, and standard data splits enhance reproducibility. The robustness analysis—evaluating accuracy drops at 50%, 90%, and 99% missing—provides a comprehensive view of performance under varying degrees of feature sparsity.

Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement  
1. Limited novelty: The core idea—gradient‐based approximation of Laplacian inversion for interpolation—has deep roots in graph signal processing and label propagation literature. Previous works (e.g., signal processing tutorials with (I–L)^k filters, IIR vs. FIR graph filters, GRAND) have already explored similar formulations. This overlap diminishes the paper’s originality.  
2. Positioning relative to existing methods: While the paper cites classical and PDE‐based approaches, it underplays connections to continuous‐time GNNs (e.g., GRAND) and other diffusion‐based filter designs. A clearer discussion of how FP differs from or extends these prior schemes would strengthen the narrative.  
3. Hyperparameter sensitivity and convergence analysis:  
   – The choice of step size (h = 1) and fixed iteration count (e.g., 40 steps) appears arbitrary; no ablation or convergence diagnostics are provided.  
   – Explicit rate bounds or sensitivity plots for step size and number of iterations would help users understand convergence behavior and runtime‐accuracy trade‐offs.  
4. Reliance on homophily and independent feature channels:  
   – FP assumes strong feature homophily; synthetic experiments show performance degrades sharply under low homophily, yet real heterophilic graphs are not evaluated.  
   – Treating each feature channel independently ignores potential inter‐feature correlations. Exploring multi‐channel diffusion or learnable mixing could improve performance on heterogeneous attribute sets.  
5. Missing comparisons and broader evaluation:  
   – The manuscript omits comparisons to recent learned imputation methods (e.g., VAE‐based, GAIN, transformer‐based SAT) and sophisticated matrix‐completion techniques, despite citing them.  
   – All experiments focus on node classification. Evaluating FP on other tasks—such as link prediction or heterogeneous graphs—would broaden applicability.  
6. Presentation issues:  
   – Pseudocode should explicitly highlight the reset step for known nodes.  
   – Figure numbering and equation formatting contain inconsistencies.  
   – A compact complexity table (runtime vs. |V|, |E|, iterations) and discussion of memory requirements would aid clarity.

Suggestions  
• Strengthen the novelty claim by explicitly contrasting FP with existing graph filter designs, continuous GNN frameworks, and classical label propagation variants.  
• Include ablation studies on step size, iteration count, and initialization of missing values, with convergence diagnostics.  
• Evaluate on at least one real heterophilic graph dataset or introduce adaptive diffusion weights to handle weak homophily scenarios.  
• Incorporate comparisons to learned‐imputation baselines (e.g., SAT, VAE/GAN approaches) to position FP within the broader imputation literature.  
• Improve presentation by clarifying pseudocode, fixing numbering issues, and providing a succinct complexity analysis.

Decision  
Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold.  
While the paper presents a practical, scalable, and empirically strong method for imputing missing node features, its limited novelty and gaps in comparison and analysis suggest that it falls short of the bar for publication in its current form.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper address the problem of  missing node features in graph by conducting feature propagation (FP). An iterative updated algorithm is presented. FP is a preprocessing step and can be used in multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on node classification shows the effectiveness of FP. The authors also provided comparison on running time and showed how homophily affects the performance of FP.
reviews.main_review:
The strengths:

1. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow
2. The performance on missing 99% of the features is impressive
3. The speed improvement is promising

The weaknesses:

1. FP is not a new idea/algorithm, it is essentially same as label propagation (LP) by Zhu et al. [1]. Although the authors argued that "Differently from our setting of diffusion of continuous node features, they deal with discrete label classes directly, resulting in a different diffusion operator.", from my understanding, there is not substantial difference. LP also propagate the label probability, which is continuous. The derivation, the update formula of FP are the same as LP, the only difference is to substitute the weight matrix in LP with Laplacian matrix. Interpreting label propagation via Dirchlet energy is also proposed in [2], where instead of using $x_i$, $x_j$, Solomon et al. were using $f_i$, $f_j$, which are label function of node $i$ and $j$. Consider $x_i$'s as the soft label with label function $f_i$, then they are essentially the same thing.
2. The experiment part shows that simple averaging of the neighbors gives good result when the missing portion is $\leq$ 50%. In that case, I think it is also important to show experimental results of algorithms that do not use node features, to see if there is really a gap on the performance. (Note that under strong assumption of homophily, it is not surprising that simple averaging of neighbors works well. FP or LP takes account the topology of the graph and changes simple averaging of neighbors to weighted average of the whole network. The global information of the graph is the key to a better performance in this case, and I doubt other algorithms using the global information can also get similar good performance.) For example, one can use simple label propagation on the node classes, or using LINE, 
DeepWalk or node2vec to get a embedding of each node and then classifying with the embeddings. If the performance is similar as what FP gives when there are 99% features missing, I would argue that in that case we can simply go with classical algorithms using only the network topology. We will only use GNN when we have enough features available, while in which case it seems simple averaging of neighbors is good enough. The paper is not so convincing at the current stage.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

[2] Solomon, Justin, Raif Rustamov, Leonidas Guibas, and Adrian Butscher. "Wasserstein propagation for semi-supervised learning." In International Conference on Machine Learning, pp. 306-314. PMLR, 2014.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: In general I like the idea of using FP as a preprocessing step. However, I am not convinced that the idea is novel enough to be accepted in ICLR, details are referred to my main review.

[1] Xiaojin, Zhu, and Ghahramani Zoubin. "Learning from labeled and unlabeled data with label propagation." Tech. Rep., Technical Report CMU-CALD-02–107, Carnegie Mellon University (2002).

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the important problem of imputing missing node features in graph-based learning by proposing Feature Propagation (FP), an iterative diffusion process derived from minimizing the Dirichlet energy on the graph. Known node features serve as Dirichlet boundary conditions, and at each step, features diffuse via the normalized adjacency followed by a reset of observed entries. The reconstructed feature matrix can then be fed into any downstream GNN. Empirical evaluation on six standard benchmarks and two large OGBN datasets (up to 2.5 M nodes, 123 M edges) shows that FP+GNN suffers only about a 4% relative accuracy drop even when 99% of features are missing, and completes reconstruction in seconds on a single GPU.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and presentation. The paper is clearly written, with a concise derivation of FP as an explicit Euler discretization of a heat diffusion PDE linked to Dirichlet energy minimization. The modular separation between preprocessing (FP) and downstream GNN makes the approach easy to understand and integrate.  
2. Theoretical motivation. By connecting feature imputation to classical graph signal processing and convex optimization, the authors ground FP in solid theory and guarantee convergence to the unique harmonic interpolant.  
3. Simplicity and scalability. Algorithm 1 requires only sparse-dense multiplications and boundary resets, scaling linearly in the number of edges. Results on OGBN-Products (2.5 M nodes) in ≈10 s on a single GPU demonstrate practical efficiency unmatched by many learned imputation schemes.  
4. Empirical robustness. Comprehensive experiments across small and large graphs show that FP+GNN outperforms prior methods (e.g., GCNMF, PaGNN) and strong baselines (Neighbor Mean, Label Propagation) under extreme missing‐feature regimes (>90%). Synthetic ablations on homophily levels further clarify when FP is most effective.  

Weaknesses  
1. Novelty and positioning. At its core, FP closely mirrors classical label propagation and harmonic interpolation techniques (Zhu et al. 2002; Solomon et al. 2014). The update formula and diffusion operator differ only in minor matrix‐weight substitutions, and continuous label‐probability propagation in LP already yields a continuous diffusion. The manuscript does not sufficiently distinguish FP from these well‐known methods or compare to other PDE‐based GNNs (e.g., GRAND).  
2. Missing comparisons to relevant baselines. While the paper benchmarks against PaGNN and GCNMF, it omits recent learned imputation approaches such as SAT or graph-based VAEs (e.g., GAIN), as well as classical topology-only methods like DeepWalk or node2vec embeddings followed by classification. Including these would clarify whether FP’s gains derive purely from global graph structure rather than any novel algorithmic twist.  
3. Limited hyperparameter and convergence analysis. The choice of step size h and iteration count (e.g., h = 1, 40 iterations) appears arbitrary. No ablation or convergence rate is provided, and there is no complexity analysis relating runtime to |V|, |E|, and iteration number.  
4. Homophily assumption and heterophilic evaluation. All real‐world experiments are conducted on homophilic graphs. Given FP’s reliance on feature and label smoothness, performance in low-homophily or heterophilic settings may degrade sharply, yet no real heterophilic benchmarks are tested.  
5. Ignoring inter-feature correlations. FP processes each feature channel independently, foregoing any modelling of feature covariance or multi-modal interactions, which may limit its applicability to richer attribute spaces (e.g., image or text features).  
6. Scope of downstream tasks. The paper focuses exclusively on node classification. Exploring tasks like link prediction or heterogeneous graph settings would strengthen the claim of generality.  
7. Minor clarity and formatting issues. Algorithm pseudocode does not explicitly highlight the reset step, figure numbering is occasionally inconsistent, and some displayed equations and citations lack uniform styling.  

Recommendation  
While Feature Propagation offers a simple, scalable, and empirically strong preprocessing step for missing-feature imputation, its core idea closely parallels established label propagation and harmonic interpolation methods. The novelty over classical diffusion schemes is incremental, and key baselines and ablations are missing. I therefore recommend a score of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold. A more thorough positioning against prior work, expanded baseline comparisons (including topology-only and learned imputation methods), hyperparameter and convergence studies, and evaluation on heterophilic and additional downstream tasks would be necessary to elevate this submission to acceptability.

title: Learning Audio-Visual Dereverberation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?

But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines an audio-visual approach for dereverberation, where dereverberation of speech is conditioned on RGB and depth images (either field-of-view or panoramic). It proposes a dataset for this task based on real-world 3D scans of homes, using Librispeech data. Real data is also used The model is based on a U-Net conditioned on embeddings extracted by a "visual acoustics" network doing direct spectrogram prediction. The method is evaluated using PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic and real data. The audio-visual method is found to perform marginally better than an audio-only version of the model. The audio-only version of the model outperforms several baselines from the literature on synthetic data, with mixed results on real data.

Main Review:
### Strengths:

S1) To my knowledge, this is the first audio-visual approach to dereverberation. I think it is interesting to see how much visual information can help.

S2) The proposed dataset seems quite useful. A lot of existing synthetic reverb for such audio tasks is generated using shoebox room simulators, so having more realistic environments seems quite useful. The accompanying data recorded in real environments using loudspeaker playback (such that the clean reference is available) is also quite useful, and I hope the authors plan to release a recipe for this data.

S3) The paper includes a lot of ablations, which I really appreciate. Though the focus of the paper is on audio-visual approaches to the task, I'm really glad the paper evaluates audio-only versions of the model, as well as other components such as the matching loss.

S4) The paper is clear and well-written.

### Weaknesses:

W1) I listened to the demos in the supplementary material, and I feel like I heard a fair amount of artifacts and distortion in some examples: for moderate reverb, the model predictions have a certain "buzziness" to them, and more extreme levels of reverb have a "twanginess" to them. Something that would make this paper stronger would be a subjective human listening evaluation, e.g. MUSHRA, which would measure these kinds of artifacts better than an objective measure like PESQ. Also, alternative loss functions may help reduce artifacts. E.g. L1 loss instead of L2 on spectrogram magnitude, or MSE on power-compressed spectrograms can provide more perceptually-relevant loss functions.

W2) The processing method of using 2.56s frames, then concatenating the non-overlapping parts together seems suboptimal to me. Why not use e.g. a Hann window to stitch the predicted patches with overlap-add, or invert back to the time-domain and do overlap-add and train through this?

W3) Where does the "random speech embedding sampled from the data batch." come from? How is it computed? Is this a speaker ID embedding?

W4) The improvements by adding visual information are marginal compared to the audio-only model. The audio-only model does perform better than the baselines, but degrades PESQ compared to other baselines on real data. To benchmark the audio-only baseline against the literature, it would be interesting to compare to competitive systems in e.g. the REVERB challenge. I'm also not very convinced that visual input really helps that much.

W5) Table 1 would be improved by adding trainable parameter counts. Is the proposed model only doing better because it has more trainable parameters? WPE itself has no trainable parameters, since it's just a model-based EM algorithm.

W6) The lack of noise in the training and eval data limits real-world applicability, but I understand the focus of the paper in only on dereverberation. I'm glad that the authors tested noise robustness with WHAM! data, but 20 dB is a pretty high SNR for noise robustness. It would be interesting to see how performance degrades with decreasing SNR.

### Minor comments

M1) I feel that this statement isn't quite right: "R is a function of...the relative positioning of the speaker and the listener." I would argue that an RIR can be a function of the absolute positioning of the speaker and the listener. E.g. what if a source is close to a wall, versus in the the middle of a room?

M2) For the real data, would it be possible to be more specific about the source-to-microphone distances used in the real recordings, beyond "near-field to mid-field to far-field."?

M3) Also concerning real data: would it be possible to more more specific for this statement? "For each location, we play around 10 utterances.". How many utterances? Gender balanced? Different speakers across rooms?

M4) Nit: U-Net instead of UNet?

M5) "While our model is agnostic to the audio source type,": depends on the training data.

M6) In Tables 1 and 2, I would say "anechoic speech (upper bound)" instead of "clean speech (upper bound)", because to me "clean" suggests there's additive noise.

M7) About the blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions: "After converging, the average errors for them are 3e-3s, 160 dB and 17.92m respectively." Are these absolute or squared errors?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I vote for marginal acceptance. This is an interesting novel task (audio-visual dereverberation), and the proposed synthetic and real data are quite interesting and useful for the community. But the paper's results show that visual input provides marginal performance gain over the audio-only baseline. The audio-only baseline outperforms some benchmarks in terms of PESQ, WER for speech recognition, and EER for speaker verification on synthetic data, and WER and EER for real data, but I have concerns about the subjective listening quality of the demos, compared to other dereverberation algorithms I have listened to. Thus, I am concerned that this isn't a significant improvement over audio-only prior work, at least in terms of subjective listening quality.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper addresses the novel task of audio-visual speech dereverberation by conditioning a U-Net spectrogram-prediction model on visual scene cues (RGB and depth, either narrow field-of-view or panoramic). The authors build VIDA, which combines a Visual Acoustics Network (VAN) to encode environment geometry and materials with a dereverberation U-Net, and train/evaluate it on a new large-scale synthetic dataset based on 3D home scans (SoundSpaces+LibriSpeech) as well as a small real-world loudspeaker playback dataset with ground-truth clean references. Experiments on PESQ, WER (ASR), and EER (speaker verification) show that (1) the audio-only version already outperforms several baselines; (2) adding visual inputs yields consistent but marginal gains; and (3) the model transfers reasonably to real recordings.

Strengths:
1. Novel Task and Approach: To our knowledge, VIDA is the first end-to-end audio-visual dereverberation framework. The idea of leveraging visual geometry and depth to inform speech dereverberation is well motivated and technically sound, filling a clear gap in prior work.
2. Valuable Dataset: The proposed synthetic dataset—rendered over 82 Matterport3D scans—and the accompanying real playback recordings provide a rich resource beyond traditional shoebox simulators. The inclusion of real data with known clean references is particularly useful, and the authors’ plan to release data collection recipes is commendable.
3. Thorough Ablations: The paper presents extensive ablation studies on audio-only vs. audio-visual, narrow vs. panorama views, the contrastive matching loss, and human mesh cues. These analyses convincingly demonstrate the contributions of each component.
4. Multi-Task Evaluation: By evaluating on speech enhancement (PESQ), ASR (WER), and speaker verification (EER), the paper shows that the audio-visual model yields statistically significant improvements over strong audio-only baselines across tasks.
5. Clarity and Reproducibility: The writing is clear, implementation details and hyperparameters are provided, and the model design (ResNet encoders for RGB/depth, U-Net conditioning) is well explained.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Marginal Visual Gains: While visual conditioning consistently improves metrics, the gains over the audio-only model are modest. It would strengthen the claim of “utility of vision” to benchmark against additional dereverberation baselines (e.g. REVERB challenge winners) and recent audio-visual separation/enhancement methods. Reporting statistical significance or error bars across environments could clarify the robustness of these gains.
2. Subjective Listening Evaluation: Objective metrics like PESQ may not fully capture artifacts. The demos exhibit “buzziness” or “twanginess” under moderate or extreme reverberation. A subjective human listening study (e.g. MUSHRA) or perceptually-motivated losses (L1 on magnitude, compressed-spectrogram MSE) could better assess and mitigate artifacts.
3. Frame Stitching and Time-Domain Overlap: The current use of non-overlapping 2.56s spectrogram frames may introduce boundary artifacts. Consider Hann window overlap-add in the spectrogram domain or time-domain reconstruction with trainable overlap-add to produce smoother outputs.
4. Baseline and Parameter Comparisons: Table 1 should include trainable parameter counts for VIDA vs. baselines (e.g. WPE has zero learned parameters). This will clarify whether performance gains stem from multimodal conditioning or simply a larger model capacity.
5. Real-World Evaluation Scope: The real dataset (≈200 recordings) spans only a few room types and near-to-far-field distances. Expanding to more varied, dynamic, and cluttered environments (including outdoor or public spaces) would better demonstrate real-world applicability. Similarly, evaluating performance under lower SNRs (beyond the 20 dB WHAM! test) and with noisy or imperfect depth estimates would probe noise robustness and sensor limitations.
6. Visual Assumptions and Temporal Context: Relying on static panoramic RGB-D may limit consumer applications. Investigating monocular or partial FoV inputs, depth noise, occlusions, and temporal scene dynamics (e.g. moving speakers) would enhance generality. Furthermore, exploring recurrent or transformer-based temporal modeling could capture longer reverberation tails.
7. Hyperparameter Justification: The margin in the matching loss, λ weights, and number of Griffin-Lim iterations lack detailed motivation. A sensitivity analysis or validation curves would guide reproducibility and model selection.
8. Clarifications and Minor Points:
   • Please specify how the “random speech embedding” is computed—does it represent speaker identity?
   • Clarify source-to-microphone distances and utterance counts/gender balance in real recordings.
   • In Tables 1–2, consider “anechoic speech (upper bound)” rather than “clean speech” to avoid confusion with additive noise.
   • Correct nit: “U-Net” instead of “UNet.”
   • For blind RT60/DRR/distance predictions, clarify whether reported errors are absolute or squared.
   • On terminology: an RIR is a function of absolute source–receiver positions (e.g. proximity to walls) as well as relative placement.

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. Overall, this work makes a timely and novel contribution to multimodal dereverberation, proposing valuable datasets and a well-designed model with solid empirical results. Addressing the above concerns—particularly expanding real-world validation, enriching baseline comparisons, and conducting subjective evaluations—would further solidify the impact, but the current submission merits marginal acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a deep neural net-based dereverberation algorithm that uses both audio and video modalities. Based on the observation that a visual scene captured by a camera conveys information that is related to room characteristics, the authors propose a visually informed audio dereverberation method that aims to extract clean, anechoic speech from reverberant speech. In doing so, they first construct a large audio-visual dataset synthesized using a 3D simulator for real-world scanned environments and LibriSpeech data. They then train deep neural networks that take as inputs both visual data (RGB and depth images) and audio data (reverberant speech), and output clean speech. When training, two types of losses - one for clean speech spectrogram estimation and the other for reverb-visual matching - are used. Through the experiments with several downstream tasks for speech, they showed that the proposed audio-visual dererberation method outperforms the baseline models, both for synthetic and real-world test data.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- first multi-modal (audio-visual) approach applied to dereverberation
- paper is well organized and straightforward to follow
- solid experiments with ablation studies and in-depth analyses
- supplementary materials provide useful information not explained/described in the main text

Weaknesses:
- some of the key arguments are not sufficiently validated
- contributions are marginal and limited
- experiments and evaluation are not thorough

The key idea in this work is that visual input captured by a camera conveys information that characterizes room acoustics. With all the experiments and ablation studies, however, I don't believe that the authors provide sufficient information that support the aforementioned key hypothesis. The authors did take into account this matter in two ways: first, they included a reverb-visual matching loss term to penalize a matching between random speech and visual input, but little experiment or analysis was performed on this loss term - varying the weight or using different loss functions, for example. With or without using a reverb-visual matching loss was included in the ablation studies, but the effect was not significant compared with other methods. The performance was better with the audio-only model even in speech quality metric (PESQ). The second way is to use a random image instead of using the image that matches the room impulse response. In such scenario, the images does not carry any information relevant to the room acoustics, and thus the performance should be worse than or comparable to that of the audio-only method. However, the performance is better in general and is even best for some metric. Further experiments and deeper analysis are warranted to investigate this issue.

Another point that I hope to be added is the comparison of the model efficiency. Adding a visual embedding network increases the number of model parameters and computational costs. The proposed method is likely to work in real-time scenarios, and possibly on small devices, the efficiency should also be considered.

Lastly, I'd like to suggest to include R-vectors approaches as another baseline for comparison.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposes a multi-modal learning framework that is applied to speech dereverberation. The idea is interesting and somewhat novel, but the experimental validation is not thorough enough to support the key idea of reverb-visual matching nor to provide sufficient evidences that visual stream does convey information about room acoustics.
