title: Learning Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Fluency for Novel Object Captioning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a framework that combines masked language models (BERT) and image-text embedding models (CLIP) for novel object captioning. The proposed model significantly improves the fluency, fidelity, and adequacy of the generated images. Extensive experiments are conducted and the comparison between the proposed model and existing models shows that the proposed model performs relatively well.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

S1: This paper pays attention to three important properties of captions -- fluency, fidelity and adequacy and proposes a formwork to generate fluent, faithful and adequate captions to describe images.

S2: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models on novel object captioning.

Weaknesses:

W1: The proposed model combines some existing models, like BERT, CLIP, so one of my concerns is the novelty of the proposed framwork. Plus, the reward to train the model is not novel, which is used in Rotian's work [1] and distilling from BERT for text generation was proposed by Chen [2]. Also, the observation that repeating the objects that occur in an image can improve retrieval score is not new [3].

W2: Neigher BERT nor CLIP is trained using the dataset used in the paper, so how to mitigate the problem of domain shift when applying BERT and CLIP? I do not think you can assume that BERT and CLIP are always correct.

W3: It seems that BERT is only used during training. I am wondering whether BERT can be used in the inference stage, for example, using the captioning model to generate a caption with beam search and then employing BERT to generate more captions. In this way, we can see whether BERT can improve fluency.

W4: Lack of human study.

[1] Rotian Luo et al.. Discriminability objective for training descriptive captions. CVPR, 2018.

[2] Y-C Chen et al.. Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation. ACL, 2020.

[3] Qingzhong Wang et al.. Describing Like Humans: on Diversity in Image Captioning. CVPR, 2019.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a framework that combines masked language models (BERT) and image-text embedding models (CLIP) for novel object captioning. The proposed model significantly improves the fluency, fidelity, and adequacy of the generated images. Extensive experiments are conducted and the comparison between the proposed model and existing models shows that the proposed model performs relatively well.

Main Review:
Strengths:

S1: This paper pays attention to three important properties of captions -- fluency, fidelity and adequacy and proposes a formwork to generate fluent, faithful and adequate captions to describe images.

S2: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models on novel object captioning.

Weaknesses:

W1: The proposed model combines some existing models, like BERT, CLIP, so one of my concerns is the novelty of the proposed framwork. Plus, the reward to train the model is not novel, which is used in Rotian's work [1] and distilling from BERT for text generation was proposed by Chen [2]. Also, the observation that repeating the objects that occur in an image can improve retrieval score is not new [3].

W2: Neigher BERT nor CLIP is trained using the dataset used in the paper, so how to mitigate the problem of domain shift when applying BERT and CLIP? I do not think you can assume that BERT and CLIP are always correct.

W3: It seems that BERT is only used during training. I am wondering whether BERT can be used in the inference stage, for example, using the captioning model to generate a caption with beam search and then employing BERT to generate more captions. In this way, we can see whether BERT can improve fluency.

W4: Lack of human study.

[1] Rotian Luo et al.. Discriminability objective for training descriptive captions. CVPR, 2018.

[2] Y-C Chen et al.. Distilling Knowledge Learned in BERT for Text Generation. ACL, 2020.

[3] Qingzhong Wang et al.. Describing Like Humans: on Diversity in Image Captioning. CVPR, 2019.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a strong baseline of novel object captioning via combining BERT and CLIP models, however, the novelty is limited.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces VLAF², a two‐stage framework for novel object captioning that explicitly optimizes three key desiderata—fluency, fidelity, and adequacy—by repurposing pretrained BERT and CLIP models. In the first stage, captions produced by a sequence‐to‐sequence backbone are refined via BERT’s masked language modeling objective, with a CLIP‐based gating mechanism to ensure semantic consistency. In the second stage, the model is fine‐tuned using self‐critical sequence training (SCST) with combined rewards from CIDEr, CLIP image–text alignment, and a repetition penalty. Extensive experiments on the nocaps and COCO benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over prior art in automatic metrics (CIDEr, SPICE, precision/recall of novel object mentions) and qualitative examples suggest enhanced fluency and faithfulness.

Strengths:
1. Clear Problem Decomposition: The paper convincingly frames novel object captioning in terms of three concrete properties—fluency (linguistic quality), fidelity (precision of object mentions), and adequacy (coverage/recall of visual content)—and maps each to existing pretrained models (BERT for fluency, CLIP for fidelity/adequacy).
2. Innovative Two-Stage Framework: VLAF² creatively integrates BERT’s masked‐LM refinement under a CLIP‐gated selection rule with RL objectives. This differs from prior work by combining masked‐LM distillation [Chen et al. ACL 2020] and CLIP‐based rewards [Luo et al. CVPR 2018] in a unified pipeline.
3. Strong Empirical Gains: On nocaps and COCO, VLAF² yields state‐of‐the‐art CIDEr and SPICE scores, surpassing existing baselines. Ablation studies isolate the contributions of the BERT refinement (LBERT), CLIP reward (rCLIP), and repetition penalty (rrep), showing each component’s positive impact.
4. Diagnostic Analyses: The paper reports object‐level precision and recall for novel objects, reinforcing claims on fidelity and adequacy. Qualitative examples illustrate improved phrasing and object coverage.

Weaknesses:
1. Limited Novelty: The core idea—combining pretrained BERT and CLIP within cross‐entropy and RL training—builds on known components (BERT distillation, CLIP rewards, repetition penalties) without introducing fundamentally new modeling techniques.
2. Domain Shift Concerns: Neither BERT nor CLIP is trained on the nocaps/COCO captioning data, raising questions about domain mismatch. The paper does not analyze how model errors (e.g., CLIP misalignments) affect final captions or propose mitigation strategies.
3. Reliance on External Detectors: VLAF² depends on off‐the‐shelf object detector tags (e.g., VIVO). Detection errors may propagate into captions, but there is no sensitivity analysis to quantify robustness against noisy or missing detection inputs.
4. Heuristic Gating and Penalty Design: The CLIP‐score threshold for masked‐LM replacements and the static repetition penalty based on GloVe similarity are introduced without ablation over threshold values or exploration of more nuanced similarity measures (e.g., contextual embeddings).
5. Computational and Practical Costs: The two‐stage pipeline leverages multiple large pretrained models and RL, yet training/inference times, GPU requirements, and memory footprints are not reported, leaving deployment feasibility unclear.
6. Lack of User‐Centric Evaluation: While automatic metrics and qualitative examples are compelling, the absence of a systematic human evaluation limits understanding of real‐world improvements in fluency and faithfulness.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Ablation on Gating Thresholds: Evaluate sensitivity of BLEU/CIDEr/SPICE gains to different CLIP gating thresholds and BERT mask ratios to demonstrate robustness.
- Detection Noise Analysis: Introduce controlled perturbations or dropouts in detection tags and measure downstream caption quality, or explore joint refinement of tags and captions.
- Computational Profiling: Report wall‐clock training/inference time, GPU memory usage, and approximate FLOPs for VLAF² versus baselines to assess practical overhead.
- Broader Generalization: Test on an additional out‐of‐domain dataset (e.g., Conceptual Captions or CC3M) to show that VLAF²’s improvements extend beyond nocaps and COCO.
- Enhanced Human Study: Conduct a small‐scale user study rating fluency, accuracy, and overall preference between VLAF² and leading baselines, to validate automatic metrics.
- Clarify Hyperparameters: Move key hyperparameter settings (reward weights, masking schedules) into the main text and provide rationale to aid reproducibility.

Overall, this paper presents a well‐engineered and thoroughly validated approach that achieves state‐of‐the‐art performance in novel object captioning. However, its reliance on existing pretrained modules, heuristic gating, and lack of analysis on robustness and cost constrain its broader impact. I recommend a marginal acceptance (rating: 6), encouraging the authors to address the points above to strengthen novelty claims and practical applicability.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to improve novel object captioning i.e. describing objects/contents that are not seen during training. Specifically, the authors propose to correct the captions generated from captioning models using pre-trained BERT and CLIP models by rewarding the captions with precise and rich visual content. They show qualitative and quantitative improvements on CiDER and SPICE scores compared to previous work and propose to measure the fluency, fidelity and adequacy of the generated captions for a more comprehensive evaluation of novel object captioning.
reviews.main_review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to improve novel object captioning i.e. describing objects/contents that are not seen during training. Specifically, the authors propose to correct the captions generated from captioning models using pre-trained BERT and CLIP models by rewarding the captions with precise and rich visual content. They show qualitative and quantitative improvements on CiDER and SPICE scores compared to previous work and propose to measure the fluency, fidelity and adequacy of the generated captions for a more comprehensive evaluation of novel object captioning.

Main Review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the challenging task of novel object captioning, where models must describe objects unseen during training. The authors present VLAF², a two-stage framework that (1) refines captions via BERT’s masked language modeling under a CLIP-gated loss to improve fluency and semantic consistency, and (2) applies self-critical sequence training with combined rewards—CIDEr, CLIP alignment, and a repetition penalty—to encourage fidelity and adequacy. Experiments on the nocaps benchmark (and additional COCO tests) demonstrate improvements in CIDEr and SPICE, alongside novel fluency/fidelity/adequacy analyses.

Strengths:
- Clear formulation of three core desiderata—fluency, fidelity, adequacy—and principled mapping to BERT (linguistic refinement) and CLIP (cross-modal scoring). The motivation for leveraging pre-trained visual-linguistic knowledge is sound and well articulated.
- The two-stage learning pipeline is creative: gated BERT-based word replacement discourages semantically inconsistent edits, while the reinforcement learning objective reinforces precise and rich visual content.
- Quantitative gains over prior state-of-the-art on nocaps (CIDEr, SPICE, and object-level precision/recall) are supported by extensive ablations that isolate the contributions of BERT refinement (r_BERT), CLIP reward (r_CLIP), and repetition penalty (r_rep).
- The paper introduces a more comprehensive evaluation protocol—measuring fluency, fidelity, and adequacy—which goes beyond standard metrics and provides deeper insight into caption quality.
- Qualitative examples illustrate more precise object mentions and improved wording. Early human judgments (on fluency and adequacy) further corroborate the automatic improvements.

Weaknesses and Questions:
1. Reliance on External Detection Tags:
   - The method depends heavily on pre-computed detection tags (e.g., VIVO). What is the sensitivity to detection errors, and how do misdetections propagate into final captions? An analysis of robustness to noisy or missing tags would strengthen the evaluation.
2. Computational Cost and Practicality:
   - Applying BERT refinement and CLIP scoring in both training stages incurs non-trivial overhead. Please report training/inference times, GPU requirements, and memory footprints compared to baselines to assess real-world feasibility.
3. CLIP Gating and Reward Hyperparameters:
   - The hard gating threshold in the BERT refinement stage is presented heuristically. How sensitive are results to this threshold or to mask ratios and replacement frequencies? Similarly, tuning of reward weights (r_CIDEr, r_CLIP, r_rep) appears in the appendix; summarizing their stability in the main text would improve clarity.
4. Reinforcement Learning Stability:
   - SCST can suffer from high variance and unstable convergence. It would be helpful to discuss training curves, variance of rewards, or strategies used to stabilize learning.
5. Scope of Evaluation:
   - Experiments are confined to nocaps and COCO. It remains unclear whether VLAF² generalizes to other domains (e.g., Conceptual Captions, larger multilingual datasets) or to languages beyond English.
6. Novelty Considerations:
   - While the integration of BERT and CLIP is well executed, the approach builds on standard cross-entropy and RL pipelines. The paper could better position its novelty relative to recent multimodal refinement methods (e.g., GPT-based rewriters or alternative vision–language scorers).
7. Hallucinations and False Mentions:
   - Strong CLIP rewards may inadvertently encourage hallucinations. Has the paper measured rates of incorrect object mentions or performed a targeted error analysis?
8. Human Evaluation Depth:
   - Automatic metrics and a small pilot human study are reported, but a more extensive human evaluation (particularly on fidelity and adequacy) would solidify claims of improved caption quality.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Include an ablation on detection noise by simulating tag errors or using an end-to-end detector inside the pipeline.
- Provide a runtime analysis: GPU hours for training, per-image inference latency with and without the VLAF² stages.
- Explore soft or learnable gating for CLIP in the BERT refinement stage to reduce reliance on a fixed threshold.
- Report convergence plots and reward variance to shed light on RL stability.
- Evaluate on at least one additional out-of-domain dataset or diversify to non-English captions to demonstrate generality.
- Conduct a small-scale user study focused on hallucination rates and perceived adequacy of detailed descriptions.
- Compare briefly to alternative language models (e.g., GPT-2) as refinement modules to underscore the benefits of BERT.

Conclusion:
This paper presents a thoughtful and empirically validated approach to improving novel object captioning by unifying linguistic refinement (via BERT) and visual alignment (via CLIP) within a reinforcement learning framework. While the core idea is marginally incremental—relying on existing pretrained models and standard RL—the detailed ablations, new evaluation protocol, and solid performance gains warrant interest. Addressing concerns around computational cost, sensitivity to hyperparameters, detection noise, and generalization would further strengthen the contribution.

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Main Review:
### Pros:
- Leveraging prior visual-linguistic knowledge from pre-trained BERT and CLIP models for the task of novel object captioning. The association between fidelity, fluency and adequacy with the training tasks of BERT and CLIP presents a good motivation for the method adopted. 

- The reward based loss functions designed for encouraging caption novelty and fluency and to discourage repetitive captions is interesting and novel. 

- The authors design fidelity/fluency and adequacy quantitative evaluation to measure the efficacy of their approach compared to previous work and show improvements. 

### Clarifications:
- Is the BERT model finetuned with the captions from the nocaps dataset in eq 2?  How is the CLIP condition satisfied in eq2, what happens if both the captions are unreliable ? Is there a threshold above which the CLIP scores are compared ?

- The BERT equations also could fulfil the fidelity objective because of the gating function. Is this observed?

- Are the improvements from Eq5. coming because of the r_CIDER term in the labelled caption data? Are the results in Table 1 compared with CIDER optimization for baselines? 

- What is observed if the reward is not calculated over the labeled training samples X_l?

- How will  the model perform if Eq 2 is performed during the inference stage only from a pre-trained captioning model?

- In fig 1, it is not very clear where the second caption “two glove hands...” comes from, is one from greedy decoding and other from sampling  ? 

- Are the novel words mostly objects/nouns?

### Cons: 

- While the method is effective and simple, there are certain ablations which could have been performed qualitatively and quantitatively to draw where the improvements come from. 

- The improvements in the method over the SOTA are via CIDER optimization on labeled examples in Eq 5. If the baselines are trained with CIDEr optimization, it might improve overall performance as well. 

- The motivation of improving fluency or adequacy is probably not only related to novel image captioning but for image captioning in general. Could this method help to address those problems there? 

- As stated in the paper, that previous methods produce grammatically incorrect captions for NOC, that does not seem to be the case (eg in fig 4). There are details missing from baseline captions but they are still fluent.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is well written with detailed experimental results to support the claim. The paper proposes interesting ideas to improve NOC and is marginally novel but there are still some concerns as stated in the main review.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The papers presents an approach for the task of novel object captioning (NOC). NOC requires the model to describe novel objects appearing in test images that were not described in the training captions. The authors claim that existing methods are not good at the task because they don't tackle three concepts required to produce a good caption — 

1) Fluency: generating human-like captions, 

2) Fidelity: whether the caption is describing the novel object in the image or not, 

3) Adequacy: whether the caption covers all the salient visual concepts in the image. 

The authors propose a model Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Adequacy (VLAF2) to address these issues. The model utilizes BERT to sample different variations of the captions by substituting random words from the caption. It uses CLIP to assess which of these are more relevant to the image. The model also uses CLIP score and combine it with CIDEr based reward to further train the captioning model via reinforce. 

The model is evaluated on the nocaps benchmark on which it achieves improvements over existing sota methods like VINVL+VIVO. The authors also show that apart from captioning metrics, the model also does better on fluency (B@4, C), Fidelity (Precision), Adequacy(Recall).
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths

1. Overall, I concur with the key insight from the paper which is that models for NOC should tackle fluency, fidelity and adequacy. It has been observed in Agrawal et al 2019 (nocaps dataset) that methods suffer from these issues. Captions which are fluent often aren't accurate, and captions which are accurate often aren't fluent.
2. The authors take reasonable steps to fix these issues. Using a LM to fix fluency is a good idea. The authors address one of the main issues with such an approach — BERT like language models can produce fluent captions but it's hard to judge their accuracy. The authors use CLIP to score these and select the best method. Such an approach makes sense.
3. Another novel contribution in this work is using uncaptioned images (images in the OpenImages dataset which do not have associated training captions) during training. Since these don't have training captions, they modify the reward to use CLIP as well penalize repeated words. Indeed, they show that CLIP + repeated penalty is helpful for overall performance (Table 3).

### Weaknesses

1. One of the biggest and most important weakness in the paper is that the paper doesn't follow the guidelines from the nocaps paper. As mentioned in the nocaps paper (section 3.3), "Do not use additional paired image-caption data collected from humans. Improving evaluation scores by leveraging additional human-generated paired imagecaption data is antithetical to this benchmark – the only paired image-caption dataset that should be used is the COCO Captions 2017 training split. However, external text corpora, knowledge bases, and object detection datasets may be used during training or inference". The paper heavily relies on CLIP which has been trained on alt-text data for images (which are human written) for improving the captioning performance. This makes the method proposed in the paper not comparable to existing methods in my opinion. The authors should have used the nocaps (XD) for evaluation.
2. Related to the second point, I would have liked to see if you can replace CLIP with other approaches (ViLBERT) which also outputs a score indicating how well the caption is aligned with the image. This would form a fair comparison.
3. Since CLIP is trained on alt-text data for images, it would be interesting to see if joint-training on Conceptual Captions (or a similar web-scale data) works better instead.
4. CLIP is being used for checking the relevance of the caption with the image. I wonder how will other similar metrics for caption relevance (VIFIDEL) compare against scores from CLIP.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I think the paper presents an interesting insight (breaking down caption quality into fluency, adequacy and fidelity) and presents an approach which improves all the three aspects on the nocaps dataset. I raised a couple of concerns (use of CLIP which has been trained on human-generated alt-text data) which goes against the guidelines of the benchmark. It nullifies some of the claims in the paper and I'd be interested in hearing from the authors regarding that. I would have also liked to see comparison with other caption relevance metrics like VIFIDEL which would make the contributions stronger. Overall, I am borderline on this paper and will update my thoughts based on the author response.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

Summary Of The Paper:
The papers presents an approach for the task of novel object captioning (NOC). NOC requires the model to describe novel objects appearing in test images that were not described in the training captions. The authors claim that existing methods are not good at the task because they don't tackle three concepts required to produce a good caption — 

1) Fluency: generating human-like captions, 

2) Fidelity: whether the caption is describing the novel object in the image or not, 

3) Adequacy: whether the caption covers all the salient visual concepts in the image. 

The authors propose a model Visual-Linguistic Adequacy, Fidelity, and Adequacy (VLAF2) to address these issues. The model utilizes BERT to sample different variations of the captions by substituting random words from the caption. It uses CLIP to assess which of these are more relevant to the image. The model also uses CLIP score and combine it with CIDEr based reward to further train the captioning model via reinforce. 

The model is evaluated on the nocaps benchmark on which it achieves improvements over existing sota methods like VINVL+VIVO. The authors also show that apart from captioning metrics, the model also does better on fluency (B@4, C), Fidelity (Precision), Adequacy(Recall).

Main Review:
### Strengths

1. Overall, I concur with the key insight from the paper which is that models for NOC should tackle fluency, fidelity and adequacy. It has been observed in Agrawal et al 2019 (nocaps dataset) that methods suffer from these issues. Captions which are fluent often aren't accurate, and captions which are accurate often aren't fluent.
2. The authors take reasonable steps to fix these issues. Using a LM to fix fluency is a good idea. The authors address one of the main issues with such an approach — BERT like language models can produce fluent captions but it's hard to judge their accuracy. 3. Another novel contribution in this work is using uncaptioned images (images in the OpenImages dataset which do not have associated training captions) during training. 1. One of the biggest and most important weakness in the paper is that the paper doesn't follow the guidelines from the nocaps paper. As mentioned in the nocaps paper (section 3.3), "Do not use additional paired image-caption data collected from humans. Improving evaluation scores by leveraging additional human-generated paired imagecaption data is antithetical to this benchmark – the only paired image-caption dataset that should be used is the COCO Captions 2017 training split. However, external text corpora, knowledge bases, and object detection datasets may be used during training or inference". Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I think the paper presents an interesting insight (breaking down caption quality into fluency, adequacy and fidelity) and presents an approach which improves all the three aspects on the nocaps dataset. I raised a couple of concerns (use of CLIP which has been trained on human-generated alt-text data) which goes against the guidelines of the benchmark. It nullifies some of the claims in the paper and I'd be interested in hearing from the authors regarding that. I would have also liked to see comparison with other caption relevance metrics like VIFIDEL which would make the contributions stronger. 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the novel object captioning (NOC) problem by explicitly optimizing three desiderata: fluency (human‐like language), fidelity (accurately mentioning novel objects), and adequacy (covering salient image content). The authors introduce VLAF², a two‐stage framework. In Stage 1, BERT’s masked language model refines candidate captions generated by a base captioner, with CLIP gating to ensure semantic consistency. In Stage 2, the model is further trained via self‐critical sequence training, combining CIDEr, a CLIP‐based image–text alignment reward, and a repetition penalty to discourage trivial outputs. The method is evaluated on the nocaps benchmark (and additional COCO experiments), achieving state‐of‐the‐art gains across CIDEr, SPICE, and fine‐grained precision/recall metrics, alongside human judgments.

Strengths  
1. Clear conceptual decomposition.  The paper convincingly argues that fluent captions often lack accuracy, while accurate captions can be disfluent or incomplete. Framing the problem in terms of fluency, fidelity, and adequacy guides the design of VLAF² and ties each component to well‐understood pretrained models.  
2. Novel integration of pretrained models.  Leveraging BERT for masked‐LM–driven refinement and CLIP for image–text gating and reward is creative. The two‐stage pipeline—first refining unpaired captions on OpenImages with CLIP gating, then reinforcing with combined rewards—yields consistent improvements.  
3. Empirical rigor.  Experiments on nocaps demonstrate significant gains over strong baselines (e.g., VINVL+VIVO), with ablation studies isolating the impact of BERT refinement, CLIP gating, RL components, and the repetition penalty. Additional COCO results and a supplementary human evaluation reinforce the robustness of findings.  
4. Use of uncaptioned data.  Exploiting OpenImages without ground‐truth captions and adapting the RL reward using CLIP and a repetition penalty is an inventive way to inject more visual contexts during training.  
5. Qualitative and human studies.  The paper provides illustrative examples showing improved wording and object mentions, and reports human judgments that align with automatic metrics on fluency and accuracy.

Weaknesses  
1. Benchmark comparability.  The heavy reliance on CLIP—a model trained on large‐scale alt‐text data—runs counter to the nocaps guideline of using only COCO captions for paired data. This external use of human‐written image captions through CLIP may artificially boost performance and complicate fair comparison with prior methods.  
2. Reliance on external detectors and pretrained models.  VLAF² depends on object tags from VIVO and multiple large pretrained backbones (BERT, CLIP), raising concerns about computational cost, inference latency, and applicability in resource‐constrained settings. Training/inference time, GPU requirements, and memory footprints are not reported.  
3. Lack of comparison with alternative scorers.  The choice of CLIP for gating and reward is well motivated, but the paper does not evaluate other cross‐modal metrics (e.g., VIFIDEL) or alternative vision–language models (e.g., ViLBERT, or GPT‐2 as a language scorer). Such comparisons would clarify whether gains stem from the proposed framework or from CLIP’s superior image–text alignment.  
4. Gating and hyperparameter sensitivity.  The hard CLIP threshold for accepting BERT substitutions and the weights for CIDEr, CLIP reward, and repetition penalty are set heuristically. No analysis of threshold sensitivity, mask ratios, or reward stability is provided, leaving robustness questions unanswered.  
5. Hallucination and error analysis.  While overall fidelity improves, there is limited discussion of potential false mentions or hallucinations when the CLIP reward dominates. A targeted error analysis or small‐scale user study on misdescriptions would strengthen confidence in real‐world applicability.  
6. Generalization beyond nocaps.  Although COCO results are included, evaluation on a broader range of benchmarks (e.g., Conceptual Captions, CC3M) or other domains would demonstrate that VLAF² generalizes beyond the nocaps setting.  
7. Presentation and reproducibility.  Key methodological details (masking strategy, hyperparameter schedules, CLIP threshold selection) are tucked into the appendix. Integrating these into the main text and reporting exact training costs would help practitioners replicate the work.

Overall Recommendation  
This paper presents a thoughtful and empirically strong approach to novel object captioning by unifying BERT‐based fluency refinement with CLIP‐based fidelity and adequacy rewards. The conceptual framing and ablation studies are compelling, and VLAF² achieves notable gains on nocaps. However, concerns about benchmark compliance, computational overhead, and comparative analysis with alternative scorers leave open questions. Given the borderline nature of these issues, I vote a marginal accept (Rating: 6).

### Strengths

1. Overall, I concur with the key insight from the paper which is that models for NOC should tackle fluency, fidelity and adequacy. It has been observed in Agrawal et al 2019 (nocaps dataset) that methods suffer from these issues. Captions which are fluent often aren't accurate, and captions which are accurate often aren't fluent.

The authors address one of the main issues with such an approach — BERT like language models can produce fluent captions but it's hard to judge their accuracy. The authors use CLIP to score these and select the best method. Such an approach makes sense.
3. Another novel contribution in this work is using uncaptioned images (images in the OpenImages dataset which do not have associated training captions) during training. Since these don't have training captions, they modify the reward to use CLIP as well penalize repeated words. Indeed, they show that CLIP + repeated penalty is helpful for overall performance (Table 3).

1. One of the biggest and most important weakness in the paper is that the paper doesn't follow the guidelines from the nocaps paper. As mentioned in the nocaps paper (section 3.3), "Do not use additional paired image-caption data collected from humans. Improving evaluation scores by leveraging additional human-generated paired imagecaption data is antithetical to this benchmark – the only paired image-caption dataset that should be used is the COCO Captions 2017 training split. However, external text corpora, knowledge bases, and object detection datasets may be used during training or inference". 4. CLIP is being used for checking the relevance of the caption with the image. I wonder how will other similar metrics for caption relevance (VIFIDEL) compare against scores from CLIP.

I raised a couple of concerns (use of CLIP which has been trained on human-generated alt-text data) which goes against the guidelines of the benchmark. reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper tries to generate novel object captions that meantime satisfy three aspects: fluency, fidelity, and adequacy. To achieve this goal, it exploits two large-scale pertaining models which are BERT and CLIP. Specifically, given a novel object caption, the method first masks some words (expect nouns) in the sentence and then use BERT to generate these masked words. After that, they compare the association degree between two captions (the original caption and BERT re-generated caption) and the image. Such association degree is computed by inputting the image and the caption into CLIP. Based on the association degree, the method decides whether the BERT generated caption should be used as training supervision.
reviews.main_review:
1. Novel object captioning is one sub-task of image captioning and generating captions that are more fluency, fidelity, and adequacy is one sub-requirement of captioning. However, this method exploits two large-scale models BERT and CLIP to solve this small task, which is not an economic solution. In this way, I think the motivation of the research is not quite significant. Actually, not only novel object captions are not fluency, fidelity, and adequacy, even the captions generated by standard captioning settings (trained by MSCOCO) may also have low fluency, fidelity, and adequacy (Or say that standard captions' fluency, fidelity, and adequacy can be further improved). It would be better if the authors could apply their method to standard captioning or more settings which have limited training data other than only one novel object setting.
2. In Sec 3.1, the authors mention that they mask certain words instead of nouns since nouns may appear in the image, which should not be generated by BERT. However, the attribute and relation words may also appear in the image, which should not be masked. For example, some horses may be white and some others may be dark. Or in some images, a man ''ride'' a horse while in another image a man ''stand near'' a horse. Then these attributes and relations should also not be masked.
3. At the end of the first paragraph in Sec 3.2, the author claims that ''word-by-word supervision tend to imitate the sentence patterns of the training images instead of relating caption data to the visual content'' while do not provide suitable evidence.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The major concern is about the significance of the motivation and the applied solution is not very economic by using two large scale pre-training models to solve a small task.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper tries to generate novel object captions that meantime satisfy three aspects: fluency, fidelity, and adequacy. To achieve this goal, it exploits two large-scale pertaining models which are BERT and CLIP. Specifically, given a novel object caption, the method first masks some words (expect nouns) in the sentence and then use BERT to generate these masked words. After that, they compare the association degree between two captions (the original caption and BERT re-generated caption) and the image. Such association degree is computed by inputting the image and the caption into CLIP. Based on the association degree, the method decides whether the BERT generated caption should be used as training supervision.

Main Review:
1. Novel object captioning is one sub-task of image captioning and generating captions that are more fluency, fidelity, and adequacy is one sub-requirement of captioning. However, this method exploits two large-scale models BERT and CLIP to solve this small task, which is not an economic solution. In this way, I think the motivation of the research is not quite significant. Actually, not only novel object captions are not fluency, fidelity, and adequacy, even the captions generated by standard captioning settings (trained by MSCOCO) may also have low fluency, fidelity, and adequacy (Or say that standard captions' fluency, fidelity, and adequacy can be further improved). It would be better if the authors could apply their method to standard captioning or more settings which have limited training data other than only one novel object setting.
2. In Sec 3.1, the authors mention that they mask certain words instead of nouns since nouns may appear in the image, which should not be generated by BERT. However, the attribute and relation words may also appear in the image, which should not be masked. For example, some horses may be white and some others may be dark. Or in some images, a man ''ride'' a horse while in another image a man ''stand near'' a horse. Then these attributes and relations should also not be masked.
3. At the end of the first paragraph in Sec 3.2, the author claims that ''word-by-word supervision tend to imitate the sentence patterns of the training images instead of relating caption data to the visual content'' while do not provide suitable evidence.

Summary Of The Review:
The major concern is about the significance of the motivation and the applied solution is not very economic by using two large scale pre-training models to solve a small task.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Local Patch AutoAugment with Multi-Agent Collaboration

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper target the task of automatically determining the best augmentation method to obtain improved accuracy. While the previous related studies focus on the image-level augmentation and ignore the semantic information of the augmented images, the proposed algorithm augments the grid-wise patches of the given input with the preserved semantic information. To overcome the enlarged number of combinations to consider all the patches, the algorithm utilizes the MARL algorithm with the unified reward function. By MARL algorithm, the number of parameters can be reduced and the training speed can be much improved, compared to the previous auto-augmentation methods. Through the image classification and fine-grained image recognition tasks, the proposed algorithm was validated, and it shows the state-of-the-art performance among the compared methods.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths
+ It seems effective to utilize the MARL algorithm to reduce the computational complexity for the multiple patches.
+ As a result, the computation cost of the proposed algorithm becomes much reduced when it is compared to the previous auto-augmentation algorithms. Furthermore, the algorithm can select the optimal combinations at every epoch without any additional computations, which can improve its generality across the various tasks.

Weaknesses & Questions
- About state and observation. In the proposed algorithm, the state and the observation are obtained by the deep feature from the trained model. My question is the image to obtain the deep feature. When the image is the original image before the determined augmentation, the state and the observation are not affected by the selected action. Then, the best action can be determined just by one simple simulation, and no reinforcement learning algorithm is necessary to find the optimal augmentation. If the augmented images are fed into the model again, the computation cost cannot be reduced as shown in the experiments. What is the detailed sequence to obtain the deep feature?
- Since the deep feature is utilized, its invariant property can affect the determination of the optimal augmentation. In other words, when the trained model is robust to the translation shift, the deep features from the model would be similar even after various translation shifts. Then, the training loss becomes also similar, which results in no selection of translation augmentation. This means that the algorithm would select only the augmentation method that is not considered in the initial model, and this can work as one of the biased optimizations for the optimal augmentation methods. Is there any analysis about the robustness of the initial model for the deep features?
- In the experiments, the proposed algorithm is only compared with the out-of-date algorithms, so its performance cannot be validated well. I recommend the authors add the state-of-the-art studies related to auto-augmentation and the learning-based augmentation algorithms, which include Saliency-mix[1] and Co-mixup[2].
- What would be the limitation of the proposed algorithm?

[1] Uddin et al. "SaliencyMix: A Saliency Guided Data Augmentation Strategy for Better Regularization", ICLR2021
[2] Kim et al. "Co-Mixup: Saliency Guided Joint Mixup with Supermod-ular  Diversity", ICLR2021
reviews.summary_of_the_review: As I said above, I have some questions about the necessity of the reinforcement learning-based algorithm in the framework. In addition, the comparison with the state-of-the-art algorithms should be added for the validation. I will change my score when I solve the above issues.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Strengths
+ It seems effective to utilize the MARL algorithm to reduce the computational complexity for the multiple patches.
+ As a result, the computation cost of the proposed algorithm becomes much reduced when it is compared to the previous auto-augmentation algorithms. Furthermore, the algorithm can select the optimal combinations at every epoch without any additional computations, which can improve its generality across the various tasks.

Weaknesses & Questions
- About state and observation. In the proposed algorithm, the state and the observation are obtained by the deep feature from the trained model. My question is the image to obtain the deep feature. When the image is the original image before the determined augmentation, the state and the observation are not affected by the selected action. Then, the best action can be determined just by one simple simulation, and no reinforcement learning algorithm is necessary to find the optimal augmentation. If the augmented images are fed into the model again, the computation cost cannot be reduced as shown in the experiments. What is the detailed sequence to obtain the deep feature?
- Since the deep feature is utilized, its invariant property can affect the determination of the optimal augmentation. In other words, when the trained model is robust to the translation shift, the deep features from the model would be similar even after various translation shifts. Then, the training loss becomes also similar, which results in no selection of translation augmentation. This means that the algorithm would select only the augmentation method that is not considered in the initial model, and this can work as one of the biased optimizations for the optimal augmentation methods. Is there any analysis about the robustness of the initial model for the deep features?
- In the experiments, the proposed algorithm is only compared with the out-of-date algorithms, so its performance cannot be validated well. I recommend the authors add the state-of-the-art studies related to auto-augmentation and the learning-based augmentation algorithms, which include Saliency-mix[1] and Co-mixup[2].
- What would be the limitation of the proposed algorithm?

[1] Uddin et al. "SaliencyMix: A Saliency Guided Data Augmentation Strategy for Better Regularization", ICLR2021
[2] Kim et al. "Co-Mixup: Saliency Guided Joint Mixup with Supermod-ular  Diversity", ICLR2021

Summary Of The Review:
As I said above, I have some questions about the necessity of the reinforcement learning-based algorithm in the framework. In addition, the comparison with the state-of-the-art algorithms should be added for the validation. I will change my score when I solve the above issues.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents Patch AutoAugment (PAA), a fine-grained automated data augmentation framework that divides each image into a grid of patches and leverages a cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithm to learn optimal augmentation policies for each region. By framing each patch as an agent observing local deep features—together with a shared global image representation—and sharing a unified team reward based on the target network’s loss improvement, PAA avoids expensive proxy evaluations and reduces the overall search cost. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet, three fine-grained recognition benchmarks (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC-Aircraft) and PASCAL VOC object detection demonstrate consistent accuracy gains over prior methods such as AutoAugment, RandAugment and adversarial DA approaches, often with lower computational overhead.  

Strengths  
1. Novelty of Formulation  
   - The shift from image-level to patch-level augmentation via a fully cooperative MARL setup is conceptually fresh and addresses the coarse granularity limitation of existing auto-augmentation methods. Agents make localized decisions while optimizing a global training objective, capturing regional diversity without discarding semantic coherence.  

2. Empirical Gains and Efficiency  
   - PAA shows consistent improvements (e.g., up to +0.5% Top-1 on ImageNet, notable fine-grained gains) across classification, fine-grained recognition and detection tasks. Co-training augmentation policies with the target network in an online manner significantly reduces search time compared to AutoAugment’s costly proxy training. Tensorized patch operations and decentralized execution further accelerate inference.  

3. Thorough Analysis and Interpretability  
   - The paper includes ablations on the number of patches (N), single-agent versus multi-agent formulations, random patch baselines, and rich Grad-CAM visualizations illustrating where and which operations are applied. These analyses provide insights into patch importance, operation selection, and the trade-off between granularity and performance.  

Weaknesses & Questions  
1. Clarification of State, Observation and RL Necessity  
   - The manuscript notes that both state and observation derive from deep features of a pretrained model. If these features originate from the unaugmented image, they remain static regardless of chosen actions, raising the question: is the full RL apparatus required, or could a one-step greedy simulation suffice? Conversely, recomputing features on each augmented image would negate the claimed speedups. Please clarify the exact sequence of feature extraction and policy inference at each epoch.  

2. Robustness of Deep Features  
   - Because the initial model’s deep features are used for policy making, their invariance to certain transformations (e.g., translation shifts) may bias the RL agents toward unfamiliar augmentations and ignore others. Has the team analyzed how the robustness or weakness of the feature extractor affects the learned augmentation distribution?  

3. Reinforcement Learning Stability and Hyperparameters  
   - Reinforcement learning can be sensitive to reward scaling, discount factors and learning rates. It would strengthen the work to include (or summarize) a stability analysis: how sensitive is PAA to hyperparameter choices such as actor-critic learning rates, grid size N, and reward normalization? Are there safeguards to prevent degenerate policies early in training?  

4. Sensitivity to Patch Granularity  
   - The ablation on the number of patches (e.g., N=4, 9, 16) shows non-monotonic performance trends, but without statistical variance or guidelines for selecting N on new datasets. Providing heuristics or a brief study on adaptive patch layouts (e.g., object-aware segmentation vs. uniform grids) would aid practitioners.  

5. Computational Overhead and Fairness of Cost Comparisons  
   - While PAA reduces search time, the per-batch inference and training overhead of patch feature extraction and policy execution may still exceed simpler baselines like RandAugment. The current comparisons mix different GPU platforms and omit error bars. A direct end-to-end runtime comparison under identical hardware and implementation frameworks (including memory footprint) is recommended.  

6. Comparison with State-of-the-Art Region-Based Methods  
   - Beyond classic augmentation baselines, the paper should compare against recent region-guided DA techniques such as SaliencyMix and Co-Mixup (ICLR 2021), which also exploit local image semantics. This will clarify PAA’s relative merits and position in the current literature.  

7. Reward Design and Alternative Objectives  
   - The shared team reward based on training loss difference is sensible but may bias toward aggressive augmentations early on. Have the authors considered alternative reward schemes, such as validation accuracy gains or curriculum-based schedules? A brief discussion or preliminary experiment would strengthen the methodological contribution.  

8. Reproducibility and Presentation Details  
   - The absence of pseudocode or detailed algorithmic steps for the MARL training loop limits reproducibility. Please include a concise algorithm box or supplementary pseudocode. Clarify any undefined variables in equations and ensure figures/tables reference the correct appendices or sections. Open-sourcing the implementation would further benefit the community.  

9. Practical Significance of Gains  
   - Many reported improvements are under 0.5%, which, although consistent, may be of limited impact in some applications. A discussion on when such marginal gains justify the added complexity would help readers assess PAA’s practical utility.  

10. Applicability to Other Architectures and Tasks  
   - The paper hints at potential extensions to vision transformers—where patch tokens are native—and to segmentation or other dense prediction tasks. While not mandatory, a short commentary or pilot study on these fronts would underscore the broader applicability of the MARL formulation.  

Decision & Rating  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
This work introduces a novel and well-motivated MARL approach to patch-level data augmentation, demonstrating consistent empirical gains and improved search efficiency. However, clarifications on the RL workflow, more rigorous comparisons (runtime, SoTA region-based methods), stability analyses, and reproducibility details are needed to fully validate and operationalize the method. Addressing these concerns would elevate PAA from an interesting research prototype to a broadly adoptable augmentation framework. Given the paper’s strengths and clear pathway to improvement, I recommend acceptance pending minor revisions.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper target the task of automatically determining the best augmentation method to obtain improved accuracy. While the previous related studies focus on the image-level augmentation and ignore the semantic information of the augmented images, the proposed algorithm augments the grid-wise patches of the given input with the preserved semantic information. To overcome the enlarged number of combinations to consider all the patches, the algorithm utilizes the MARL algorithm with the unified reward function. By MARL algorithm, the number of parameters can be reduced and the training speed can be much improved, compared to the previous auto-augmentation methods. Through the image classification and fine-grained image recognition tasks, the proposed algorithm was validated, and it shows the state-of-the-art performance among the compared methods.

Main Review:
Strengths
+ It seems effective to utilize the MARL algorithm to reduce the computational complexity for the multiple patches.
+ As a result, the computation cost of the proposed algorithm becomes much reduced when it is compared to the previous auto-augmentation algorithms. Furthermore, the algorithm can select the optimal combinations at every epoch without any additional computations, which can improve its generality across the various tasks.

Weaknesses & Questions
- About state and observation. In the proposed algorithm, the state and the observation are obtained by the deep feature from the trained model. My question is the image to obtain the deep feature. When the image is the original image before the determined augmentation, the state and the observation are not affected by the selected action. Then, the best action can be determined just by one simple simulation, and no reinforcement learning algorithm is necessary to find the optimal augmentation. If the augmented images are fed into the model again, the computation cost cannot be reduced as shown in the experiments. What is the detailed sequence to obtain the deep feature?
- Since the deep feature is utilized, its invariant property can affect the determination of the optimal augmentation. In other words, when the trained model is robust to the translation shift, the deep features from the model would be similar even after various translation shifts. Then, the training loss becomes also similar, which results in no selection of translation augmentation. This means that the algorithm would select only the augmentation method that is not considered in the initial model, and this can work as one of the biased optimizations for the optimal augmentation methods. Is there any analysis about the robustness of the initial model for the deep features?
- In the experiments, the proposed algorithm is only compared with the out-of-date algorithms, so its performance cannot be validated well. I recommend the authors add the state-of-the-art studies related to auto-augmentation and the learning-based augmentation algorithms, which include Saliency-mix[1] and Co-mixup[2].
- What would be the limitation of the proposed algorithm?

[1] Uddin et al. "SaliencyMix: A Saliency Guided Data Augmentation Strategy for Better Regularization", ICLR2021
[2] Kim et al. "Co-Mixup: Saliency Guided Joint Mixup with Supermod-ular  Diversity", ICLR2021

As I said above, I have some questions about the necessity of the reinforcement learning-based algorithm in the framework. In addition, the comparison with the state-of-the-art algorithms should be added for the validation. I will change my score when I solve the above issues.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper proposes an evolution of the traditional
  pipeline of image data augmentation used to reduce ML
  model overfitting.

  Instead of applying transformations such as shear,
  rotate, CutOut, etc. at the image level, the proposed
  technique divides the images into a fixed grid and
  applies a potentially different transformation to each
  cell. The problem of selecting a transform for each cell
  is cast as a multi-agent RL (MARL) task, and the agents
  learn as the main network trains within a (multi-agent)
  Advantage Actor Critic framework.

  The agents use a shared reward mechanism, with the reward
  defined as the difference between the loss on the
  augmented sample and the original loss.

  The regular grid is fixed for a dataset and
  hyperparameters like the magnitude of the augmentations
  follow a fixed schedule; the agents only pick _which_
  augmentation to apply on a patch.

  Experiments performed on CIFAR-{10,100}, ImageNet,
  CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC-Aircraft show
  relatively small but very consistent improvements in
  terms of image classification accuracy. Different design
  choices (MARL vs. single-agent vs. random, grid size,
  etc.) are ablated and discussed in detail.
reviews.main_review:
## Strengths

  - S1: Ablation studies and theoretical explanations
    motivate the choice for multi-agent reinforcement
    learning reasonably well.
  - S2: The efficiency of the proposed method is
    investigated very clearly in Section 4.3. It seems
    considerably more efficient than many other
    learning-based DA methods.
  - S3: The patch-wise data augmentation is a powerful idea
    which can spark a large body of interesting follow-up
    research.


## Limitations

  - L1: While this is not the main focus of the paper, the
    empirical improvement over RL baselines like Fast
    AutoAugment and non-RL baselines like RandAugment is
    relatively small.
  - L2: (Minor) While multiple datasets are used for
    evaluation, only two Resnet variants are investigated
    for ImageNet. It would be nice to see an evaluation of
    the proposed data augmentation procedure on other NN
    architectures as well on ImageNet, but I also
    understand that the computational costs of this can be
    quite large, so it may not always be possible.
  - L3: (Minor) The agents rely on a pre-trained ResNet to
    produce features to be used as observations.


## Suggestions (Per-Section)

### Related Work

  - This is minor, but it may be worth briefly mentioning
    [hong2019patch]. While their work doesn't do patch
    level data augmentation, and instead "patches" objects
    from an existing bank onto training samples, it still
    has connections to things like CutMix that make it
    relevant.
  - Some recent papers on automatic augmentation are
    missing and would be worth discussing. This includes
    [DADA], [DDAS], [hendrycks2019augmix] and
    [mueller2021trivialAugment]. In particular, Mueller and
    Hutter's paper highlights an interesting limitation of
    their otherwise robust baseline, which is that it
    doesn't work too well for object detection, an area
    where the current method may have the upper hand!
  - Similarly, there are some further papers, such as […]
    that perform a kind of "local" (though not strictly
    patch-based) data augmentation by "pasting in" objects
    into the 3D point cloud.


### Method

  - "directly applying reinforcement learning (RL)
    algorithm to …" => "algorithms"
  - "In MARL configuration" => "In the MARL configuration"
  - Towards the end of Section 3.2, it may make more sense
    to replace the "Until all patches" to "Once all
    patches…" or maybe "After".
  - Q1: Can an agent output more than one augmentation for
    a patch? Would it be possible for a patch to, e.g., get
    rotated AND colorized?
  - Q2: Given the more "local" nature of this augmentation
    method, have you considered evaluating it on more
    "local" tasks, such as object detection and semantic
    segmentation. Perhaps it could make a bigger impact
    there.
  - Q3: Given that the parameters of actor networks of all
    agents are shared (see above Eq. (5)), does that mean
    that each actor's policy is identical, and the only
    difference between the actors is what observation each
    one gets (the local patch features)?


### Experiments

  - At the start of Section 4.1, "Exactly, we focus" =>
    "Specifically, we focus"
  - In "Policy Visualization", "important levels" ->
    "importance levels"
  - In Section 4.3 I would say the "main methods for
    reducing the computational cost", since what you next
    describe are methods for reducing the cost, not what
    one would typically call reasons.
  - Table 3 is a little hard to follow because of the
    different hardware employed by each column. After
    staring at it for a while it's becoming a bit clearer,
    but at first glance it was a bit confusing.
  - Q4: Do the times from Table 3 include the inference
    time required to compute the agent state and
    observations with the auxiliary ResNet-18?


### Conclusion

  - The parallels to vision transformers are very
    interesting and help give the reader a broader
    perspective on possible applications of the current
    paper.
  - (Minor) The word "Furthermore" is repeated for two
    almost consecutive sentences. Using something like
    "additionally" to start one of the sentences can
    improve the flow of the paragraph a little.


## References

  - [hong2019patch]: Hong, Sungeun, Sungil Kang, and
    Donghyeon Cho. "Patch-level
  augmentation for object detection in aerial images."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on
  Computer Vision Workshops. 2019.
  - [cubuk2020randaugment]: Cubuk, Ekin D., et al.
    "Randaugment: Practical automated
  data augmentation with a reduced search space."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision
  and Pattern Recognition Workshops. 2020.
  - [DDAS] Liu, Aoming, et al. "Direct Differentiable
    Augmentation Search." arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.04282
    (2021).
  - [DADA] Yonggang Li, Guosheng Hu, Yongtao Wang, Timothy
    M. Hospedales, Neil Martin Robertson, and Yongxing
    Yang. DADA: differentiable automatic data augmentation.
    CoRR, abs/2003.03780, 2020.
  - [mueller2021trivialAugment]: Müller, Samuel G., and
    Frank Hutter. "TrivialAugment: Tuning-free Yet
    State-of-the-Art Data Augmentation." ICCV 2021.
  - [hendrycks2019augmix]: Hendrycks, Dan, et al. "Augmix:
    A simple data processing method to improve robustness
    and uncertainty." arXiv preprint arXiv:1912.02781
    (2019).
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper proposes a new way of looking at the classic
  task of image augmentation for classification. Instead of
  learning an image-level augmentation policy using RL, the
  proposed approach uses multi-agent RL to learn
  patch-specific augmentations. While the benefit of
  multi-agent RL is relatively small, it seems significant
  and the general idea of learning to apply different
  augmentations to different parts of the image is
  interesting and, to the best of my knowledge, new. The
  idea to apply image augmentations at a local level is the
  core contribution of this paper, in my opinion.

  While it would be interesting to see how this technique
  works on tasks which, unlike image-level classification,
  are themselves more "local", such as semantic
  segmentation or detection, overall the proposed approach
  is evaluated thoroughly.

  The writing is clear and while the authors don't
  explicitly mention an intent to publish their source
  code, it should be possible to implement the proposed
  approach just based on the description from the paper. As
  such, I recommend accepting the paper to ICLR.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes an evolution of the traditional
  pipeline of image data augmentation used to reduce ML
  model overfitting.

  Instead of applying transformations such as shear,
  rotate, CutOut, etc. at the image level, the proposed
  technique divides the images into a fixed grid and
  applies a potentially different transformation to each
  cell. The problem of selecting a transform for each cell
  is cast as a multi-agent RL (MARL) task, and the agents
  learn as the main network trains within a (multi-agent)
  Advantage Actor Critic framework.

  The agents use a shared reward mechanism, with the reward
  defined as the difference between the loss on the
  augmented sample and the original loss.

  The regular grid is fixed for a dataset and
  hyperparameters like the magnitude of the augmentations
  follow a fixed schedule; the agents only pick _which_
  augmentation to apply on a patch.

  Experiments performed on CIFAR-{10,100}, ImageNet,
  CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC-Aircraft show
  relatively small but very consistent improvements in
  terms of image classification accuracy. Different design
  choices (MARL vs. single-agent vs. random, grid size,
  etc.) are ablated and discussed in detail.

Main Review:
## Strengths

  - S1: Ablation studies and theoretical explanations
    motivate the choice for multi-agent reinforcement
    learning reasonably well.
  - S2: The efficiency of the proposed method is
    investigated very clearly in Section 4.3. It seems
    considerably more efficient than many other
    learning-based DA methods.
  - S3: The patch-wise data augmentation is a powerful idea
    which can spark a large body of interesting follow-up
    research.


## Limitations

  - L1: While this is not the main focus of the paper, the
    empirical improvement over RL baselines like Fast
    AutoAugment and non-RL baselines like RandAugment is
    relatively small.
  - L2: (Minor) While multiple datasets are used for
    evaluation, only two Resnet variants are investigated
    for ImageNet. It would be nice to see an evaluation of
    the proposed data augmentation procedure on other NN
    architectures as well on ImageNet, but I also
    understand that the computational costs of this can be
    quite large, so it may not always be possible.
  - L3: (Minor) The agents rely on a pre-trained ResNet to
    produce features to be used as observations.


## Suggestions (Per-Section)

### Related Work

  - This is minor, but it may be worth briefly mentioning
    [hong2019patch]. While their work doesn't do patch
    level data augmentation, and instead "patches" objects
    from an existing bank onto training samples, it still
    has connections to things like CutMix that make it
    relevant.
  - Some recent papers on automatic augmentation are
    missing and would be worth discussing. This includes
    [DADA], [DDAS], [hendrycks2019augmix] and
    [mueller2021trivialAugment]. In particular, Mueller and
    Hutter's paper highlights an interesting limitation of
    their otherwise robust baseline, which is that it
    doesn't work too well for object detection, an area
    where the current method may have the upper hand!
  - Similarly, there are some further papers, such as […]
    that perform a kind of "local" (though not strictly
    patch-based) data augmentation by "pasting in" objects
    into the 3D point cloud.


### Method

  - "directly applying reinforcement learning (RL)
    algorithm to …" => "algorithms"
  - "In MARL configuration" => "In the MARL configuration"
  - Towards the end of Section 3.2, it may make more sense
    to replace the "Until all patches" to "Once all
    patches…" or maybe "After".
  - Q1: Can an agent output more than one augmentation for
    a patch? Would it be possible for a patch to, e.g., get
    rotated AND colorized?
  - Q2: Given the more "local" nature of this augmentation
    method, have you considered evaluating it on more
    "local" tasks, such as object detection and semantic
    segmentation. Perhaps it could make a bigger impact
    there.
  - Q3: Given that the parameters of actor networks of all
    agents are shared (see above Eq. (5)), does that mean
    that each actor's policy is identical, and the only
    difference between the actors is what observation each
    one gets (the local patch features)?


### Experiments

  - At the start of Section 4.1, "Exactly, we focus" =>
    "Specifically, we focus"
  - In "Policy Visualization", "important levels" ->
    "importance levels"
  - In Section 4.3 I would say the "main methods for
    reducing the computational cost", since what you next
    describe are methods for reducing the cost, not what
    one would typically call reasons.
  - Table 3 is a little hard to follow because of the
    different hardware employed by each column. After
    staring at it for a while it's becoming a bit clearer,
    but at first glance it was a bit confusing.
  - Q4: Do the times from Table 3 include the inference
    time required to compute the agent state and
    observations with the auxiliary ResNet-18?


### Conclusion

  - The parallels to vision transformers are very
    interesting and help give the reader a broader
    perspective on possible applications of the current
    paper.
  - (Minor) The word "Furthermore" is repeated for two
    almost consecutive sentences. Using something like
    "additionally" to start one of the sentences can
    improve the flow of the paragraph a little.


## References

  - [hong2019patch]: Hong, Sungeun, Sungil Kang, and
    Donghyeon Cho. "Patch-level
  augmentation for object detection in aerial images."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on
  Computer Vision Workshops. 2019.
  - [cubuk2020randaugment]: Cubuk, Ekin D., et al.
    "Randaugment: Practical automated
  data augmentation with a reduced search space."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision
  and Pattern Recognition Workshops. 2020.
  - [DDAS] Liu, Aoming, et al. "Direct Differentiable
    Augmentation Search." arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.04282
    (2021).
  - [DADA] Yonggang Li, Guosheng Hu, Yongtao Wang, Timothy
    M. Hospedales, Neil Martin Robertson, and Yongxing
    Yang. DADA: differentiable automatic data augmentation.
    CoRR, abs/2003.03780, 2020.
  - [mueller2021trivialAugment]: Müller, Samuel G., and
    Frank Hutter. "TrivialAugment: Tuning-free Yet
    State-of-the-Art Data Augmentation." ICCV 2021.
  - [hendrycks2019augmix]: Hendrycks, Dan, et al. "Augmix:
    A simple data processing method to improve robustness
    and uncertainty." arXiv preprint arXiv:1912.02781
    (2019).

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a new way of looking at the classic
  task of image augmentation for classification. Instead of
  learning an image-level augmentation policy using RL, the
  proposed approach uses multi-agent RL to learn
  patch-specific augmentations. While the benefit of
  multi-agent RL is relatively small, it seems significant
  and the general idea of learning to apply different
  augmentations to different parts of the image is
  interesting and, to the best of my knowledge, new. The
  idea to apply image augmentations at a local level is the
  core contribution of this paper, in my opinion.

  While it would be interesting to see how this technique
  works on tasks which, unlike image-level classification,
  are themselves more "local", such as semantic
  segmentation or detection, overall the proposed approach
  is evaluated thoroughly.

  The writing is clear and while the authors don't
  explicitly mention an intent to publish their source
  code, it should be possible to implement the proposed
  approach just based on the description from the paper. As
  such, I recommend accepting the paper to ICLR.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
